chapter 1.0-a-lightning-payment.coverage: Read the full markdown file (38 lines) at /Users/austinv2/code/onion-routing/client/public/onion_routing_tutorial/1.0-a-lightning-payment.md. Extracted every factual/technical assertion in the prose. Per instructions, did NOT audit the embedded visual components (<lightning-network>, <htlc-propagation>) or their hover diagrams, nor checkpoints (none present) — those are covered by other agents. Verified prose claims against staged primary sources: BOLT 3 (/tmp/onion-audit/03-transactions.md: funding 2-of-2 P2WSH multisig L75-81; commitment-tx version/locktime L83-100; to_local CSV-delayed L104,113-124; to_remote P2WPKH immediate vs 1-block-CSV with anchors L136-146), BOLT 2 (/tmp/onion-audit/02-peer-protocol.md: HTLC fields payment_hash L2788; fulfill/preimage propagation backward L2600-2664), BOLT 4 (/tmp/onion-audit/bolt04.md: outgoing_cltv_value = cltv_expiry - cltv_expiry_delta L322,337, decrementing downstream so destination expires earliest). This is a conceptual intro chapter with only ONE concrete number in prose (10,000 sats) and no fee/CLTV arithmetic in the prose itself (those live in later chapters and in the hover diagram, out of scope here). No coverage caps. NOTE: line 18 contains a spelling typo "immedieately" — recorded as Low (this is rendered prose a student sees, but a pure typo, not a technical error).

claims.quote: Bob has a channel with both Alice and Charlie, and Charlie has a channel with both Bob and Dave. So the payment travels Alice → Bob → Charlie → Dave
claims.evidence: Topology is internally consistent and defines the canonical 3-hop main path. Characters are only Alice/Bob/Charlie/Dave as required. Adjacencies stated (Bob~Alice, Bob~Charlie, Charlie~Bob, Charlie~Dave) exactly support the path Alice→Bob→Charlie→Dave.

claims.quote: every Lightning channel is anchored by a pair of commitment transactions, one held by each channel partner. The input to both commitment transactions is the Funding UTXO, which locks the channel balance to a shared 2-of-2 multisig.
claims.evidence: BOLT 3 /tmp/onion-audit/03-transactions.md:77-81 funding output is P2WSH to `2 <pubkey1> <pubkey2> 2 OP_CHECKMULTISIG` (2-of-2). L87-91 each commitment tx has txin[0] = the funding outpoint. Each side holds its own commitment tx (mirror pair).

claims.quote: the local party's output is always delayed, and the remote's is always immedieately spendable
claims.evidence: BOLT 3 L104 + L113-124: to_local is CSV-delayed by to_self_delay (correct). L136-146: to_remote is a simple P2WPKH (immediately spendable) WITHOUT option_anchors, but L138 adds a 1-block CSV WITH option_anchors. Course teaches the legacy/non-anchor model (consistent with Course 1), so 'immediately spendable' is a valid teaching simplification, not an error.

claims.quote: this asymmetric delay ensures that, if one party attempts to cheat by publishing an old state, their counterparty has time to sweep all of their funds.
claims.evidence: BOLT 3 L104: outputs returning funds to the commitment owner are delayed for to_self_delay 'to allow an opportunity for penalty transactions, in case of a revoked commitment transaction.' L115: to_local can be claimed without delay by the other party if they know the revocation private key. Matches the prose.

claims.quote: watch a 10,000-sat payment travel through the network
claims.evidence: Sats-not-msat is a declared intended simplification. 10,000 sats is the single concrete amount in this chapter's prose; no fees/CLTV arithmetic stated in prose, so nothing to cross-check internally here. Downstream drift check: any other chapter/visual stating the headline amount should match 10,000 sats.

claims.quote: an HTLC output appears, locked to the same payment hash and a decrementing expiry locktime
claims.evidence: BOLT 2 L2788 HTLC carries payment_hash; same payment_hash used across the route (L2856 allows multiple HTLCs with same payment_hash). BOLT 4 L322 outgoing_cltv_value = cltv_expiry - cltv_expiry_delta, so the CLTV (expiry locktime) decrements at each hop going downstream. 'Same payment hash' + 'decrementing expiry' both correct.

claims.quote: the timelock must expire at the last node in the payment path first, as this helps ensure that the payment will not partially succeed or fail
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 L322/L337: outgoing_cltv_value = cltv_expiry - cltv_expiry_delta — expiries DECREASE downstream, so the final hop (Dave) has the smallest/earliest expiry block height and times out FIRST. BOLT 2 L2616-2664 rationale: the downstream HTLC must resolve before the upstream one so a forwarder is never stuck with its incoming HTLC timed-out while its outgoing HTLC is still live. 'Expire at the last node first' is correct. 'Will not partially succeed or fail' (atomicity) is a reasonable educational gloss of this anti-loss property.

claims.quote: When the preimage propagates back, the HTLC unwinds and the sats move into the recipient's to_local
claims.evidence: BOLT 2 L2600-2601: on receiving update_fulfill_htlc (preimage) for an outgoing HTLC, fulfill the incoming HTLC — i.e. the preimage propagates backward (Dave→Charlie→Bob→Alice). On fulfillment the HTLC output is removed and its value is added to the claiming party's balance. On that party's OWN commitment tx the balance output is to_local (on the partner's it is to_remote). 'Into the recipient's to_local' is a correct simplification from the recipient's own-commitment perspective.

claims.quote: Bob and Charlie each hold two commitment transactions, one for each of their payment channels.
claims.evidence: Per the topology in L10: Bob has 2 channels (Alice, Charlie) and Charlie has 2 channels (Bob, Dave). In each channel a node holds its own commitment tx, so Bob and Charlie each hold 2. Alice (1 channel) and Dave (1 channel) hold 1 each — consistent; prose correctly highlights only Bob and Charlie.

claims.quote: Hover any commitment to inspect the full BOLT 3 transaction.
claims.evidence: Commitment transactions are specified in BOLT 3 (/tmp/onion-audit/03-transactions.md 'Commitment Transaction' L83). The reference is accurate; the actual hover content is a visual-component concern out of scope for this prose audit.

claims.quote: immedieately
claims.evidence: Spelling typo in rendered prose a student reads. Should be 'immediately'. Technical content is unaffected — Low severity, not a protocol error.
claims.correctVersion: immediately

chapter 6.0-key-derivation.coverage: Audited the full prose of /Users/austinv2/code/onion-routing/client/public/onion_routing_tutorial/6.0-key-derivation.md (90 lines), including the surrounding prose of inline <m>/<g>/<spec-formula>/<python-snippet> references. Per instructions, I did NOT separately audit the embedded visual components (blinding-factor, operations-lifecycle, kdf-pipeline, operations-lifecycle-keyed) or the two checkpoints (cp-key-separation-draft, cp-key-domain-separation-draft) — those are other agents' scope — but I recorded every number/formula the prose states for cross-cutting drift checks. Verified all key-generation claims against /tmp/onion-audit/bolt04.md Key Generation section (lines 104-125), Pseudo Random Byte Stream (127-135), packet construction/pad (780-795), and error onion / ammag / ammagext (1060-1200). Reproduced the rho/mu/um/ammag derivations from the first hop shared secret (53eb63...) with Python HMAC-SHA256 and confirmed ammag = 3761ba4d... matches onion-error-test.json EXACTLY; confirmed um_key for hop[4] (4da7f2...) matches; confirmed all five ASCII labels decode correctly from their BOLT 4 hex byte-types (rho=0x72686F, mu=0x6d75, um=0x756d, pad=0x706164, ammag=616d6d6167, ammagext=616d6d6167657874). Verified the generate-key python-snippet (PythonSnippet.tsx:85) matches the prose formula. This chapter contains NO fee/CLTV/amount BACKWARD computations and no byte-decomposition arithmetic (those live in other chapters), so those FOCUS items are N/A here. One claim (the "September 2025" merge date for attribution data) is UNVERIFIABLE from the staged snapshot (no date metadata) but is a non-load-bearing historical aside. No errors found; all technical claims CORRECT.

claims.quote: Four are derived from each hop's shared secret, and one (`pad`) is derived from Alice's session key to initialize the packet buffer.
claims.evidence: BOLT4:108-116 defines rho/mu/um from the shared secret and pad; error section (BOLT4:1070) defines ammag also from the (per-hop) shared secret. pad is derived from the session key: BOLT4:866 `paddingKey := generateKey("pad", sessionKey.Serialize())`. So 4 per-hop (rho,mu,um,ammag) + 1 session-key (pad) = 5. Internally consistent with lines 50 and 62.

claims.quote: Encrypt the forward payload with a stream cipher (in this case, ChaCha20)
claims.evidence: BOLT4:83 'Pseudo-random stream: ChaCha20 is used'; BOLT4:131 stream generated 'using ChaCha20'. rho is the forward stream-cipher key (BOLT4:108-109).

claims.quote: Authenticate the forward layer with a message authentication code (MAC) (HMAC-SHA256) ... so the forwarder can detect tampering *before* processing.
claims.evidence: mu is the forward HMAC key (BOLT4:110 'used during the HMAC generation'). HMAC check-before-decrypt is the standard processing order (the hmac authenticates the packet, BOLT4:146). Matches chapter's line-57 'checks it first, before decrypting'.

claims.quote: Encrypt return-path errors with a stream cipher so only Alice can read the failure reason.
claims.evidence: ammag is the return-path stream-cipher (ChaCha20) key applied via XOR (BOLT4:1070-1072). Each return hop re-wraps with its own ammag; only Alice holds all hops' ammag keys to peel.

claims.quote: Authenticate return-path errors with a MAC so Alice can verify the error actually came from a specific hop on her route.
claims.evidence: um is the return HMAC key: BOLT4:1066-1068 'hmac ... with a key generated ... with key type um'. Confirmed um_key=4da7f2923edce6... for hop[4] in onion-error-test.json.

claims.quote: Initialize the empty buffer with a stream cipher so unused space at the end of the packet doesn't leak path length.
claims.evidence: pad key seeds a ChaCha20 stream to pre-fill the 1300-byte buffer: BOLT4:787-792 'initialized with 1300 random bytes ... The pad key ... ChaCha20 ... generate 1300 random bytes ... starting state of the mix-header.' BOLT4:112 'random filler bytes for the starting mix-header packet'.

claims.quote: BOLT 4 uses ChaCha20 with the same nonce (zero) everywhere, including for both forward and backward encryption. This is safe only because every operation gets its own key: the spec notes that *"the use of a fixed nonce is safe, since the keys are never reused."*
claims.evidence: BOLT4:131-135: streams use ChaCha20 with a 96-bit zero-nonce, and verbatim 'The use of a fixed nonce is safe, since the keys are never reused.' The same byte-stream method (zero nonce) governs both forward (rho) and backward (ammag) per BOLT4:1146 ('identical to the obfuscation steps the erring node carries out').

claims.quote: If forward and backward encryption shared a key, an attacker could XOR the forward and backward payload ciphertexts together and potentially recover the original plaintext.
claims.evidence: Standard two-time-pad attack: with a stream cipher under a fixed (zero) nonce, two ciphertexts C1=P1 XOR KS and C2=P2 XOR KS share keystream KS, so C1 XOR C2 = P1 XOR P2, removing the key and leaking plaintext relationships. The spec's safety rests precisely on per-operation keys (BOLT4:135). Correct as a 'potentially recover' / weakening claim.

claims.quote: generate_key(name, secret) = HMAC-SHA256(key=name, msg=secret)
claims.evidence: BOLT4:119-122: 'Keys are generated by computing an HMAC (with SHA256 ...) using the appropriate key-type ... as HMAC-key and the 32-byte shared secret as the message.' Matches python-snippet 'generate-key' (PythonSnippet.tsx:85) hmac.new(name.encode(), secret, hashlib.sha256).digest().

claims.quote: the output of this function will be another 32-byte pseudorandom value ... by using standardized names, the derivation becomes deterministic and both Alice and each subsequent hop can derive the exact same keys from the same shared secret.
claims.evidence: HMAC-SHA256 output is 32 bytes (BOLT4:116-117 'returns a 32-byte key'). Determinism: same (label, shared_secret) -> same HMAC, and both Alice and the hop share ss_i (ECDH), so both derive identical keys. Reproduced all four labels from ss=53eb63... and they match onion-error-test.json (ammag) exactly.

claims.quote: Four labels (`rho`, `mu`, `um`, and `ammag`) are used with each hop's shared secret ss_i, making them unique to that hop. The fifth label (`pad`) is used with Alice's session key.
claims.evidence: rho/mu/um/ammag derive from the per-hop shared secret (BOLT4:106-111, 1070; per-hop ammag_key/um_key confirmed in onion-error-test.json). pad derives from sessionKey (BOLT4:866). Internally consistent with line 3 and 62.

claims.quote: **`rho`**: the forward encryption key. A ChaCha20 stream-cipher key. ... One key both ways, because XOR-ing twice with the same keystream cancels out.
claims.evidence: rho = obfuscation/stream key (BOLT4:108-109). XOR is involutive: (P XOR KS) XOR KS = P, so Alice's encrypt and the forwarder's strip use the same rho keystream. Correct.

claims.quote: **`mu`**: the forward authentication key. An HMAC key. ... the forwarder recomputes that tag and checks it *first*, before decrypting anything.
claims.evidence: mu = HMAC key (BOLT4:110). The 32-byte packet hmac authenticates integrity (BOLT4:146); check-then-process is the standard order so a forwarder rejects tampered packets before acting.

claims.quote: **`um`**: the return authentication key. An HMAC key. A hop that fails signs its error with `um`; Alice later recomputes the tag with the same key.
claims.evidence: BOLT4:1066-1068 error-packet hmac uses key type um. um_key=4da7f2923edce6c2... confirmed for the erring hop[4] in onion-error-test.json. um is mu reversed (return-direction MAC).

claims.quote: **`ammag`**: the return encryption key. A ChaCha20 stream-cipher key. The failing hop (and each hop on the way back) XORs the error with `ammag`'s keystream to wrap it.
claims.evidence: BOLT4:1070-1072 erring node generates ammag key -> pseudo-random stream applied via XOR; BOLT4:1146 each forwarding hop re-applies its own ammag. Per-hop ammag_key values present for all 5 hops in onion-error-test.json and reproduced exactly (hop0 ammag=3761ba4d3e726d8a...).

claims.quote: **`pad`**: the buffer-setup key. Derived from Alice's session key (not a per-hop secret). Alice alone uses it, once, to pre-fill the empty payload area.
claims.evidence: BOLT4:787-792 + 866: pad/paddingKey from sessionKey, used once by the origin to seed the 1300-byte starting buffer. Hops never derive pad. Consistent with line 62.

claims.quote: each hop has its own `rho`, `mu`, `um`, and `ammag` key ... The `pad` key is different: Alice derives it from the session key once while building the packet.
claims.evidence: Same as C11/C16; per-hop derivation confirmed by distinct per-hop ammag_key/um_key in onion-error-test.json; pad from session key (BOLT4:866). Internally consistent.

claims.quote: `rho` and `mu` match the Sphinx paper's stream-cipher (h_p) and MAC (h_m) hash oracles. `ammag` is `gamma` reversed ... `um` is `mu` reversed.
claims.evidence: Label byte-checks: 'ammag' reversed = 'gamma'; 'um' reversed = 'mu'. rho/mu correspond to the Sphinx rho (stream) and mu (MAC) oracles, consistent with BOLT4:108-110 roles. Etymology aside, internally consistent and accurate.

claims.quote: In September 2025, BOLT 4 added an optional **attribution data** feature ... along with a fifth per-hop key called `ammagext` (derived the same way: ammagext_i = HMAC-SHA256("ammagext", ss_i)).
claims.evidence: ammagext key exists and is generated by the same generate-key process: BOLT4:1128 'a new key is generated, using the key type ammagext'; BOLT4:1175-1176, 1195 confirm per-hop ammagext for attribution_data XOR. Label 'ammagext' = 616d6d6167657874. The derivation formula is correct. The 'September 2025' merge DATE is not verifiable from the staged snapshot (no date metadata) but is a non-load-bearing historical aside, not a rendered number.

claims.quote: The construction is identical to the other four per-hop keys, so once you have `rho`, `mu`, `um`, and `ammag` down ... We're scoping this chapter to the original four.
claims.evidence: ammagext uses the identical HMAC-SHA256(label, ss_i) generate-key construction (BOLT4:1128, same process as 119-122). The 'four per-hop keys' here = rho/mu/um/ammag, consistent with line 50 (pad correctly excluded as the non-per-hop session-key key).

claims.quote: Bob performs ECDH between it and his node private key to derive ss_AB. He then produces E_AC ... Charlie does the same, producing E_AD for Dave.
claims.evidence: Sphinx per-hop ECDH + blinding chain (BOLT4 Shared Secret / blinding sections). Main path Alice->Bob->Charlie->Dave only; ephemeral notation E_AB/E_AC/E_AD and ss_AB consistent with chapter-4 conventions referenced. No stray Carol; characters limited to Alice/Bob/Charlie/Dave.

claims.quote: <python-snippet id="generate-key"></python-snippet>  (def generate_key(name: str, secret: bytes) -> bytes: return hmac.new(name.encode(), secret, hashlib.sha256).digest())
claims.evidence: PythonSnippet.tsx:85 implements exactly HMAC-SHA256(key=name.encode(), msg=secret), matching the prose formula on line 42 and BOLT4:119-122. Returns 32 bytes (sha256 digest).

secondaryWording.note: 'five core operations'/'Deriving the five keys'/'the fifth label (pad)' restate the same 4+1 framing already established verbatim at line 3. The third repetition (line 50 'Four labels ... The fifth label') slightly over-explains a count the reader has now seen twice; could be trimmed, but it is technically correct and not an error.

Each bullet's parenthetical re-explains XOR-cancellation / check-first behavior that the operations-lifecycle visual and the earlier 'Cryptographic operations' list (lines 20-24) already convey; mild redundancy between the per-key bullets and the adjacent operations prose.

chapter 8.0-wrapping.coverage: Audited the full prose of /Users/austinv2/code/onion-routing/client/public/onion_routing_tutorial/8.0-wrapping.md (83 lines). Verified every factual/numeric/crypto claim against staged primaries: BOLT 4 Packet Construction (/tmp/onion-audit/bolt04.md:748-904), Filler Generation (:971-1037), Key Generation (:104-125), Packet Structure (:137-176), hop_payload bigsize definition (:99-100); BOLT 2 associated_data=payment_hash + replay rationale (/tmp/onion-audit/02-peer-protocol.md:2788, 2864, 2873-2874). Cross-checked prose against the referenced <python-snippet> bodies in client/src/components/onion-routing-draft/PythonSnippet.tsx:89-117 and chapter-9 peel snippets for hop_size consistency. Did NOT audit the embedded checkpoints or the <wrap-trace> visual per instructions. No claim was UNVERIFIABLE; no errors found.

claims.quote: we're going to build the onion **in reverse**, starting with the destination's hop payload and working backward toward Bob
claims.evidence: BOLT4:784-785 'The packet construction is performed in the reverse order of the route, i.e. the last hop's operations are applied first.' Go loop bolt04.md:872 'for i := numHops - 1; i >= 0; i--' (Dave first, Bob last).

claims.quote: she'll need to initialize a 1,300-byte buffer with `pad_key`. To do this, she derives a `pad_key` from her session key and uses it as a ChaCha20 key to generate 1,300 bytes of pseudorandom noise
claims.evidence: BOLT4:787-792 packet initialized with 1300 random bytes from pad key; Go bolt04.md:866-868 'paddingKey := generateKey("pad", sessionKey.Serialize())' then ChaCha20 over 1300. Snippet wrap-pad-init (PythonSnippet.tsx:91) hmac.new(b"pad", session_key,...) + chacha20_keystream(pad_key, 1300). pad key-type derives from the SESSION key, not a hop shared secret — matches.

claims.quote: If she started with zeros, then the destination's decrypted view would decrypt to a sequence of zeros, which would leak information about how long the route was! By starting with pseudorandom bytes, the destination would be unable to decipher how long the route was.
claims.evidence: Standard Sphinx rationale; BOLT4:976-977 the un-padded field 'would make the field shorter at each hop, allowing an attacker to deduce the route length' and the pad/filler exist to keep the trailing region indistinguishable from further encrypted hops. Slight imprecision (filler actually overwrites the tail the destination reads) but the motivation for a RANDOM pad is the accepted educational explanation.

claims.quote: Alice can iterate over each hop (in reverse order: Dave → Charlie → Bob)
claims.evidence: Main path Alice→Bob→Charlie→Dave; reverse build order is Dave,Charlie,Bob (Alice is origin, not a hop). Only Alice/Bob/Charlie/Dave appear; no stray Carol. Matches BOLT4:872 reverse loop.

claims.quote: she will **right-shift** by `hop_size = len(payload) + 32` bytes ... The last `hop_size` bytes fall off the end (they're permanently dropped). `hop_size` bytes of placeholder space appear at the front
claims.evidence: BOLT4:801-803 shift_size = bigsize-len(1 for l<253) + l + 32; field right-shifted discarding last shift_size bytes. With `payload` = bigsize_LEN‖TLV (per line 36 note), len(payload)=1+l, so len(payload)+32 = 1+l+32 = shift_size. Snippet wrap-shift (PythonSnippet.tsx:96) hop_size=len(payload)+32; bytearray(hop_size)+buffer[:-hop_size] keeps 1300.

claims.quote: `payload` is the bigsize-prefixed TLV records (`bigsize_LEN ‖ TLV_bytes`), so `len(payload)` already includes the LEN prefix. The `+ 32` accounts for the HMAC field that comes after.
claims.evidence: BOLT4:99-100 hop_payload prefixed with a bigsize encoding the length 'excluding the prefix and the trailing HMAC'; HMAC is 32 bytes (BOLT4:171 [32*byte:hmac]). So full shift = LEN(1+) + TLV(l) + HMAC(32). Consistent with chapter-9 peel snippet hop_size = header_len + payload_len + 32 (PythonSnippet.tsx:142).

claims.quote: Alice writes the `payload` into the space at the front that just opened up, followed by the 32-byte `next_hmac`
claims.evidence: BOLT4:804-805 'The bigsize-serialized length, serialized hop_payload and hmac are copied into the shift_size bytes as the beginning of the mix-header.' Snippet wrap-write (PythonSnippet.tsx:101) buffer[:len(payload)+32] = payload + next_hmac.

claims.quote: `next_hmac` is **not this hop's own HMAC**! It's the HMAC of the layer *inside* this hop ... it's the HMAC from the previous iteration
claims.evidence: BOLT4:876 'hopsData[i].HMAC = nextHmac' writes the PRIOR iteration's HMAC into this hop's payload; BOLT4:893 'nextHmac = calcMac(...)' computed at end of iteration becomes next iteration's value. The HMAC chain: each layer commits to the encrypted contents beneath it.

claims.quote: **Dave's iteration** (innermost, runs first): `next_hmac` is 32 zero bytes. Dave is the destination, so there's no inner layer for his hop payload to commit to.
claims.evidence: BOLT4:862 'var nextHmac [32]byte' (zero-init); first iteration i=numHops-1 = final hop Dave assigns these zeros (BOLT4:876). Final node's HMAC field is all-zero.

claims.quote: She XORs the entire 1,300-byte buffer with the keystream generated from this hop's **rho** key (`rho_i`, derived from the shared secret `ss_AX` ...). Remember, one XOR pass both encrypts the bytes Alice just wrote AND adds a fresh encryption layer on top of everything written in inner iterations.
claims.evidence: BOLT4:806-807 'rho-key used to generate a 1300 byte pseudo-random stream which is then applied, with XOR, to the hop_payloads field' (full 1300, not just new region). BOLT4:108-110 rho derived from shared secret. Snippet wrap-xor (PythonSnippet.tsx:106) chacha20_keystream(rho,1300). The single-pass-re-encrypts-inner-layers property is the Sphinx layering invariant — correct.

claims.quote: she needs to overwrite the trailing `len(filler)` bytes of the buffer with the filler we built in chapter 7 ... this only needs to happen in the innermost layer because the filler was precomputed ... to anticipate every later iteration's `rho` XOR. We place it once here, and Charlie's and Bob's subsequent XOR passes transform it
claims.evidence: BOLT4:808-809 'If this is the last hop, i.e. the first iteration, then the tail of the hop_payloads field is overwritten with the routing information filler.' Go bolt04.md:888-889 'if i == numHops-1 { copy(mixHeader[len-len(filler):], filler) }' — innermost only, AFTER that iteration's XOR. Subsequent Charlie/Bob XOR passes over the full 1300 buffer transform the filler region. Snippet wrap-filler-overlay (PythonSnippet.tsx:111) buffer[:1300-len(filler)]+filler.

claims.quote: Alice computes the HMAC over the buffer concatenated with `associated_data` (the 32-byte `payment_hash`) using this hop's `mu` key. Save it as `next_hmac` for the iteration that builds the layer above.
claims.evidence: BOLT4:810-811 'next HMAC is computed (with mu-key as HMAC-key) over the concatenated hop_payloads and associated data.' Order = buffer || associated_data (confirmed BOLT4:936 HMAC256(mu, hop_payloads || associated_data)). associated_data = payment_hash (BOLT2:2864), payment_hash is sha256 = 32 bytes (BOLT2:2788). Snippet wrap-hmac (PythonSnippet.tsx:116) hmac.new(mu, buffer + associated_data,...).

claims.quote: an attacker who captured an in-flight onion could re-attach it to a different HTLC, and forwarders would have no way to notice the swap! With the `payment_hash` baked into every hop's HMAC, a re-attached onion fails verification at the first hop it touches.
claims.evidence: BOLT2:2873-2874 'commits to the HTLC by setting the payment_hash as associated data ... This prevents replay attacks that would reuse a previous onion_routing_packet with a different payment_hash.' Adversarial: re-attached onion with new payment_hash makes the first hop's recomputed HMAC256(mu, payloads||new_hash) mismatch the original outer tag → fails at first hop touched. Correct.

claims.quote: the buffer is what goes in the packet's `hop_payloads` field, and the HMAC we just computed is the packet's outer `hmac` tag. Alice prepends the 1-byte version (`0x00`) and her 33-byte ephemeral pubkey `E_AB`, appends the 32-byte `bob_hmac`, and ships the 1,366-byte packet to Bob.
claims.evidence: BOLT4:157-160 packet = version(1) + public_key(33) + hop_payloads(1300) + hmac(32) = 1366; BOLT4:162 version=0x00. Final (outermost=Bob) iteration's HMAC is the outer tag / first receiving peer's HMAC (BOLT4:813-814, 816-818). 1+33+1300+32 = 1366. Names consistent (E_AB, bob_hmac).

claims.quote: your `derive_keys` from chapter 6 (called per hop for each `rho`/`mu`, and once on the session key for the pad key)
claims.evidence: rho/mu derived per-hop from each hop's shared secret (BOLT4:800,873-874); pad derived once from the session key (BOLT4:866). Matches snippet wrap-pad-init using session_key.

claims.quote: a top-level `build` function that calls `wrap_hop` for each hop in reverse order, threading the `next_hmac` through the loop, passing the filler to `wrap_hop` on the innermost iteration, and, finally, assembling the 1,366-byte packet
claims.evidence: Describes the BOLT4:797-818 construction loop accurately: reverse order, next_hmac threading (nextHmac carried across iterations bolt04.md:876/893), filler only on innermost (i==numHops-1), final 1366-byte packet assembly (BOLT4:782).

secondaryWording.note: 'Each iteration's Step 5 produces THIS hop's HMAC, which then becomes the NEXT iteration's next_hmac. That's the HMAC chain...' partially restates lines 41-45 (the per-hop next_hmac examples). It does add synthesis (naming it 'the HMAC chain'), so mild redundancy only.

Step 5 prose ('computes the HMAC over the buffer concatenated with associated_data ... using this hop's mu key') and the immediately following paragraph at line 64 ('Notice that we don't hash the buffer alone. We concatenate it with a 32-byte value called associated_data: the payment_hash') restate the same buffer||associated_data fact back-to-back. Line 64's first sentence is redundant with line 62.
'derived from the shared secret ss_AX with the current hop' re-explains rho's derivation that was already established in chapter 6; minor over-explaining inside the step caption.

chapter 4.0-shared-secrets.coverage: Read the entire target file (118 lines) at /Users/austinv2/code/onion-routing/client/public/onion_routing_tutorial/4.0-shared-secrets.md. Per task scope I did NOT audit the embedded visual components (<ecdh-recap>, <node-key-attempt>, <naive-packet>, <blinding-factor>) or the checkpoint answer keys (covered by other agents), but I recorded every number/formula the prose states for cross-cutting drift checks. No fee/CLTV/amount/byte-decomposition arithmetic appears in this chapter (it is purely the ECDH shared-secret + ephemeral-key-blinding derivation), so those backward-computation checks are N/A here. All core crypto formulas (ss=SHA256(e·N)=SHA256(k·E), bf=SHA256(E||ss), e_next=bf·e, E_next=bf·E, e_1=sessionkey, E=e·G) match BOLT 4 exactly. Two non-technical issues found: a spelling typo ("Protocl") and one terminology deviation ("slice" vs locked "hop payload").

claims.quote: a public key is just a large, private number multiplied by the elliptic curve generator point: `A = a · G`
claims.evidence: BOLT4:498 `E_0 = e_0 \cdot G`; standard secp256k1 pubkey = privkey·G. Chapter's lowercase=private/uppercase=public convention matches BOLT usage.

claims.quote: you cannot recover `a` from `A` ... you'd have to solve the **elliptic curve discrete log problem**, which is computationally infeasible
claims.evidence: Standard ECDLP hardness assumption underlying secp256k1 (BOLT4:82 specifies secp256k1). Accurate.

claims.quote: scalar multiplication on the curve is **commutative** ... given Alice's keypair `(a, A)` and Bob's `(b, B)`, the point `a · B` equals `b · A`, because both expand to `ab · G`
claims.evidence: a·B=a·(b·G)=ab·G and b·A=b·(a·G)=ab·G. BOLT4:739 uses the same phrasing 'Due to the commutative property of scalar multiplication'. The integer 3·5=5·3 analogy is correct.

claims.quote: If Alice and Bob take the SHA256 hash of this point (serialized in its compressed 33-byte form, matching BOLT 4), they end up with a **shared secret**
claims.evidence: BOLT4:717-719 'resulting curve point is serialized to the compressed format and hashed using SHA256. The hash output is used as the 32-byte shared secret.' BOLT4:142 '33-byte compressed secp256k1 public_key'. BOLT4:745 'serialized in its compressed format'.

claims.quote: derive a unique shared private key for *each hop* ... only Alice and the given hop will be able to derive the shared private key
claims.evidence: The ECDH+SHA256 output is a 32-byte symmetric shared SECRET (BOLT4:719), not an EC 'private key'. Line 12 itself correctly calls it 'shared secret'. 'Shared private key' is loose; reads as 'a secret only the two share' so meaning survives, but the term conflicts with the strict private-key/public-key vocabulary the chapter just established on line 10.
claims.correctVersion: Call it a 'shared secret' (as line 12 does) rather than 'shared private key' to avoid conflating the symmetric ECDH output with an EC private scalar.

claims.quote: Then she'd embed her own node public key at the top of the packet. Each forwarder reads it, performs ECDH between their own node's private key and that public key, and derives the same shared secret
claims.evidence: Strawman first attempt (node-key reuse). Mechanically valid ECDH. The pedagogical point — reusing the identity-tied node key leaks Alice's identity — matches BOLT4:32-33 'Each hop ... only sees an ephemeral key for the origin node, in order to hide the sender's identity.' Chapter line 32 draws the correct lesson.

claims.quote: Alice would generate one `(ephemeral private key, ephemeral public key)` pair per forwarder ... `(e_Bob, E_Bob)`, `(e_Charlie, E_Charlie)`, and `(e_Dave, E_Dave)`
claims.evidence: This is the naive SECOND attempt (N independent ephemeral keys), explicitly framed as not the real protocol before the single-session-key design in attempt 3. Internally consistent: 3 hops Bob/Charlie/Dave = 3 pairs, matching line 42 'Alice must create three ephemeral key pairs'.

claims.quote: Alice will generate just **one** random private key, called the **session key** ... only the *first* ephemeral key ever needs to be embedded in the packet. For every subsequent hop, the *next* hop's ephemeral key is computed on the fly by the current hop
claims.evidence: BOLT4:755 'generates a random 32-byte sessionkey'; BOLT4:762-763 'initializes the ephemeral private key for the first hop ek_1 to the sessionkey'; BOLT4:817 only 'the ephemeral pubkey for the first hop' is in the packet header; forwarders advance the chain. onion-test.json session_key=0x41*32 (single key). Correct.

claims.quote: Alice sets the ephemeral private key equal to the session key ... e_AB = sessionkey
claims.evidence: BOLT4:762-763 'initializes the ephemeral private key for the first hop ek_1 to the sessionkey'. Hop 0 (Bob) = first hop. Correct.

claims.quote: E_AB = e_AB · G
claims.evidence: BOLT4:763-764 'derives ... the corresponding ephemeral public key epk_1 by multiplying with the secp256k1 base point.' G = base/generator point. Correct.

claims.quote: ss_AB = SHA256(e_AB · B)
claims.evidence: BOLT4:501 ss_i = SHA256(e_i * N_i); BOLT4:768-770 ECDH with hop's public key + ephemeral private key, hashed SHA256. B = Bob's node pubkey. Correct.

claims.quote: bf_AB = SHA256(E_AB ‖ ss_AB)
claims.evidence: BOLT4:743-746 'SHA256 hash value of the concatenation of the public key serialized in its compressed format and the shared secret'; BOLT4:503 e_{i+1}=SHA256(E_i || ss_i)*e_i (the SHA256(E||ss) term is the blinding factor). ‖ = concatenation = BOLT's ||. Correct.

claims.quote: e_AC = bf_AB · e_AB (mod n, the curve's order, which keeps the result a valid private key)
claims.evidence: BOLT4:773-774 'ephemeral private key for the next hop ek_{k+1} ... multiplying the current ephemeral private key ek_k by the blinding factor'; BOLT4:503 e_{i+1}=SHA256(E_i||ss_i)*e_i. Private-key scalar arithmetic on secp256k1 is implicitly mod n (group order); the 'mod n' annotation is correct and pedagogically appropriate. Negligible chance of 0 is ignored as in spec.

claims.quote: E_AC = e_AC · G
claims.evidence: BOLT4:775-776 'ephemeral public key for the next hop epk_{k+1} is derived from the ephemeral private key ek_{k+1} by multiplying with the base point.' Equivalent to BOLT4:504 E_{i+1}=SHA256(E_i||ss_i)*E_i. Correct.

claims.quote: No blinding factor for Dave: he's the final hop, so there's no successor that would need one.
claims.evidence: BOLT4:765-776 advances ek_{k+1} only to feed the NEXT hop; the final hop has no next hop, so its blinding factor / next ephemeral key is unused. Correct.

claims.quote: Alice was able to compute (E_AB, ss_AB, E_AC, ss_AC, E_AD, ss_AD) in advance, all from her session key. Now, she just needs to include E_AB into the start of the onion
claims.evidence: BOLT4:778-781 construction needs r ephemeral pubkeys + r shared secrets all derived from sessionkey; BOLT4:817 only the first hop's ephemeral pubkey is placed in the packet header. The 6-tuple (3 pubkeys + 3 secrets) for a 3-hop path is internally consistent. Correct.

claims.quote: Bob takes E_AB from the packet header and performs ECDH between it and his node private key: ss_AB = SHA256(bob_priv · E_AB). **Crucially, this is the same ss_AB that Alice derived using e_AB · B** ... because ... scalar multiplication on the curve is commutative.
claims.evidence: BOLT4:501 'ss_i = SHA256(e_i * N_i) = SHA256(k_i * E_i)' — explicitly equates sender side and forwarder side; BOLT4:724-728 sender uses ephemeral priv + hop pub, forwarder uses ephemeral pub + node priv, 'they will both derive the same value.' bob_priv·E_AB = bob_priv·e_AB·G = e_AB·B. Correct.

claims.quote: He then uses the blinding factor to compute the next ephemeral public key, which he'll hand to Charlie: E_AC = bf_AB · E_AB.
claims.evidence: BOLT4:504 E_{i+1}=SHA256(E_i||ss_i)*E_i; BOLT4:737-741 blinding of a pubkey is scalar mult of the EC point by the 32-byte blinding factor. bf_AB=SHA256(E_AB||ss_AB). Correct.

claims.quote: Dave does the same with his private key to derive ss_AD (he doesn't need to advance the chain, since there's no next hop).
claims.evidence: Final hop derives its shared secret via ECDH (ss_AD=SHA256(dave_priv·E_AD)) but does not need a blinding factor / next ephemeral key. Consistent with C15 and BOLT4 chain semantics.

claims.quote: If he hands Charlie a bogus E_AC, Charlie's ECDH will derive the wrong ss_AC, his decryption will produce garbage, and the payment will fail.
claims.evidence: If Bob substitutes E_AC' != E_AC, Charlie computes ss'=SHA256(charlie_priv·E_AC') != ss_AC. Alice encrypted Charlie's payload under rho derived from the true ss_AC (BOLT4:800,806-807), so decryption yields garbage and the per-hop HMAC (mu-key, BOLT4:810-811) fails -> forward error/payment fail. Adversarial check: Bob cannot forge a valid E_AC that produces Alice's ss_AC without breaking ECDLP. Correct.

claims.quote: Each hop retrieves its own ephemeral public key from the packet, performs ECDH between that key and its own node private key, and recovers the matching shared secret, which it then uses to decrypt its hop payload.
claims.evidence: Describes the naive attempt-2 design but the per-hop ECDH-then-decrypt mechanic matches BOLT4:724-728 forwarding side. Uses correct locked term 'hop payload'. Correct.

claims.quote: A reused, identity-tied key is not a good choice for an ephemeral key. Alice needs a **fresh, identity-detached keypair per payment**.
claims.evidence: Matches BOLT4:32-34 rationale: ephemeral key hides sender identity and blinding makes onions unlinkable. Correct lesson from the strawman.

claims.quote: In this scenario, Alice must create three ephemeral key pairs for this payment!
claims.evidence: For the naive attempt-2 on the 3-hop Bob/Charlie/Dave path, 3 ephemeral pairs is internally consistent (one per forwarder). This is the deliberately-rejected design, not the real protocol (which uses 1 session key). Correctly labeled as such.

claims.quote: a tool that we reviewed in the Noise Protocl course
claims.evidence: Spelling typo: 'Protocl' should be 'Protocol'. Visible to the student in prose. Not a technical error.
claims.correctVersion: Noise Protocol course

claims.quote: With that secret, they can decrypt the slice meant for them.
claims.evidence: Locked terminology requires 'hop payload', not 'slot/slice' (per onion-routing-terminology memory). Line 46 correctly uses 'hop payload'; line 22 deviates with 'slice'. Terminology consistency issue, not a technical error.
claims.correctVersion: the hop payload meant for them

claims.quote: each shared secret expands into five keys, each with a specific job
claims.evidence: BOLT4:106 'A number of encryption and verification keys are derived from the shared secret' — the five keys are rho, mu, um, pad, ammag (BOLT4 key generation section). Teaser for the next chapter; '5 keys' matches BOLT 4 (rho/mu/um/pad/ammag). Correct.

secondaryWording.note: Step 2 prose 'Bob can do this because he has both variables needed: E_AB is included in the packet, and he just derived ss_AB' restates what the formula bf_AB = SHA256(E_AB ‖ ss_AB) and the surrounding sentence already make obvious; mildly redundant but pedagogically reinforcing.

'Each key will sit in plain sight, as they have to be readable: a forwarder needs its ephemeral pubkey to perform ECDH and derive the shared secret.' partially re-explains the point made one sentence earlier ('include all three ephemeral pubkeys in the packet ... one per hop'); slight over-explanation.

This intro sentence ('she will derive an ephemeral private key, an ephemeral public key, a shared secret with that hop, and ... a blinding factor') enumerates exactly the four bullet items that immediately follow in the Bob/Charlie/Dave breakdown; the preview duplicates the per-hop list.

Verified every factual assertion against staged primary sources: BOLT 4 (/tmp/onion-audit/bolt04.md packet structure L137-186, key generation L104-125, pseudo-random stream L127-135, shared secret/blinding L713-776, construction L755-900, decryption/processing order L915-965), BOLT 2 (/tmp/onion-audit/02-peer-protocol.md onion_routing_packet 1366B L2780-2790, payment_hash as associated_data L2862-2873), BOLT 1 TLV format (/tmp/onion-audit/01-messaging.md L115-138), and the BOLT 4 worked packet test vector (/tmp/onion-audit/onion-test.json: onion begins "0002eec7..." = version 0x00 + 33-byte compressed pubkey). claims.quote: Sphinx addresses the first vulnerability by making every packet exactly 1,366 bytes
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 L782: 'The construction returns a single 1366-byte packet'. BOLT 2 L2790: '[1366*byte:onion_routing_packet]'. Decomposes 1+33+1300+32=1366.

claims.quote: each hop can derive its shared secret using one single ephemeral Diffie-Hellman operation
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 L715-728: shared secret = ECDH(ephemeral key, node ID key); forwarder uses ephemeral public key + own node priv. One ECDH per hop, compact header enabled by ephemeral-key blinding (L730-741).

claims.quote: Sphinx ... fixes two problems they had: packet sizes leaking the route length, and header sizes growing with the route
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 L9-37 + filler section L971-982 (fixed 1300B padding hides route length); blinded ephemeral keys (L730-741) keep header constant-size regardless of route length. Accurate characterization of Sphinx's improvements.

claims.quote: Each hop payload's contents follow the TLV (Type-Length-Value) format, where every field gets a type byte, a length, and the value bytes.
claims.evidence: BOLT 1 L120-138: a tlv_record is [bigsize:type][bigsize:length][length:value]. The 'type' is BigSize (1 byte only for small types), not strictly a single byte; chapter says 'type byte'. The onion TLV types actually used (2,4,6,8) ARE one byte each, and the chapter explicitly defers byte-level detail to ch7 ('we'll come back to the byte-level details in chapter 7'). Reasonable intro simplification.
claims.correctVersion: Strictly, the TLV type is BigSize-encoded (one byte only for types <253); the onion's actual types (2/4/6) fit in one byte.

claims.quote: Sphinx uses a stream cipher (specifically ChaCha20), which produces a deterministic stream of pseudo-random bytes given a key.
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 L83 ('ChaCha20 is used to generate a [pseudo-random stream]') and L131-135 (ChaCha20 over a 0x00-byte stream with zero nonce; deterministic given key). Correct.

claims.quote: Alice and Bob can each use the same shared secret to independently generate the exact same keystream ... encrypt the message by simply XOR-ing the plaintext with the keystream, byte-by-byte ... Bob can then XOR the ciphertext with the same keystream to decrypt it
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 L940-942: derive rho from ss, derive bytestream from rho, set unwrapped = XOR(hop_payloads, bytestream). Stream-cipher XOR is its own inverse with the same keystream. Correct (the keystream key is rho, derived from ss; chapter abstracts rho away here, fixed in 'A small confession').

claims.quote: HEADER holds the version byte (currently 0x00) and a single ephemeral public key for the current hop.
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 L157-162: [byte:version][point:public_key]; 'version has a constant value of 0x00'. Test vector onion.json L29 begins '0002eec7245d...' = 0x00 version then 33-byte compressed key (0x02 prefix). One ephemeral pubkey. Correct.

claims.quote: the forwarder combines it with its own node private key (via ECDH) to derive its shared secret with Alice
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 L726-727: 'during packet forwarding, the hop uses the ephemeral public key and its own node ID private key'. L934: ss = ECDH(public_key, node_privkey). Correct.

claims.quote: the hop advances this key with the blinding factor from chapter 4 and writes the next hop's ephemeral pubkey into the packet it sends on
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 L956-959: derive blinding_tweak = SHA256(public_key||ss); forward with public_key set to incoming public_key multiplied by blinding_tweak. Construction side L771-776 confirms multiply-by-blinding-factor advances the ephemeral key. Correct.

claims.quote: PAYLOAD AREA carries one hop payload per hop, each holding a short length prefix ... the encrypted routing instructions (amount, CLTV, the next hop, etc.), and a 32-byte HMAC that the next hop will check.
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 L167-172: hop_payloads = [bigsize:length][length:payload][32:hmac] repeated. Payload TLV types: 2=amt_to_forward, 4=outgoing_cltv_value, 6=short_channel_id (L194-205). HMAC is 32 bytes, checked by the next hop. Correct.

claims.quote: each forwarder strips one layer off the entire buffer, exposing its own hop payload at the front while the inner layers still cover the rest.
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 L940-952: XOR rho over the whole hop_payloads buffer, then remove length+payload+32-byte next_hmac from the FRONT. One XOR pass over the entire buffer; current payload emerges at front, deeper layers remain encrypted. Correct.

claims.quote: HMAC TAG is a 32-byte authentication tag that proves the integrity of the payload area ... each hop recomputes this tag over the payload with a key derived from its shared secret with Alice and checks it against the one in the packet; if they don't match ... the hop rejects it immediately.
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 L935-939: mu = HMAC256('mu', ss); HMAC = HMAC256(mu, hop_payloads || associated_data); constant-time compare; abort on mismatch. The tag actually commits to hop_payloads AND associated_data (= payment_hash, BOLT 2 L2864/2873), not the payload area alone. Saying 'over the payload' / 'integrity of the payload area' omits associated_data. The key being 'derived from its shared secret' is correct (mu from ss). Acceptable intro simplification; the payment_hash binding is a topic for later chapters.
claims.correctVersion: The HMAC commits to the payload area plus the associated_data (the HTLC payment_hash), not the payload area alone.

claims.quote: Before decrypting anything, each hop recomputes this tag over the payload ... and checks it
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 processing order: derive mu + compute/compare HMAC (L935-939) comes BEFORE deriving rho and XOR-decrypting (L940-942). 'Before decrypting anything' ordering is correct per spec.

claims.quote: The innermost layer is peeled last, so it has to be added first ... Alice builds each layer on top of the prior one, working from the inside out!
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 construction L862-893 iterates hops from the last (final) hop backward; nextHmac begins all-zero for the final hop and each outer HMAC is computed over the already-built inner buffer. Inside-out construction = Dave first, Bob last. Correct.

claims.quote: each hop's HMAC is computed over the encrypted layer beneath it, so an inner layer has to exist before Alice can authenticate the layer wrapped around it.
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 L810-816 + L862-893: each next HMAC computed (with mu-key) over the current packet state, which already contains the inner layers; final HMAC is for the first hop. Nesting forces inside-out build. Correct.

claims.quote: the original padding is 1,300 bytes
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 L144,159,165 (1300-byte hop_payloads) and L787 ('packet is initialized with 1300 random bytes'). Cross-checked ch7 L5 ('Payload: 1,300-bytes'). Correct.

claims.quote: Alice has a 1,300-byte payload area with three nested encryption layers. She appends an outer HMAC (bob_hmac), since Bob is the first recipient
claims.evidence: 3-hop path Alice->Bob->Charlie->Dave => 3 hop payloads => 3 nested layers within the 1300-byte hop_payloads. BOLT 4 L813-816: final HMAC (computed last) is the one used by the first hop (Bob). Correct.

claims.quote: Verify the outer HMAC ... If they don't match, they drop the packet. Decrypt with XOR ... Read the hop payload at the front ... extract the next-hop's HMAC ... construct the new 1,300-byte packet
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 L936-961 in exact order: compute/compare HMAC -> abort if differ -> XOR with rho -> remove length+payload -> remove 32-byte next_hmac -> forward hop_payloads truncated to 1300 with hmac=next_hmac. Matches the three steps. Correct.

claims.quote: the encryption layer comes off the entire payload, not just the current hop's payload ... when Bob XORs his payload, it reveals his plaintext TLV, but Charlie's and Dave's are still encrypted within deeper layers.
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 L940-942: single XOR of rho over entire hop_payloads buffer. Only the front (current hop) payload becomes plaintext; remaining bytes are still under the inner hops' rho layers (Alice applied one rho layer per hop during construction). Adversarial check: Bob holds the full 1300-byte buffer and can left-shift/inspect downstream-region byte counts, but cannot decrypt Charlie's/Dave's TLVs (needs their rho). Chapter makes no over-claim about hiding route length here. Correct.

claims.quote: When the packet finally reaches Dave, his hop payload's HMAC field contains 32 zero bytes. Per BOLT 4, an all-zero next-hop HMAC signals that the receiving node is the final destination
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 L955 ('If next_hmac is not all-zero (not the final node)') and L964-965 ('Otherwise (all-zero next_hmac): This is the final destination of the onion'). Construction L862 sets initial nextHmac = [32]byte{} (zeros) for the final hop. Correct.

claims.quote: The packet's outer HMAC is bob_hmac. Since this is the last HMAC Alice derives when creating the onion, it commits to all other payload data within the onion.
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 L810-816: 'The resulting final HMAC value is the HMAC that will be used by the first hop.' Built last over the fully-wrapped buffer, so it commits to everything beneath. Correct.

claims.quote: Inside Bob's hop payload sits charlie_hmac ... Inside Charlie's hop payload sits dave_hmac ... Inside Dave's hop payload, the HMAC field is 0x00... (32 zeros)
claims.evidence: Each hop payload carries the NEXT hop's HMAC (BOLT 4 L171, L952 'Remove 32 bytes as next_hmac'). For Alice->Bob->Charlie->Dave: Bob's payload holds Charlie's hmac, Charlie's holds Dave's hmac, Dave's holds all-zero (final). Consistent with C19. Correct.

claims.quote: Each one commits to everything below it: the hop payload, the next-inner HMAC, and all the encrypted layers underneath.
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 L178-180: 'payload is protected under the packet-wide HMAC ... fully authenticated'; construction computes each HMAC over the buffer that already contains inner layers. Nested commitment is correct.

claims.quote: digging into each of the five keys that the Sphinx protocol uses.
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 Key Generation L108-113 lists rho, mu, um, pad (four); the error section adds 'ammag' (L1070) as the return-encryption key. Total of five core keys (rho/mu/um/ammag/pad) is exactly the framing chapter 6 uses (6.0-key-derivation.md L35 'five core operations', L50 'Four labels (rho, mu, um, ammag)... The fifth label (pad)'). Internally consistent forward reference. Correct.

claims.quote: If we reused one raw secret for all of the encryption and authentication jobs, we'd be opening ourselves up to some pretty bad attack vectors.
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 derives distinct keys (rho/mu/um/pad/ammag) from the shared secret via HMAC256 with different labels (L115-122), precisely to avoid key reuse across encryption vs authentication. Consistent with ch6 L26-32 rationale. Correct.

secondaryWording.note: The sentence draws attention to its own formatting ('In the previous sentence, "deterministic" is bolded because this is very important') which is meta-commentary that over-explains; the emphasis would land without narrating the bolding.

Step 2 'Decrypt with XOR' re-explains 'XORing it again with the same keystream decrypts it', which restates the same point already made in the 'Encryption is XOR with a keystream' section a few paragraphs above; mildly redundant.

'Once Bob forwards the payload to Charlie, he decrypts and, once again, only sees his payload, with Dave's remaining hidden' restates the same hidden-inner-layers point made in the immediately preceding sentence about Bob; the Charlie example adds little beyond repetition.

Could NOT independently verify the external "LDK fails back within 39 blocks" implementation figure against the staged BOLT sources (it is attributed to LDK, not the spec); its 6.5-hour conversion is internally correct (39*10min=390min). All numeric/formula/byte claims that appear in prose were re-derived (2016 blocks=14 days; fee floor division; CLTV/fee boundary inequalities match BOLT 4 L336-337 exactly).

claims.quote: If the packet isn't 1,366 bytes or the version byte isn't 0x00, Bob rejects it immediately. ... the only currently acceptable version is 0x00 and the length must be 1,366.
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:782 'returns a single 1366-byte packet'; L162 'For this specification (version 0), version has a constant value of 0x00'; L40-43 higher-version packet MUST be discarded. Reader requirements L918-919 check version!=0 first.

claims.quote: Bob recomputes HMAC-SHA256(mu_i, hop_payloads || associated_data) with his own mu key and compares it against the hmac field ... rejects them with invalid_onion_hmac.
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:935-939 'Derive mu as HMAC256("mu",ss)'; 'Derive the HMAC as HMAC256(mu, hop_payloads || associated_data)'; 'MUST use a constant time comparison ... If ... differ: MUST abort'. Failure code L1516-1517 invalid_onion_hmac.

claims.quote: verify, then peel ... HMAC verification (the integrity check). Next ... Peel. If the HMAC verifies, then Bob decrypts the onion ... He derives rho, XORs the extended keystream
claims.evidence: BOLT4 Onion Decryption reader order bolt04.md:934-942: derive mu -> compute HMAC -> constant-time compare -> abort if differ -> THEN derive rho -> XOR. HMAC is verified strictly before rho-decrypt. Matches chapter's FOCUS claim (verify HMAC BEFORE decrypt).

claims.quote: He derives rho, XORs the extended keystream across the buffer, and lifts his hop payload off the front.
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:940-949: derive rho, derive bytestream twice length of hop_payloads, XOR, remove bigsize length then payload_length bytes from front as payload.

claims.quote: is there a short_channel_id? If so, then he's a forwarder ... If there is payment_data and no short_channel_id, then Bob is the destination
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:282-285 non-final MUST include short_channel_id & MUST NOT include payment_data; final MUST NOT include short_channel_id & includes payment_data if secret provided.

claims.quote: Type 2: amt_to_forward (msat). Type 4: outgoing_cltv_value (block height). Type 6: short_channel_id ... Type 8: payment_data (final hop only): payment_secret + total_msat.
claims.evidence: BOLT4 payload format bolt04.md:194-206: type2 amt_to_forward tu64; type4 outgoing_cltv_value tu32; type6 short_channel_id; type8 payment_data = [32*byte payment_secret][tu64 total_msat].

claims.quote: Bob will read the TLV records in order (their type numbers have to increase as he goes).
claims.evidence: BOLT1 01-messaging.md:147 'MUST order tlv_records in a tlv_stream by strictly-increasing type'; L205 all types must appear in increasing order.

claims.quote: What if both are present? Then the payment is malformed ... If neither are there, it's also malformed!
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:282-285: non-final MUST include scid + MUST NOT include payment_data; final MUST NOT include scid. A payload with both, or neither (no scid and no amt/cltv), violates the MUST/MUST NOT structure and a reader rejects (invalid_onion_payload). Spec doesn't phrase it as 'both/neither malformed' but the simplification is sound.

claims.quote: Sufficient fee (fee_insufficient). The incoming amount must exceed amt_to_forward by at least Bob's advertised fee (fee_base_msat plus fee_proportional_millionths).
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:336 fails if incoming amount_msat - fee < amt_to_forward, i.e. incoming - amt_to_forward >= fee. Failure code fee_insufficient (bolt04.md:1495-1498). Prose's 'fee_base plus fee_proportional' is loose but the actual product/floor is shown in snippet C16.

claims.quote: Enough CLTV cushion (incorrect_cltv_expiry). The incoming CLTV must exceed outgoing_cltv_value by at least Bob's advertised cltv_expiry_delta
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:337 fails if cltv_expiry - cltv_expiry_delta < outgoing_cltv_value, i.e. cltv_expiry - outgoing_cltv_value >= cltv_expiry_delta; L1349-1353 incorrect_cltv_expiry requires cltv_expiry - cltv_expiry_delta >= outgoing_cltv_value.

claims.quote: Above the channel minimum (amount_below_minimum). amt_to_forward must be at least the outgoing channel's htlc_minimum_msat. Whose minimum? Charlie's
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:1491-1494 forwarder fails amount_below_minimum if HTLC amount < currently specified minimum for the OUTGOING channel. BOLT7 bolt07.md:522-523 htlc_minimum_msat = minimum the channel PEER will accept (the receiving peer = Charlie on Bob->Charlie). The outgoing HTLC carries amt_to_forward.

claims.quote: when the channel was opened, Charlie set the smallest HTLC he'd accept
claims.evidence: BOLT7 bolt07.md:522 htlc_minimum_msat is advertised in channel_update and can be revised after opening, not fixed 'when the channel was opened'. Direction/ownership claim (Charlie's minimum) is correct; the temporal 'when the channel was opened' is an over-precise simplification.
claims.correctVersion: Charlie advertises the smallest HTLC he'll accept in his channel_update for that direction (it can be updated, not fixed at channel open).

claims.quote: LDK fails back any HTLC within 39 blocks (about six and a half hours) of expiry, a number built from the same reorg-and-confirmation math BOLT 2 uses to recommend the 34-block cltv_expiry_delta.
claims.evidence: 39 blocks * 10 min = 390 min = 6.5 hours (internally correct). BOLT2 02-peer-protocol.md:2690-2691 confirms 'cltv_expiry_delta of at least 34 is reasonable (R=2,G=2,S=12)' from 3R+2G+2S reorg math. The specific LDK '39 blocks' figure is an external implementation claim not in staged BOLT sources; attributed to LDK, plausible but not spec-verifiable here.

claims.quote: every major implementation enforces a standard of 2,016 blocks, about two weeks, so BOLT 4 eventually pinned max_htlc_cltv at exactly that.
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:1777 'This max_htlc_cltv value is defined as 2016 blocks, based on historical value deployed by Lightning implementations.' 2016*10min=20160min=14 days = about two weeks.

claims.quote: expiry_too_soon ... BOLT 4 lets Bob reject a CLTV 'unreasonably near the present' ... expiry_too_far ... Bob MAY reject a CLTV more than his configured maximum
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:1504-1508: 'if the cltv_expiry is unreasonably near the present: return an expiry_too_soon error'; 'if the cltv_expiry is more than max_htlc_cltv in the future: return an expiry_too_far error'. Both under 'A forwarding node MAY'.

claims.quote: required_fee = policy.fee_base_msat + (amt_to_forward * policy.fee_proportional_millionths) // 1_000_000 ; if incoming_amount_msat - amt_to_forward < required_fee: return 'fee_insufficient'
claims.evidence: BOLT7 bolt07.md:981 fee = fee_base_msat + (amount_to_forward * fee_proportional_millionths / 1000000) with integer (floored) division. Snippet floor-divides with // and the failure boundary matches BOLT4 L336 (exact fee passes). PythonSnippet.tsx:189.

claims.quote: It isn't the Route C policy from chapter 2 (where Bob's delta was 20).
claims.evidence: ForwarderPolicyMap.tsx:114 Route C Bob->Charlie cltvDelta:20. The standalone ch10 exercise uses cltv_expiry_delta=40 (onion-routing-exercises-draft.ts:547); the note correctly distinguishes them. Internally consistent.

claims.quote: jump back to the CLTV race-condition deep dive (/onion-routing-tutorial/pathfinding-101#cltv-race-condition) at the bottom of chapter 2.
claims.evidence: Anchor exists: 2.0-pathfinding-101.md:92 ''. Chapter slug 'pathfinding-101' confirmed in onion-routing-tutorial.tsx:444/448. Cross-reference resolves.

claims.quote: If Bob's downstream channel is offline or has insufficient liquidity, then he will fail the HTLC with temporary_channel_failure.
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:1478-1482 'A forwarding node MAY ... if during forwarding ... a transient error occurs in the outgoing channel (e.g. channel capacity reached, too many in-flight HTLCs, etc.): return a temporary_channel_failure error.'

claims.quote: Forward (short_channel_id present): send the peeled next_packet ... with amt_to_forward as the amount and outgoing_cltv_value as the timelock. ... Reject ... builds an error onion using the appropriate failure code and his um key.
claims.evidence: BOLT4 forward uses amt_to_forward/outgoing_cltv_value (bolt04.md:223-235). um key 'used during error reporting' (bolt04.md:112). Error-onion construction with um covered in ch11.

claims.quote: verify_hmac ... compute the HMAC-SHA256 over the inbound hop_payloads || associated_data with the forwarder's mu key and compare ... returning True or False.
claims.evidence: Matches exercise exercise-verify-hmac-draft (onion-routing-exercises-draft.ts:404-405): recompute HMAC-SHA256(mu, hop_payloads || associated_data), compare packet[1334:1366], return bool via hmac.compare_digest.

claims.quote: check_forward ... validate two things: 1) incoming amount covers amt_to_forward plus Bob's fee (else fee_insufficient) 2) incoming CLTV clears outgoing_cltv_value by at least his delta (else incorrect_cltv_expiry). This function returns the BOLT 4 failure code, or nothing if the forward is safe.
claims.evidence: Matches exercise exercise-check-forward-draft (onion-routing-exercises-draft.ts:505-539): returns str|None; fee_insufficient and incorrect_cltv_expiry codes; fee check run first. Return None when safe.

claims.quote: BOLT 2 lists the forwarding requirements, and BOLT 7's channel_update is where Bob advertises the fee and timelock he'll enforce.
claims.evidence: BOLT7 bolt07.md:520-528 channel_update sets cltv_expiry_delta, htlc_minimum_msat, fee_base_msat, fee_proportional_millionths. BOLT2 contains HTLC forwarding requirements (link valid).

secondaryWording.note: Step label '(the integrity check)' on the HMAC step and '(the policy check)' on step 5 partly duplicate the section headings 'The processing order' and 'The policy check: fees and timelocks' that follow; minor parenthetical over-labeling, not an error.
The doubled exclamatory 'So, again, error onion!' / 'it's also malformed! So, again, error onion!' restates the same conclusion twice in close succession; slight over-explaining.

'(fee_base_msat plus fee_proportional_millionths)' in prose elides the * amt / 1_000_000 term that the very next snippet (line 50) shows correctly; redundant-but-loose paraphrase of the snippet, low priority.
