Audited every factual assertion in prose, including the surrounding prose of the four referenced python-snippets (route-fee-formula, route-fee-example, route-timeout-formula, route-timeout-example), whose definitions I read in PythonSnippet.tsx to cross-check the numbers the prose states. Verified against staged primary sources: BOLT 7 (channel_update/channel_announcement fields lines 446-528, HTLC fee formula line 981, backward-calc note lines 440-444, routing example lines 1056-1144) and BOLT 4 (CLTV/amount forward semantics lines 194-343, total_cltv_delta line 263, payment_relay TLV lines 623-627). For cross-cutting drift I also read the route data in RouteComparisonDiagram.tsx (Route A/B), RouteCalcExercise.tsx (Route C), ForwarderPolicyMap.tsx (gossip policies), and CltvSafetyLab.tsx (race-condition lab) to confirm the prose's numeric claims (2,000-sat fee example; 168/1168 timeout example; "first forwarder forwards more than 400,000 sats" on Route B; max_total_cltv=200) are internally consistent with the visuals/exercise. Per instructions I did NOT separately audit the embedded checkpoints/visual components for their own correctness — only recorded prose-stated numbers for drift comparison. No byte-decomposition claims exist in this chapter's prose. NOTE (out of scope for prose audit but flagged for the visual auditors): Hazel→Dave feePpm differs between ForwarderPolicyMap (3000) and RouteComparisonDiagram ROUTE_A (2000); the prose does not state Hazel's ppm, so it is not a prose error, but it is a visual-vs-visual inconsistency worth a separate look.

claims.quote: we saw Alice send a 10,000 sat HTLC through Bob → Charlie → Dave
claims.evidence: Chapter-1 recap value. CltvSafetyLab.tsx:28 DAVE_AMOUNT = 10_000 over Bob/Charlie/Dave path. Main-path characters only; consistent.

claims.quote: channel_announcement: ... points to the on-chain 2-of-2 multisig output and provides both the bitcoin public keys and signatures, enabling other nodes ... to verify that the publisher of the message truly owns the private key to the on-chain Funding Transaction.
claims.evidence: BOLT 7 bolt07.md:155-168 channel_announcement carries bitcoin_signature_1/2, bitcoin_key_1/2, node_id_1/2, short_channel_id; lines 144-145/188 funding output is P2WSH 2-of-2; lines 147-149 bitcoin_signatures prove key ownership. Minor: both parties sign (not just 'the publisher'), but acceptable tutorial simplification.

claims.quote: channel_update: Published whenever a forwarder sets or changes its forwarding policy and fee structure. Each channel_update is signed
claims.evidence: BOLT 7 bolt07.md:429-434 ('each side independently announces the fees and minimum expiry ... can do this multiple times, in order to change fees') and line 448/496-497 signature field signed by node_id.

claims.quote: timestamp: Lets peers keep only the most recent update per channel.
claims.evidence: BOLT 7 bolt07.md:551-560: receiver ignores updates with timestamp <= last-received for that short_channel_id/node_id; timestamp orders updates so the newest is kept.

claims.quote: chain_hash: Tells peers which network (mainnet, testnet, etc.) this update belongs to.
claims.evidence: BOLT 7 bolt07.md:548-550: 'if the specified chain_hash value is unknown (meaning it isn't active on the specified chain): MUST ignore'. chain_hash identifies the chain.

claims.quote: channel_flags: Indicates which side of the channel is publishing this update.
claims.evidence: BOLT 7 bolt07.md:460-468: channel_flags bit 0 = direction ('identifies the node that this update originated from'); bolt07.md:501-504 direction 0 if node_id_1 else 1.

claims.quote: htlc_minimum_msat / htlc_maximum_msat: The smallest and largest HTLCs this forwarder is willing to relay.
claims.evidence: BOLT 7 bolt07.md:522-523 htlc_minimum_msat = minimum HTLC value the peer will accept; bolt07.md:505 htlc_maximum_msat = max value it will send through this channel for a single HTLC.

claims.quote: fee_base_msat (often shortened to base_fee): A flat fee paid for every forwarded HTLC regardless of size.
claims.evidence: BOLT 7 bolt07.md:525-526 'MUST set fee_base_msat to the base fee (in millisatoshi) it will charge for any HTLC'; appears as flat additive term in fee formula bolt07.md:981. Unit msat-vs-sat is the course-wide accepted simplification (concept stated, no value).

claims.quote: fee_proportional_millionths (often shortened to ppm or fee_rate): A proportional fee, in parts per million of the forwarded amount.
claims.evidence: BOLT 7 bolt07.md:527-528 'in millionths of a satoshi ... per transferred satoshi'; multiplied by amount_to_forward/1000000 in formula bolt07.md:981. 'parts per million of the forwarded amount' is accurate.

claims.quote: cltv_expiry_delta: The number of blocks of safety margin the forwarder requires between the timelock on its incoming HTLC and the timelock on its outgoing HTLC. This buffer gives the forwarder enough time to claim its incoming HTLC on-chain if the downstream hop stalls or only reveals the preimage at the very last second.
claims.evidence: BOLT 7 bolt07.md:520-521 'MUST set cltv_expiry_delta to the number of blocks it will subtract from an incoming HTLC's cltv_expiry' => delta = incoming_cltv - outgoing_cltv. BOLT 4 bolt04.md:1499-1500 enforces incoming - outgoing >= cltv_expiry_delta. The 'safety margin to claim incoming on-chain' rationale matches BOLT 7 Recommendations-for-Routing (worst-case unavailability time).

claims.quote: Every major Lightning implementation runs a variant of Dijkstra's algorithm, a classic shortest-path search, over the gossip graph. Each one weights channels a little differently, but they all blend the channel's fee with an estimate of how likely the payment is to actually succeed.
claims.evidence: Not a BOLT claim; well-known about LND/CLN/LDK/Eclair pathfinding (Dijkstra/modified-Dijkstra variants weighting fee + success probability). BOLT 7 bolt07.md:1029-1054 'Recommendations for Routing' frames fee vs cltv tradeoff. General/accurate.

claims.quote: fee = base_fee + (amount_forwarded * fee_per_millionth) // 1_000_000  (route-fee-formula snippet referenced by prose)
claims.evidence: Matches BOLT 7 bolt07.md:981 'fee_base_msat + ( amount_to_forward * fee_proportional_millionths / 1000000 )'. Python // is floor; for positive operands floor == integer truncation, matching BOLT integer arithmetic.

claims.quote: a forwarder with base_fee = 1_000 and fee_per_millionth = 500, forwarding 2,000,000 sats, charges 2,000 sats
She has to work them out from Dave backwards: Dave's amount is fixed by the invoice, and each hop upstream tacks its own fee on top of everything below it.
claims.evidence: BOLT 7 bolt07.md:440-444 'amounts and expiries for HTLCs need to be calculated backward from the destination to the source. The exact initial value for amount_msat ... for the last HTLC ... are provided in the payment request.' Backward accumulation confirmed.

claims.quote: on Route B below, where the first forwarder actually forwards more than 400,000 sats to cover the second forwarder's fee.
claims.evidence: Route B first forwarder = Frank. RouteComparisonDiagram.tsx:81 htlcAmounts [402_002, 401_000, 400_000] => Frank's outgoing (forwarded to Greg) = 401,000 > 400,000. Greg's fee = 200 + (400,000*2000)//1e6 = 1000, so Frank must forward 401,000. Internally consistent.

claims.quote: She also has to set the timeout, the CLTV expiry, on each HTLC. And just like the amounts and fees, the timeouts are computed backwards, starting from the destination
claims.evidence: BOLT 7 bolt07.md:440-444 backward calc; BOLT 4 bolt04.md:263 total_cltv_delta = cltv_delta(0)+...+cltv_delta(n)+min_final_cltv_expiry_delta. CLTV accumulates from destination upward.

claims.quote: his HTLC has to stay valid for at least the invoice's min_final_cltv_expiry_delta blocks past the current height. ... example of calculating the expiries for Route A (Alice paying Dave through Hazel), assuming the current block height is at 150
claims.evidence: Snippet route-timeout-example PythonSnippet.tsx:58: 150+18=168 (Hazel->Dave), 168+1000=1168 (Alice->Hazel, +Hazel cltv_expiry_delta 1000). Matches RouteComparisonDiagram ROUTE_A htlcTimeouts [1168,168] and cltvDelta 1000 (lines 43,46,58-59). BOLT semantics: final hop outgoing_cltv = current_block + min_final_cltv_expiry_delta (bolt04.md:328/343).

claims.quote: the cltv_expiry_delta is the forwarder's safety cushion. It guarantees the forwarder enough time to claim its incoming HTLC on-chain after its outgoing one resolves, even if the downstream hop drags its feet and only reveals the preimage at the last possible second.
claims.evidence: Consistent with C10 and BOLT 7 Recommendations-for-Routing rationale (bolt07.md:1031-1035). The incoming HTLC expires cltv_expiry_delta blocks AFTER the outgoing, giving the forwarder a window to claim incoming after learning the preimage from the resolved outgoing.

claims.quote: let's imagine Alice is paying Dave 400,000 sats. The first two routes (A and B) are already calculated for us. Our job is to calculate the third (route C).
claims.evidence: RouteComparisonDiagram.tsx:36/70 invoice.amount = 400_000 for A and B; RouteCalcExercise (Route C) is the fillable tab (RouteComparisonDiagram.tsx:193). Matches prose.

claims.quote: Alice's node enforces a max_total_cltv_expiry_delta = 200. ... caps how many blocks Alice is willing to lock her funds ... Any candidate route whose total cltv_expiry_delta exceeds that ceiling should not be chosen.
claims.evidence: Matches visual ceiling (RouteComparisonDiagram.tsx:63/108 'max_total_cltv_expiry_delta of 200'). Route A total = Hazel 1000 + min_final 18 = 1018 > 200 (fails); Route B total = 22+20+18 = 60 < 200; Route C total = 20+15+18 = 53 < 200. Concept matches real implementations' max-cltv cap. Consistent.

claims.quote: Route A goes directly through Hazel (one forwarder).
claims.evidence: RouteComparisonDiagram.tsx:33-41 ROUTE_A hops = Alice, Hazel(forwarder), Dave. Exactly one forwarder. 'directly through Hazel' = single intermediary.

claims.quote: Route B goes through Frank and Greg (two forwarders).
claims.evidence: RouteComparisonDiagram.tsx:71-76 ROUTE_B hops = Alice, Frank(forwarder), Greg(forwarder), Dave. Two forwarders. Alternate-route names allowed.

claims.quote: Route C goes through Bob and Charlie. The channel_updates are filled in for you, but the fee math and HTLC values aren't.
claims.evidence: RouteCalcExercise.tsx:197-198 forwarders Bob, Charlie; channelUpdates pre-filled (Bob base15/ppm2000/cltv20; Charlie base10/ppm1000/cltv15, lines 216-217) matching ForwarderPolicyMap NAMED_CHANNEL_UPDATES (ForwarderPolicyMap.tsx:114-115); fee/timeout cells are student-fillable. Matches prose.

claims.quote: note how the cltv_expiry_delta accumulates as we work our way backwards from Dave.
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 bolt04.md:263 total_cltv_delta is a sum of per-hop deltas + min_final; per-hop expiry grows from destination upstream. Route C timeouts 168 -> 183 (+15) -> 203 (+20) confirm accumulation (RouteCalcExercise.tsx:47-49).

claims.quote: the HTLC at the final hop is set to expire first, with each upstream contract expiring later in reverse order. This way, if any intermediary hop closes their channel on-chain or stalls until the very last second to reveal the preimage, their counterparty still has time to grab the preimage and claim funds from their incoming HTLC.
claims.evidence: Final hop has smallest cltv_expiry (e.g. 168) and each upstream HTLC larger (183, 203 / 1168), i.e. expires later. The reverse ordering is exactly the mechanism that lets an upstream node, after seeing the preimage on its outgoing HTLC, still claim its (later-expiring) incoming HTLC. Consistent with BOLT 7 cltv_expiry_delta rationale and CltvSafetyLab.tsx simulate() (Dave->Charlie->Bob claim chain).

claims.quote: imagine all contracts expire at the same time and Charlie waits until the last second to reveal the preimage. If Charlie uses it to claim Bob's HTLC, Bob may not have enough time to turn around and claim his HTLC from Alice ... Alice would claw her funds back via the timeout path, and Dave would still have been paid (since Charlie already paid him). Bob, caught in the middle, eats the loss!
claims.evidence: Trace: Charlie learns preimage only by paying Dave (Dave claims Charlie->Dave HTLC, so Dave is paid). Charlie then claims Bob->Charlie HTLC at the deadline; if Alice->Bob already expired, Alice reclaims via timeout. Bob paid Charlie but cannot collect from Alice => Bob's loss. Cryptographically and economically sound; matches CltvSafetyLab.tsx 'broken same-expiry' default (DEFAULT_EXPIRY 140, line 18) which models exactly this.

claims.quote: By the time she goes to find the ideal path, her node will have accumulated thousands of channel_updates and assembled a live snapshot of the Lightning Network graph.
claims.evidence: Descriptive/qualitative; consistent with gossip accumulation per BOLT 7. No specific number to verify.

claims.quote: Alice has to pay this fee in addition to the actual payment amount.
claims.evidence: Consistent with backward amount accumulation: sender's outgoing amount = invoice amount + sum of downstream fees (BOLT 7 routing example bolt07.md:1126 amount_msat 5010198 > 4999999). Route B: Alice sends 402,002 to deliver 400,000 (RouteComparisonDiagram.tsx:81).

claims.quote: every onion packet is 1,366 bytes ... Header: 34 bytes: 1-byte version, and 33-byte ephemeral pubkey; Payload: 1,300-bytes ...; HMAC: 32-bytes
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:141-160: version(1)+public_key(33)+hop_payloads(1300)+hmac(32). 1+33=34 header, +1300+32=1366 total. Matches.

claims.quote: Bob, the first hop, receives a 306-byte packet and peels his 60-byte hop payload off the front. Then, he forwards the next 246-byte onion packet to Charlie. Charlie peels his 80-byte payload and forwards the next 166-byte onion packet to Dave.
claims.evidence: Strawman (no-fixed-size) arithmetic. Envelope=1+33+32=66. Payloads 60+80+100=240. 66+240=306. 306-60=246. 246-80=166. 166-100=66. All internally consistent with the example 60/80/100 sizes.

claims.quote: intermediate hops should not know where they are in the path. This rule is breaking right in front of our eyes!
claims.evidence: Adversarial: a shrinking packet leaks DOWNSTREAM hops remaining (size proportional to remaining payloads), i.e. distance-to-destination/positional info. It does not leak absolute upstream position, but the educational claim 'know where they are in the path' is a fair reading of a positional leak. Consistent with prompt note (middle forwarder can infer downstream hops remaining). Acceptable as stated.

claims.quote: the Lightning Network uses the Sphinx packet format, which fixes the size of each onion packet at exactly 1,366 bytes for every hop
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:782 'returns a single 1366-byte packet'; fixed size for every hop (forward truncates to 1300 hop_payloads, §960). Matches.

claims.quote: he shifts the inner contents forward to fill the front gap, and then fills the new gap at the back of the buffer with bytes, called "padding"
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:975 'deobfuscating and left-shifting the field'; §977 'the field is pre-padded before forwarding'. The removed front payload creates a back gap filled by deterministic filler. Matches.

claims.quote: The bytes must be indistinguishable from the encrypted hop payloads that came before. If they're not, then a forwarder could still tell where the "real" content ends and the "padding" begins
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:988-989 the XOR 'while simultaneously obfuscating the added 0x00-bytes at the end' — padding is rho-obfuscated so it is ciphertext-indistinguishable. A distinguishable boundary would re-leak route length. Reasoning sound.

claims.quote: before a forwarder does anything, it recomputes the HMAC over the exact bytes it received and drops the packet if the result doesn't match the HMAC in the packet
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:935-939: derive mu, HMAC over hop_payloads||associated_data, constant-time compare, abort if differ — done BEFORE rho-decryption (§940). 'before a forwarder does anything' is accurate re: before decrypting. Matches.

claims.quote: that would invalidate Charlie's HMAC, which Alice computed when building the onion using her shared secret with Charlie
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:810-811 next HMAC computed with mu-key over hop_payloads; mu=HMAC256("mu",ss) where ss is Alice<->Charlie shared secret. Wrong back-padding changes hop_payloads and breaks the HMAC. Matches.

claims.quote: derive his keys, verify the HMAC, prepare the buffer, XOR the whole thing in a single pass, slice off his hop payload from the front, and then fill in the gap before forwarding the next 1,366-byte packet
claims.evidence: Ordering matches BOLT4 bolt04.md:934-961: derive ss->mu, verify HMAC (935-939), derive rho (940), XOR over 2x buffer (941-942), strip front payload (943-952), forward (957-961). Forwarded packet is again 1366 bytes. Matches.

claims.quote: he does it all in one XOR pass over a 2,600-byte buffer ... it pads the 1,300-byte hop_payloads area with 1,300 zero bytes to reach 2 × ROUTING_INFO_SIZE = 2,600 bytes and XORs the whole thing against chacha20(rho_B, 2600)
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:984-987 'pads it with 1300 0x00-bytes, such that the total length is 2*1300' then XORs a stream of matching length. §941 'bytestream of twice the length of hop_payloads'. 2*1300=2600. Matches exactly.

claims.quote: Decrypts Bob's hop payload at the front (the first 60 bytes become plaintext). Peels one of Alice's encryption layers off the downstream payloads and the padding. Produces 60 brand-new bytes at the back, which is the exact same bytes that Alice used when calculating the HMAC.
claims.evidence: Single XOR with rho_B over (hop_payloads||1300 zeros): front 60 bytes (bigsize+payload+hmac for s_B=60) decrypt, inner layers peel one layer, and (zeros XOR rho_B[1300..1359]) = the deterministic filler Alice pre-generated. Consistent with BOLT4 bolt04.md:988-989 and filler reasoning.

claims.quote: Of those 2,600 bytes, the first 1,360 (the original 1,300 plus the 60 new bytes at the back) are what Bob forwards to Charlie.
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:960: forwarded hop_payloads = unwrapped_payloads 'truncated to the incoming hop_payloads size' = 1300 bytes, NOT 1360. After the XOR the meaningful region is positions 0..1359 (1360 bytes), but Bob removes his own 60-byte payload from the FRONT (§949) before forwarding, so he forwards positions 60..1359 = 1300 bytes. Saying 'the first 1,360 ... are what Bob forwards' double-counts (ignores the 60 front bytes dropped) and contradicts line 77 ('the 1,300-byte payload area he forwards to Charlie') and BOLT4.
claims.correctVersion: Of those 2,600 bytes, the meaningful region is the first 1,360 (the original 1,300 plus the 60 new bytes at the back). After Bob slices his 60-byte payload off the front, the remaining 1,300 bytes (positions 60..1,359) are what he forwards to Charlie.

claims.quote: a left-shift by Bob's hop-payload size (his bytes are gone from the front; the remaining content moves up; new bytes are added at the back)
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:975 describes extraction as 'left-shifting the field'. Net effect of removing s_B front bytes + appending s_B back filler = left-shift by s_B. Matches.

claims.quote: Alice ... appends 60 zero bytes (step 2), generates Bob's full 1,360-byte rho_B keystream (step 3), slices off just its trailing 60 bytes (step 4), and XORs the two together (step 5). That is Bob's slice of the filler.
claims.evidence: For s_B=60: keystream length 1300+60=1360; trailing 60 bytes = positions 1300..1359; XOR with 60 zeros gives rho_B[1300..1359]. These are exactly the bytes Bob regenerates at peel (positions 1300..1359 of his 2600-XOR), since chacha20 keystream[0:1360] is a prefix of keystream[0:2600]. Consistent with BOLT4 filler algorithm (bolt04.md:1006-1030).

claims.quote: The bytes at positions 1,300 through 1,359 of his result (zeros ⊕ rho_B[1,300..1,359]) become the back of the 1,300-byte payload area he forwards to Charlie.
claims.evidence: Positions 1300..1359 = 60 bytes (1359-1300+1=60=s_B). In the forwarded region (positions 60..1359), these are the LAST 60 bytes = the back. Correctly states 1,300-byte payload area (consistent with BOLT4 §960 and contradicts the looser line 64). Matches.

claims.quote: It is the same zeros ⊕ rho_B[1,300..1,359] both times, because Alice and Bob share rho_B (each derives it from ss_AB, their ECDH shared secret).
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:940 rho=HMAC256("rho",ss); §934 ss=ECDH(public_key,node_privkey). Alice computes the identical ss via her session key and Bob's pubkey, so identical rho_B, identical keystream positions -> identical bytes. Matches.

claims.quote: Alice has to handle Bob's shift (which affects what Charlie sees as his last s_B bytes ...) and Charlie's shift (which affects what Dave sees as his last s_C bytes ...). Dave doesn't shift anything (he's the final hop), so no filler is generated for the final hop.
claims.evidence: Forwarders are Bob and Charlie only (Dave is destination). BOLT4 bolt04.md:1011 loop runs numHops-1 times; §1035 'last hop need not obfuscate the filler'. Bob's s_B new back-bytes become Charlie's last s_B bytes; Charlie's s_C become Dave's last s_C bytes. Matches.

claims.quote: Alice generates Bob's rho keystream, extended to cover both the original 1,300-byte hop_payloads area and s_B extra bytes past it (so 1,300 + s_B bytes total) ... Alice XORs the last s_B bytes of the 1,300 + s_B keystream into the filler.
claims.evidence: After step 2 filler length = s_B. Last s_B bytes of a (1300+s_B)-byte keystream = positions 1300..(1300+s_B-1) = Bob's back-generated filler positions. Matches the peel-time bytes (C14/C15) and BOLT4 reference algorithm.

claims.quote: the filler has length s_B + s_C: the first s_B bytes still hold Bob's keystream extension from step 2, and the new s_C zero bytes are placeholders ... (which will end up as the last s_C bytes of Dave's view)
claims.evidence: Append s_C zeros to an s_B-length filler -> s_B+s_C total. Charlie's new back bytes (s_C) become the last s_C bytes Dave receives. Consistent with the incremental-filler invariant in BOLT4 bolt04.md:1006-1030.

claims.quote: Alice generates Charlie's rho keystream extended to cover 1,300 + s_C bytes, and XORs the last s_B + s_C bytes of that keystream into the filler. Because the filler is now s_B + s_C bytes long, the s_B + s_C bytes of keystream ... overlap Charlie's regular 1,300-byte region by s_B bytes, which is exactly where Bob's filler bytes will already be sitting when Charlie peels.
claims.evidence: Last s_B+s_C bytes of a (1300+s_C)-byte keystream = positions (1300-s_B)..(1300+s_C-1). Intersection with Charlie's 0..1299 region = (1300-s_B)..1299 = s_B bytes. So the s_B-byte overlap into the regular region is exactly where Bob's accumulated filler sits at Charlie's peel — matching the LND-style incremental filler (BOLT4 left-shift+XOR loop, bolt04.md:1011-1024). Internally and spec-consistent.

claims.quote: Dave is the final hop. Since he doesn't forward the packet, Alice doesn't have to add any filler bytes; there is no next recipient who needs to verify an HMAC.
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:1035-1036 'The last hop need not obfuscate the filler, since it won't forward the packet ... and thus need not extract an HMAC either.' Matches.

claims.quote: you'll write OnionPacketBuilder.generate_filler, which takes the route's rho keys and the hop payload sizes for the forwarder hops, and produces the filler bytes Alice writes into the packet during construction
claims.evidence: Consistent with the prose algorithm: filler is built from each forwarder's rho key and hop-payload size (forwarders Bob+Charlie, not Dave). Not auditing the exercise code body (out of scope), but the prose description is accurate to the algorithm above.

claims.quote: In chapter 5, Onion Routing 101, we saw that every onion packet is 1,366 bytes
claims.evidence: Cross-checked 5.0-onion-routing-101.md:5 'making every packet exactly 1,366 bytes' and :53/:65 '1,300-byte payload area'. The chapter-5 reference and the 1,366/1,300 figures are consistent. No drift.

claims.quote: why fized-size packets are needed
claims.evidence: Typo: 'fized-size' should be 'fixed-size'. Internal-comment-class spelling error in rendered prose.
claims.correctVersion: why fixed-size packets are needed

claims.quote: how do Alice and Bob independently know how to calculate theem?
claims.evidence: Typo: 'theem' should be 'them'.
claims.correctVersion: how do Alice and Bob independently know how to calculate them?

claims.quote: A forwarder does't know their own hop-payload size until after they decrypt it
claims.evidence: Typo: 'does't' should be "doesn't". The underlying claim (forwarder learns its payload length only after decrypting the bigsize prefix) is correct per BOLT4 bolt04.md:943; only the spelling is wrong.
claims.correctVersion: A forwarder doesn't know their own hop-payload size until after they decrypt it

claims.quote: While the mechanism isn't a literal in-place shift, but the result is.
claims.evidence: Ungrammatical 'While ... but' construction. Should drop 'While' or drop 'but'. Grammar only; not a technical error.
claims.correctVersion: The mechanism isn't a literal in-place shift, but the result is.

claims.quote: the encrypted routing info Alice fills with each per-hop payloads
claims.evidence: Grammar: 'with each per-hop payloads' is number-disagreement; should be 'with the per-hop payloads' or 'with each per-hop payload'. Not a technical error.
claims.correctVersion: the encrypted routing info Alice fills with the per-hop payloads

secondaryWording.note: 'In case we haven't hammered it home enough, the key takeaway from the visual above is that both Alice and Bob produce the exact same 60 bytes!' restates the same point already made at lines 46, 48, and again at line 79 ('Since it's the same key, zero padding, and XOR operation, it's the same result'). The 'same bytes both times' idea is repeated 4x; the line-74 framing is redundant with the filler-trace visual's own caption.

'Wow, that was a mouthful!' and 'Watch him receive the packet, derive his keys, verify the HMAC, prepare the buffer, XOR... slice off... and then fill in the gap' pre-narrates the exact step list that the <forwarder-peel> visual's own step captions render, duplicating the StepCaption content in prose.

'bytes, called "padding", that look like more encrypted onion data' then line 34 re-explains the same indistinguishability requirement ('must be indistinguishable from the encrypted hop payloads'). The line-30 parenthetical preview is redundant with the bullet that immediately follows.

claims.quote: version 2 (thumbnail title strip)
claims.evidence: BOLT 3 /tmp/onion-audit/03-transactions.md:85 'version: 3 when using zero_fee_commitments, otherwise 2'. The standard (non-zero-fee) commitment tx is version 2.

claims.quote: locktime 0x20… (thumbnail)
claims.evidence: BOLT 3 03-transactions.md:86 'locktime: upper 8 bits are 0x20, lower 24 bits are the lower 24 bits of the obscured commitment number'. The 0x20 high byte matches; the ellipsis correctly hides the variable lower 24 bits.

claims.quote: inputs 1
claims.evidence: BOLT 3 03-transactions.md:87 'txin count: 1'. A commitment tx always has exactly one input (the funding output).

claims.quote: · funding 2-of-2
claims.evidence: BOLT 3 03-transactions.md:77-79 'The funding output script is a P2WSH to: 2 <pubkey1> <pubkey2> 2 OP_CHECKMULTISIG'. The single input spends a 2-of-2 multisig.

claims.quote: Bitcoin transaction version. Version 2 enables BIP 68 (relative locktimes via sequence), which Lightning channels rely on for the to_local timeout.
claims.evidence: BOLT 3 03-transactions.md:85 (version 2) and :128 'The output is spent by an input with nSequence field set to to_self_delay (which can only be valid after that duration has passed)'. BIP 68 relative locktimes require tx version>=2; the to_local to_self_delay is enforced via nSequence on the version-2 spending tx. Educationally accurate.

claims.quote: the upper 8 bits are 0x20 and the lower 24 bits carry the lower half of the obscured commitment number, the 48-bit count XORed with the lower 48 bits of SHA256(payment_basepoint_open || payment_basepoint_accept)
claims.evidence: BOLT 3 03-transactions.md:86 'locktime: upper 8 bits are 0x20, lower 24 bits are the lower 24 bits of the obscured commitment number' and :93-95 'The 48-bit commitment number is obscured by XOR with the lower 48 bits of: SHA256(payment_basepoint from open_channel || payment_basepoint from accept_channel)'. 'lower half' of a 48-bit number = lower 24 bits; ordering open||accept matches.

claims.quote: That lets a node recognize which state was broadcast without leaking the channel's payment count to outside observers.
claims.evidence: BOLT 3 03-transactions.md:97-99 'This obscures the number of commitments made on the channel in the case of unilateral close, yet still provides a useful index for both nodes (who know the payment_basepoints) to quickly find a revoked commitment transaction.'

claims.quote: locktime: 0x20‖
claims.evidence: BOLT 3 03-transactions.md:86. 0x20 high byte concatenated with the lower-half (lower 24 bits) of the obscured commitment number; the <commit_lo> placeholder correctly denotes the lower half.

claims.quote: A commitment tx always has exactly one input: the channel's funding output (a 2-of-2 multisig).
claims.evidence: BOLT 3 03-transactions.md:87-90 (txin count: 1; outpoint = funding txid/output_index) and :77-79 (funding output P2WSH 2-of-2).

claims.quote: Transaction ID of the previous output being spent. For a commitment tx, this is the funding transaction's txid, the on-chain anchor of the channel.
claims.evidence: BOLT 3 03-transactions.md:88 'txin[0] outpoint: txid and output_index from funding_created message'. The outpoint references the funding transaction.

claims.quote: Output index within the funding transaction. The funding output is at index 0 in our model.
claims.evidence: Presented as 'in our model'; matches BOLT 3 worked example 03-transactions.md:1276 'funding_output_index: 0' and :1256 'funding output: 0'. Acceptable model assumption.

claims.quote: scriptSig: (empty) — Legacy script-signature field. SegWit moved authentication into the witness, so this stays empty.
claims.evidence: BOLT 3 03-transactions.md:90 'txin[0] script bytes: 0' (empty scriptSig); authentication is in the witness :91.

claims.quote: the upper 8 bits are 0x80 and the lower 24 bits carry the upper half of the obscured commitment number (locktime holds the lower half).
claims.evidence: BOLT 3 03-transactions.md:89 'txin[0] sequence: upper 8 bits are 0x80, lower 24 bits are upper 24 bits of the obscured commitment number'. 'upper half' = upper 24 bits; complementary to locktime's lower half. CORRECT.

claims.quote: The 0x80 high byte also leaves BIP 68 relative-timelock disabled on this input; the per-output to_self_delay lives in the output scripts instead.
claims.evidence: BIP 68: nSequence is a relative lock-time only if bit 31 (the disable flag) is clear. Upper 8 bits 0x80 = binary 10000000, so bit 31 is set, disabling relative locktime on this input. Confirmed consistent with BOLT 3 03-transactions.md:89. The to_self_delay is enforced on the to_local OUTPUT's spend (:128), not this commitment input.

claims.quote: sequence: 0x80‖
claims.evidence: BOLT 3 03-transactions.md:89. 0x80 high byte concatenated with upper-half (upper 24 bits) of obscured commitment number; <commit_hi> placeholder denotes the upper half. CORRECT.

claims.quote: The SegWit witness: the script and signatures that authorize spending the input. The funding output is a 2-of-2 multisig, so its witness contains both channel partners' signatures.
claims.evidence: BOLT 3 03-transactions.md:91 'txin[0] witness: 0 <signature_for_pubkey1> <signature_for_pubkey2>' and :77-79 P2WSH 2-of-2. Witness carries both signatures.

claims.quote: <alice_sig> <bob_sig>
claims.evidence: BOLT 3 03-transactions.md:91 specifies the witness as '0 <signature_for_pubkey1> <signature_for_pubkey2>' — there is a mandatory leading dummy '0' element (OP_CHECKMULTISIG off-by-one bug) and the two sigs are ordered by lexicographic pubkey, not by party. The card omits the leading 0 and labels sigs by party name. This is a deliberate pedagogical simplification (sigs labeled by channel partner; Alice/Bob are valid main-path characters), not a protocol error. Internally consistent with the 2-of-2 framing.
claims.correctVersion: Strictly per BOLT 3 the witness is '0 <sig_for_pubkey1> <sig_for_pubkey2>' (leading dummy 0, sigs ordered by lexicographic pubkey); the named-party simplification is acceptable for teaching.

claims.quote: Both signatures are required to spend the funding output. Each side signs the other's commitment tx so they can broadcast it unilaterally if the channel goes uncooperative.
claims.evidence: BOLT 3 funding output is a 2-of-2 multisig (:79), requiring both signatures. The cross-signing model (each party holds the counterparty's signature for unilateral broadcast) is standard Lightning channel mechanics and consistent with the 2-of-2 design.

claims.quote: o.immediate ? OP_0 <pubkey_hash> (P2WPKH) : OP_0 <script_hash> (P2WSH)
claims.evidence: Maps immediate output (to_remote) to P2WPKH 'OP_0 <pubkey_hash>' and delayed output (to_local, carrying to_self_delay) to P2WSH 'OP_0 <script_hash>'. Consistent with BOLT 3: to_remote is a simple key payment while to_local/HTLC outputs use witness scripts (delay/penalty branches). Field comment :33-36 correctly notes to_remote is pubkey-hash not script-hash.

claims.quote: A typical commitment tx has two outputs (to_local and to_remote) plus an extra HTLC output for each in-flight payment.
claims.evidence: BOLT 3 'Commitment Transaction Outputs' section: to_local and to_remote outputs plus one output per offered/received HTLC. Matches the described structure.

claims.quote: WitnessScriptPanel: witness script lines -> SHA256 -> <script_hash> fills the placeholder in scriptPubKey
claims.evidence: A P2WSH scriptPubKey is 'OP_0 <32-byte SHA256(witnessScript)>'. The panel correctly shows witness script -> SHA256 -> <script_hash>. Consistent with BIP141/BOLT 3 P2WSH semantics.

claims.quote: IMMEDIATE outputs tinted green; DELAYED tinted amber. (RoleTag renders IMMEDIATE/DELAYED)
claims.evidence: Internal labeling: to_local (script-hash, to_self_delay) -> DELAYED; to_remote (pubkey-hash, spendable immediately) -> IMMEDIATE. Consistent with BOLT 3 (to_local funds delayed by to_self_delay :104, to_remote immediately spendable). Pure visual convention, internally consistent.

visual CommitmentTxCard.tsx.coverage: Audited the entire file client/src/components/onion-routing-draft/CommitmentTxCard.tsx (721 lines, all of it). This is a dual-mode (thumbnail/full) non-stepped diagram component, so there are no animation beats/steps; I examined every rendered string, tooltip, literal value, and code comment. Coverage of rendered surfaces: (1) thumbnail title strip + transaction frame (version 2, locktime 0x20…, inputs 1, funding 2-of-2) — lines 290-358; (2) full-mode version/locktime block with both tooltips — lines 433-451; (3) full-mode inputs block (txid/index/scriptSig/sequence) with four tooltips — lines 453-497; (4) full-mode outputs block + scriptPubKey rendering (OP_0 <pubkey_hash> vs OP_0 <script_hash>) — lines 499-530, 199-227; (5) witness block (<alice_sig> <bob_sig>) with two tooltips — lines 532-555; (6) WitnessScriptPanel SHA256->script_hash flow — lines 561-718; (7) RoleTag IMMEDIATE/DELAYED and HTLC payment_hash/cltv/IN-FLIGHT rows — lines 94-104, 228-250. Verified every numeric/crypto claim against BOLT 3 (/tmp/onion-audit/03-transactions.md): version (:85), locktime upper-8-bits 0x20 + lower 24 bits (:86), obscuring XOR with lower 48 bits of SHA256(payment_basepoint open||accept) (:93-95, intent :97-99), txin count 1 / outpoint / scriptSig empty / sequence upper-8-bits 0x80 (:87-90), witness 0<sig1><sig2> (:91), P2WSH 2-of-2 funding (:77-79), funding_output_index 0 (:1256,:1276). Confirmed no stray characters (no Carol/Hazel/etc.) and no forbidden terminology (router/slot/slice-as-noun/preamble) via grep. I did NOT independently re-derive the SHA256 obscuring-factor value 0x2bb038521914 against the test-vector basepoints (the component does not render any concrete obscured-number hex, only the 0x20/0x80 high bytes and <commit_lo>/<commit_hi> placeholders, so no concrete value needs cross-checking). No INCORRECT or MISLEADING claims found; the only deviation from a strict BOLT 3 reading is the witness simplification (C17), which is a deliberate, internally-consistent pedagogical choice.

secondaryWording.note: The sequence tooltip restates '(locktime holds the lower half)', which is already explained in full in the adjacent locktime tooltip at :444. Minor cross-tooltip redundancy; harmless and arguably helpful for a hover-isolated tooltip, but flagged as over-explaining.

Thumbnail comment narrates 'hover the card for the full transaction' which duplicates the visible 'hover for the full transaction' affordance text at :357. Comment-vs-UI redundancy only (not user-facing duplication).
The locktime tooltip is quite long (covers obscuring, XOR, basepoint concatenation, and the privacy rationale in one breath). Educationally dense but accurate; could be split, low priority.

Cross-checked every prose factual claim against: the capstone trace engine (client/src/lib/onion-capstone-trace.ts), the orchestrator (client/src/lib/onion-capstone-orchestrator.ts), the exercise definitions (client/src/data/onion-routing-exercises-draft.ts), the lab component (CapstoneStage.tsx / OnionCapstoneLab.tsx), sibling chapters 1, 2, 10, and the staged BOLT 4 (/tmp/onion-audit/bolt04.md). chapter 11.0-error-onion.coverage: Audited the full prose of /Users/austinv2/code/onion-routing/client/public/onion_routing_tutorial/11.0-error-onion.md (115 lines). Verified every factual/numeric/crypto assertion against the staged primary sources: BOLT 4 error-onion section (/tmp/onion-audit/bolt04.md lines 1038-1267: Erring node return-packet layout, um/ammag key gen, pad>=256 rule, intermediate XOR re-wrap, origin iterative decrypt), BOLT 4 worked Returning-Errors trace (lines 1782-1854, 5-hop, failure node 4), the simpler error vector /tmp/onion-audit/onion-error-test.json (failure_message 2002), and key generation (lines 104-135). Recomputed byte decompositions in Python: simpler vector payload = 260 bytes (failure_len 2 + msg 2 + pad_len 2 + pad 254; failure_len+pad_len=256), confirming the chapter's 260/292-byte example; spec worked example payload = 1028 bytes with failure_len 320 + pad_len 704 = 1024, raw_error_packet = 1060 bytes, confirming the chapter's '1,024 bytes / 1,060-byte packet' claim. Cross-checked the inline <python-snippet> bodies in client/src/components/onion-routing-draft/PythonSnippet.tsx (ids error-package/error-hmac/error-wrap/error-rewrap/error-trial-peel/error-parse, lines 161-184) against the surrounding prose formulas. Confirmed main path Alice->Bob->Charlie->Dave (1.0-a-lightning-payment.md line 10) so the error example (Charlie fails, Bob re-wraps) is internally consistent. Did NOT audit the embedded visual components (<error-boomerang>, <error-unwrap>), the checkpoint (cp-error-trial-decrypt-draft), or the exercise (exercise-decrypt-error-onion-draft) per instructions — those are other agents' scope, though I recorded the prose numbers for cross-cutting drift checks. No terminology/character/em-dash violations found.

claims.quote: When a hop decides to fail a payment, it authenticates the failure message with its `um` key, encrypts it with its `ammag` keystream, and then hands it to its *upstream* peer.
claims.evidence: BOLT4 /tmp/onion-audit/bolt04.md:1066-1072 — return packet hmac uses key type `um`; erring node then generates `ammag` key and XORs the packet. Upstream = toward sender (Alice); Charlie's upstream peer is Bob. Order (auth then encrypt) matches spec.

claims.quote: As each upstream hop receives the message, it will add one more `ammag` layer of encryption and pass it along.
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:1146-1149,1188 — every intermediate node generates its `ammag` key and XORs (obfuscation step identical to erring node), then MUST return-forward.

claims.quote: Alice doesn't know which hop the error came from, so she finds out by trial and error: peel a layer with Bob's `ammag`, check the result against Bob's `um` HMAC. If there is no match, then she tries again with Charlie's key... Whichever hop's keys produce a valid HMAC, that's the failing hop
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:1198,1223-1226 — origin iteratively decrypts with each hop's ammag, computes HMAC with hop's um; when it matches `hmac`, that hop is the sender of the failure. Peel order most-recent-first (Bob then Charlie) is required for the HMAC to verify at the correct position.

claims.quote: Alice can trust the error... that it wasn't forged or modified along the way back. Why? Because only the failing hop knows its own `um` key.
claims.evidence: um is derived from the Alice-Charlie shared secret (bolt04.md:111,119-122 key type `um`=0x756d over ss). Only Alice and Charlie hold ss_AC, so a valid um_charlie HMAC authenticates Charlie as author; no intermediate (Bob) can forge it. From Alice's verifier standpoint the claim holds.

claims.quote: The hops between the failing one and Alice can't read what the error says, or even tell where it originated. Each hop only knows its own `ammag` and `um` keys, not anyone else's.
claims.evidence: Bob receives error XORed under ammag_charlie which he lacks, so he cannot decrypt (bolt04.md:1146-1149 intermediate only re-XORs, never reads). Bob knows the error arrived from his downstream peer Charlie's direction but cannot determine the true origin hop (could be Charlie or any node beyond) — standard Sphinx property. Each forwarder derives keys only from its own ss.

claims.quote: Re-wrapping at each hop means no two channels ever carry correlating bytes, the same property the forward onion gets from changing at every hop.
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:1146-1149 — each hop applies its own ammag XOR so the bytes on each channel differ; this is the deliberate analogue of the forward onion's per-hop transformation. Rationale matches spec intent (untraceability).

claims.quote: Charlie constructs the failure message: the failure code plus any associated data (like a `channel_update` for `UPDATE`-flagged failures).
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:1251-1253 failuremsg = 2-byte failure_code + type data; bolt04.md:1269 `0x1000 (UPDATE)`; bolt04.md:1303-1347 UPDATE-flagged codes (temporary_channel_failure, fee_insufficient, incorrect_cltv_expiry...) each carry `[len*byte:channel_update]`.

claims.quote: he writes four pieces, one after another (260 bytes total in our example): `failure_len` (2 bytes)... the `failure_message` itself (`failure_len` bytes)... `pad_len` (2 bytes)... the padding itself (`pad_len` bytes).
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:1059-1064 return packet = [32:hmac][u16:failure_len][failure_len:failuremsg][u16:pad_len][pad_len:pad]. The 4 post-HMAC fields match. Simpler vector onion-error-test.json hop[4].payload decodes (Python) to 260 bytes: failure_len=2, msg=2002, pad_len=254, total 2+2+2+254=260.

claims.quote: BOLT 4 only constrains the *length* of the pad, not its contents, but senders conventionally zero-fill it (and the spec's own test vector uses `0x00` bytes)
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:1134-1135 only constrains failure_len+pad_len length, says nothing about pad contents. onion-error-test.json hop[4].payload pad bytes are all 0x00 (254 zero bytes verified in Python). Worked example payload (bolt04.md:1817) pad is also 0x00.

claims.quote: Per BOLT 4, `failure_len + pad_len` must come to at least 256... and the two length fields bring the total to 260.
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:1134 'MUST set `pad` such that the `failure_len` plus `pad_len` is at least 256'. In the 260-byte example: failure_len+pad_len=256, plus the two 2-byte length fields = 260. Confirmed by onion-error-test.json decode (256+4=260).

claims.quote: what about failure messages *bigger* than 256 bytes... Those get padded to a bigger fixed total instead.
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:1134-1137 mandates only failure_len+pad_len>=256 (SHOULD be exactly 256). There is no spec-mandated 'fixed' larger total for big messages; it is implementation/test-vector convention. The word 'fixed' slightly over-states the spec, but the chapter immediately anchors it to the test vector (which is correct), and the conceptual point is sound. Acceptable for a tutorial deep-dive.
claims.correctVersion: Soften 'Those get padded to a bigger fixed total instead' to e.g. 'Those get padded to a larger total' (BOLT 4 only mandates failure_len+pad_len >= 256; the exact larger size is convention, not a fixed spec value).

claims.quote: In fact, the spec's own test vector pads to 1,024 bytes, making a 1,060-byte packet!
claims.evidence: Python decode of BOLT4 bolt04.md:1817 payload: failure_len=320, pad_len=704, failure_len+pad_len=1024, payload total=1028 bytes; raw_error_packet (bolt04.md:1819) = 1060 bytes (= 32-byte HMAC + 1028). Both numbers exact.

claims.quote: Charlie computes an HMAC over the 260-byte payload with his `um` key: `error_hmac = HMAC-SHA256(um_charlie, payload)`.
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:1066-1067 hmac authenticates the remainder of the packet with key type `um`; key gen uses SHA256 (bolt04.md:119). HMAC covers the payload (everything after the 32-byte hmac). Matches snippet error-hmac (PythonSnippet.tsx:167 hmac.new(um_charlie, payload, hashlib.sha256)).

claims.quote: he prepends the HMAC to the payload: `error_hmac (32 bytes) || payload (260 bytes)`. That's the full error packet, 292 bytes in all for our example.
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:1059-1060 [32*byte:hmac] is the first field. 32 + 260 = 292. Arithmetic correct; field order (hmac first) matches spec.

claims.quote: he encrypts the whole packet by XOR-ing it with his `ammag` keystream: `wrapped = error_packet ⊕ ChaCha20(ammag_charlie, len(error_packet))`.
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:1070-1072 — erring node generates `ammag` key, generates a pseudo-random stream (ChaCha20, bolt04.md:131-133), applies to the packet using XOR. Whole-packet XOR over its full length. Matches snippet error-wrap.

claims.quote: When Bob receives the error onion... he doesn't know what's inside (he doesn't have Charlie's `ammag` key), and BOLT 4 says he doesn't need to. His only job is to wrap one more layer: `wrapped = wrapped ⊕ ChaCha20(ammag_bob, len(wrapped))`.
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:1146-1149 intermediate node only generates ammag key and XORs ('obfuscation step identical to the erring node'); it never decrypts/reads. XOR over however-many bytes arrived (fixed size, no re-padding). Matches snippet error-rewrap.

claims.quote: If there were more hops between Alice and Charlie (say, a 5-hop route), each one would wrap with its own `ammag` in turn. By the time the error reaches Alice, it's been XORed with every intermediate hop's keystream plus the originating Charlie's keystream.
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:1187-1189 all intermediate nodes MUST transform (XOR) and return-forward. The wire bytes Alice receives = error XORed by Charlie then each intermediate in turn. Matches the spec's 5-hop worked trace (bolt04.md:1821-1853 successive ammag XOR stages).

claims.quote: Alice... has shared secrets ss_AB, ss_AC, ss_AD (one per hop) and can derive the corresponding `um` and `ammag` keys for each.
claims.evidence: Main path Alice->Bob->Charlie->Dave (1.0-a-lightning-payment.md:10). Alice shares a secret with each of Bob/Charlie/Dave; she precomputes um+ammag for all because she does not know which hop will fail. BOLT4 bolt04.md:1195-1196 origin 'generates the ammag, ammagext and um keys for each hop in the route.' Listing all three is correct general capability even though this example fails at Charlie.

claims.quote: Check whether the leading 32 bytes are a valid HMAC over the rest under that hop's `um` key, i.e. whether `HMAC-SHA256(um_i, wrapped[32:])` equals `wrapped[:32]`.
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:1223-1225 compute HMAC for return packet with hop's um; when it matches `hmac` (the leading 32 bytes), that hop is the failure source. wrapped[:32]=hmac, wrapped[32:]=authenticated payload. Matches snippet error-trial-peel.

claims.quote: The wrapping order from the network's perspective is: Charlie XORs first, then Bob XORs... the *outermost* layer is the most recent hop's wrap (Bob's...), and the *innermost* is Charlie's (the failing hop). Alice peels in the order Bob → Charlie, which is `i = 0, 1, ...` since hop 0 is the first forwarder.
claims.evidence: Charlie wraps first (bolt04.md:1070-1072), Bob wraps last before Alice (bolt04.md:1146). To verify Charlie's HMAC, Alice must remove exactly Bob's layer then Charlie's, leaving `error`. Peeling order (most-recent/outermost Bob=i0 first, then Charlie=i1) is required: peeling Charlie first would leave error XOR ammag_bob and fail. Indexing i=0 first forwarder (Bob) is self-consistent.

claims.quote: What if she peels through every layer and never finds a valid HMAC? Then the error packet is malformed, which means someone along the return path tampered with it (or sent garbage).
claims.evidence: If no hop's um verifies, the origin cannot attribute the failure (BOLT4 bolt04.md:1239 'When the failure source cannot be identified from the return packet'). Implies corruption/tampering on the return path. Accurate.

claims.quote: First, Charlie builds: `error = HMAC(um_charlie, padded_msg) || padded_msg`. Then he wraps: `wire = error ⊕ ammag_charlie_stream` and sends `wire` back to Bob.
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:1059-1072: hmac (over remainder) prepended to packet, then ammag XOR. padded_msg = failure_len||failuremsg||pad_len||pad. Charlie's upstream peer is Bob (Alice->Bob->Charlie). Consistent with C1/C13/C15.

claims.quote: Iteration i=0 (Bob's keys): `peeled = wire ⊕ ammag_bob_stream`. ... Bob isn't the failing hop, so his `um` won't authenticate... Iteration i=1 (Charlie's keys): `peeled = peeled ⊕ ammag_charlie_stream`. Now `peeled` is exactly Charlie's original `error` packet, so the HMAC check will validate... parse the length prefix at `peeled[32:34]`... read that many bytes starting at `peeled[34:]`.
claims.evidence: wire = error XOR ammag_charlie XOR ammag_bob. i=0 removes ammag_bob -> error XOR ammag_charlie (Bob um fails). i=1 removes ammag_charlie -> error (Charlie um verifies). In `error`: bytes [0:32]=hmac, [32:34]=failure_len, [34:]=failuremsg. Slicing exact. Matches snippet error-parse (which reads payload[0:2]/payload[2:] on payload=peeled[32:]).

claims.quote: the failing hop is identified by **whose `um` key verifies**, which Alice checks by trial-and-decrypt as she peels.
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:1223-1226 — the hop whose um produces a matching HMAC is the sender of the failure. Restates C3/C19 accurately.

claims.quote: he sends `wrapped` back to Bob, his upstream hop, as the failure data on the HTLC.
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:1147 stores the wrapped bytes as the `reason` field of update_fail_htlc/update_htlc_fail. The chapter abstracts this as 'failure data on the HTLC' — substantively correct, consistent with the course's level of wire-message abstraction. Upstream peer = Bob, correct.

claims.quote: a node might be offline, a channel might lack liquidity, a CLTV might be too tight, or an invoice might have expired.
claims.evidence: Illustrative, maps to real failure codes: temporary_node_failure (offline), temporary_channel_failure (liquidity), incorrect_cltv_expiry/expiry_too_soon (tight CLTV), incorrect_or_unknown_payment_details (expired invoice). No precise code claim made; all plausible.

claims.quote: (whole chapter) — no payment_hash field is stated to be present in the error/failure message.
claims.evidence: Failure messages (BOLT4 bolt04.md:1251-1463) carry failure_code + type-specific fields (sha256_of_onion, channel_update, htlc_msat, etc.) but never a payment_hash. The chapter correctly never claims a payment_hash is in the error onion, consistent with the boomerang-back-to-source design (association handled out-of-band via the HTLC, bolt04.md:1228-1229).

secondaryWording.note: The 'So, just to hammer things home...' sentence restates the identification rule already given verbatim in the high-level review (line 11) and parsed in the walkthrough (line 101). Mild intra-prose redundancy / over-explaining; not a technical issue.
'You'll see why she starts with Bob below.' is a forward-pointer that duplicates the explanation delivered nine lines later (line 81). Minor over-signposting.

visual FeeCalculatorModal.tsx.coverage: Read the entire file client/src/components/onion-routing-draft/FeeCalculatorModal.tsx (309 lines, single component + LabeledNumberInput helper). This is a non-stepped interactive calculator widget, not a stepped diagram, so there are no beats/steps to enumerate; the only varying state is user-typed input. I examined every factual/numeric assertion: the docstring formula (line 15), the computed formula (line 138-139), the prose blurb (lines 198-199), all three input labels (base_fee/fee_per_millionth/amount, lines 204-216), the result-block equation rendering and labels (Base Fee / Fee Rate / Total, lines 232-261), and the default seed values (lines 30-32). Cross-checked against BOLT 7 HTLC Fees (/tmp/onion-audit/bolt07.md:981 formula and the worked example at :1118-1120 yielding 10199, which proves FLOOR not round). Cross-checked internal consistency against chapter 2 markdown (2.0-pathfinding-101.md:19-20,50) and the PythonSnippet registry (route-fee-formula, route-fee-example, ch10 policy snippet) which all use Python `//` floor division and the identical fee_per_millionth shorthand. This calculator is the anchor of the requested "drift cluster" and ALL cluster members agree on FLOOR. Could not (and need not) execute the widget in a browser; verified math by hand. Note: amounts are in sats (documented intended simplification), whereas BOLT uses msat — flagged as ACCEPTABLE and internally consistent.

claims.quote: total = base_fee + floor((fee_per_millionth * amount) / 1_000_000)
claims.evidence: BOLT 7 /tmp/onion-audit/bolt07.md:981 'fee_base_msat + ( amount_to_forward * fee_proportional_millionths / 1000000 )'. Multiplication is commutative so fee_per_millionth*amount == amount*fee_proportional_millionths. FLOOR is confirmed by the worked example bolt07.md:1120 '200 + ( 4999999 * 2000 / 1000000 ) = 10199' (9999.998 -> 9999, not rounded to 10000).

claims.quote: const feeRate = Math.floor((ppm * amt) / 1_000_000);
claims.evidence: Implements BOLT 7 proportional fee with floor. Matches the worked example bolt07.md:1120 (floor, not round). Also matches the course-wide '//' floor-division convention in PythonSnippet.tsx route-fee-formula ('(amount_forwarded * fee_per_millionth) // 1_000_000') and the ch10 policy snippet (line 189) whose comment states 'same convention as chapter 2's fee calculator'. Math.floor of a non-negative quotient == Python //.

claims.quote: const total = base + feeRate;
claims.evidence: Matches BOLT 7 bolt07.md:981: total = fee_base_msat + proportional_part. base + floor(proportional).

claims.quote: The Lightning forwarding fee floors the fee_rate result (real nodes drop fractional sats).
claims.evidence: Confirms FLOOR semantics, matching bolt07.md:1120 (4999999*2000/1000000 = 9999.998 floored to 9999). 'drop fractional sats' is an accurate plain-English description of integer floor division. Note BOLT amounts are msat not sats, but sats is the course's documented intended simplification.

claims.quote: label="base_fee (sats)"
claims.evidence: BOLT 7 field is fee_base_msat (millisatoshi). Course uses 'base_fee' shorthand and sats unit by design: 2.0-pathfinding-101.md:19 'fee_base_msat (often shortened to base_fee)'. Unit sats vs msat is the course's stated simplification and is internally consistent.

claims.quote: label="fee_per_millionth"
claims.evidence: BOLT 7 field is fee_proportional_millionths (bolt07.md:457,527). Course uses 'fee_per_millionth' as the consistent shorthand: 2.0-pathfinding-101.md:50 'fee_per_millionth = 500' and PythonSnippet.tsx route-fee-formula. Internally consistent across the cluster.

claims.quote: label="amount (sats)"
claims.evidence: BOLT 7 uses amount_to_forward in msat; course uses sats by documented simplification. Label is the proportional-fee base, consistent with bolt07.md:981.

claims.quote: ({fmt(ppm)} / 1,000,000) × {fmt(amt)}  =  {fmt(feeRate)}
claims.evidence: Displayed real-valued expression (ppm/1,000,000)*amt equals (ppm*amt)/1,000,000 over the reals; the bold result {feeRate} is the floored integer (line 138). The expression intentionally shows the pre-floor real value and the bold number shows the floored result, with the floor itself documented in the prose (lines 198-199). Not misleading. With defaults (3000/1,000,000)*400000 = 1200 exactly.

claims.quote: useState<string>("100") ... useState<string>("3000") ... useState<string>("400000")
claims.evidence: Default seed: base_fee=100, fee_per_millionth=3000, amount=400000. feeRate = floor(3000*400000/1,000,000) = floor(1200.0) = 1200; total = 100+1200 = 1300. Renders 'Base Fee=100', 'Fee Rate=1,200', 'Total=1,300'. Arithmetic verified by hand; 400,000 sats matches the ch2 Alice->Dave scenario. (Defaults divide evenly so they don't themselves demonstrate the floor, but the widget is interactive.)

claims.quote: BOLT 7 forwarding fee formula
claims.evidence: The fee formula is indeed specified in BOLT 7 'HTLC Fees' (bolt07.md:975-981), not BOLT 4. Attribution is correct.

claims.quote: Fee Rate
claims.evidence: 'Fee Rate' as the label for the proportional component is consistent with 2.0-pathfinding-101.md:20 'fee_proportional_millionths (often shortened to ppm or fee_rate)'. Internally consistent.

secondaryWording.note: Prose 'real nodes drop fractional sats' restates the parenthetical idea of flooring twice in one sentence ('floors the fee_rate result' then 'drop fractional sats'). Minor redundancy; both are accurate. Low priority, not a technical issue.

claims.quote: math: `${fc.baseFee} + (${fmt(fc.feePpm)} / 1,000,000 × ${fmt(fc.amount)}) = ${fmt(fc.total)} sats`
claims.evidence: BOLT 7 /tmp/onion-audit/bolt07.md:981 'fee_base_msat + ( amount_to_forward * fee_proportional_millionths / 1000000 )'. The rendered math matches base + (ppm/1e6 × amount) = total. Verified for every consumer-supplied fc (Route A Hazel 100+(2000/1e6×400000)=900; Route B Frank 200+(2000/1e6×401000)=1002, Greg 200+(...×400000)=1000).

claims.quote: `${fc.forwarderName} takes ${fc.baseFee} flat, plus ${fmt(fc.feePpm)} ppm of the ${fmt(fc.amount)} sats passing through.` + (grew ? ` (Notice that's ${fmt(fc.amount)}, not ${fmt(invoice.amount)}: this hop's amount has to cover the fees of every hop after it.)` : ``)
claims.evidence: The 'grew' branch fires only when fc.amount != invoice.amount, i.e. for upstream hops whose forwarded amount includes downstream fees. Simulated: Route B Frank (amount 401,000 != 400,000) correctly shows the 'Notice that's 401,000, not 400,000' clause; Route B Greg and Route A Hazel (amount==400,000) correctly omit it. Matches the back-to-front fee accumulation in BOLT 7 routing.

claims.quote: Dave's HTLC has to stay valid until the current block height plus the invoice's min_final_cltv_expiry_delta.
claims.evidence: Final-hop timeout = current_height + min_final_cltv_expiry_delta. Consumer data: Charlie/Greg/Hazel→Dave timeout = 150 + 18 = 168 (150 = teaching current-height constant, 18 = min_final, the BOLT 7 default per /tmp/onion-audit/bolt07.md:1079 'min_final_cltv_expiry_delta of 18 (the default)'). 'Start at Dave' kicker is hardcoded but every route's receiver is Dave.

claims.quote: The incoming HTLC has to expire later than the outgoing one by that forwarder's cltv_expiry_delta, so we stack it on top.
claims.evidence: Backward CLTV accumulation: each hop's incoming timeout = outgoing timeout + that hop's cltv_expiry_delta. Verified per route: Route B A→F = 150+18+20+22=210, F→G=150+18+20=188 (deltas 22=Frank,20=Greg); Route C A→B=150+18+15+20=203 (20=Bob), B→C=150+18+15=183 (15=Charlie). Consistent with BOLT 7 cltv_expiry_delta semantics (/tmp/onion-audit/bolt07.md:520).

claims.quote: math: `${rev[0].calculation} = ${rev[0].total}` ... math: `${tc.calculation} = ${tc.total}`
claims.evidence: Walkthrough echoes consumer timeoutCalculations verbatim. All calculation strings evaluate to their stated totals: '150 + 18'=168, '150 + 18 + 1000'=1168 (Route A), '150 + 18 + 20'=188, '150 + 18 + 20 + 22'=210 (Route B), '150 + 18 + 15'=183, '150 + 18 + 15 + 20'=203 (Route C).

claims.quote: {(fc.total - fc.baseFee).toLocaleString("en-US")}
claims.evidence: FeeCalcCard renders the proportional part as total - baseFee. For all consumer data this equals floor(ppm×amount/1e6): Route A 900-100=800=floor(2000×400000/1e6); Route B Frank 1002-200=802=floor(2000×401000/1e6), Greg 1000-200=800. The card's hardcoded 'calculation' strings ('...=800','...=802') agree with total-baseFee.

claims.quote: htlcAmounts:  [400_900, 400_000], htlcTimeouts: [1168, 168]
claims.evidence: Route A (Alice→Hazel→Dave). Hazel fee = 100 + floor(2000×400000/1e6) = 100+800 = 900, so Alice→Hazel amount = 400000+900 = 400900. Timeouts: Hazel→Dave 150+18=168; Alice→Hazel 150+18+1000=1168 (1000 = Hazel cltvDelta). All match.

Route B (Alice→Frank→Greg→Dave). Greg fee 200+floor(2000×400000/1e6)=1000 → Frank receives 401000; Frank fee 200+floor(2000×401000/1e6)=200+802=1002 → Alice sends 402002. Timeouts 168/188/210 verified (deltas Frank 22, Greg 20). All match.

claims.quote: expression="150 + 18 + 15 + 20" ... expression="150 + 18 + 15" ... expression="150 + 18"

visual ComputedRouteDiagram.tsx.coverage: Audited client/src/components/onion-routing-draft/ComputedRouteDiagram.tsx in full (presentational component, 889 lines). Because it is purely presentational (all amounts/fees/CLTVs are props), I traced every numeric/formula claim back through its two data-supplying consumers — RouteComparisonDiagram.tsx (Route A via Hazel, Route B via Frank+Greg) and RouteCalcExercise.tsx (Route C = the focus chapter-2 DRIFT CLUSTER, Alice→Bob→Charlie→Dave) — plus ChannelUpdateCard.tsx and FeeCalculatorModal.tsx (fee formula cross-check). Steps/beats covered in the stepped walkthrough overlay (buildWalkthrough): I simulated the full step list for Route A (7 steps: goal, why-fee, Hazel fee, why-timeout, Start-at-Dave, Alice→Hazel timeout, verdict) and Route B (8 steps: goal, why-fee, Frank fee w/ 'Notice' clause, Greg fee, why-timeout, Start-at-Dave, Frank→Greg, Alice→Frank, verdict); Route C uses RouteCalcExercise's fillable EXPECTED values, all verified. Verified BOLT 7 fee floor (800.82→800), backward CLTV stacking, total-fee and CLTV-delta subtitles, and the route-picker feedback math (1225, 777, 1018, 200 ceiling). Cross-checked the 200-block ceiling claim against 2.0-pathfinding-101.md:74. RESULT: every checkable assertion is CORRECT and internally consistent; no INCORRECT/MISLEADING findings. NOTE ON SCOPE: this diagram renders NO Sphinx/onion packet bytes or hex — there are no 1366/1300/32/33 byte-size claims in this component, so those canonical decompositions were not applicable here. I did not run the app/Pyodide; arithmetic was verified with an independent Python reimplementation of the BOLT 7 formulas.

visual RouteCalcExercise.tsx.coverage: Read the full target file RouteCalcExercise.tsx (964 lines) and cross-checked it against three sibling components that establish the canonical Route-A/B/C numbers and shared formula: ComputedRouteDiagram.tsx (static analog, same layout), RouteComparisonDiagram.tsx (defines ROUTE_A via Hazel and ROUTE_B via Frank/Greg and wires Route C to this exercise), and FeeCalculatorModal.tsx (shared floored fee formula). Also read BOLT 7 (fee formula line 981, cltv_expiry_delta semantics lines 438-439/520-521, routing example) and grepped BOLT 4 for CLTV/fee context, plus the chapter-2 markdown 2.0-pathfinding-101.md (line 74 defines max_total_cltv_expiry_delta = 200 as the ceiling on TOTAL cltv_expiry_delta). Verified all arithmetic with a calculator: BOLT-7 floored fees, the backward CLTV stacking, the fee-on-outgoing-amount mapping, route totals, and the picker deltas. This is a fee/CLTV route-calculation exercise, NOT a Sphinx packet diagram: there are no onion-packet byte/hex/length literals in the file, so the 1366=1+33+1300+32 decomposition and 32/33/66/292/1060 sizes legitimately do not appear and were not applicable. I examined every numeric literal in EXPECTED, every channelUpdate, both FillableFeeCard instances, all three FillableTimeoutCard expressions, the invoice card, all three RoutePickerPanel buttons, and all three feedback strings. Every checkable number reconciles. No INCORRECT/MISLEADING claims found; one redundant-but-correct override and a couple of low-priority wording redundancies noted.

visual CltvSafetyLab.tsx.coverage: Read the entire file (697 lines) including: module-level comment block, all constants (expiries, fees, amounts, colors), the simulate() function, all 5 steps of viewAtStep() (step 0 setup, step 1 Dave reveals, step 2 Charlie claims w/ success+fail branches, step 3 Bob claims w/ 3 branches, step 4 final summary w/ 3 branches), the caption accent/title logic, the constraint-enforcement setters (handleSetExpiryAB/BC/CD + clampExpiry), the ContractCard input min wiring, NodeRow roles/labels, and the footer claim-latency assumption text. Verified against BOLT 4 (forwarding cltv requirement, lines 1499-1503: incoming cltv_expiry - outgoing_cltv_value >= cltv_expiry_delta) and BOLT 7 HTLC fee formula (line 981). NOTE: I could NOT render the component (static review only) and could not inspect the StepCaption child component's internals, but all caption text is passed in from this file. This component has NO Sphinx-packet/byte/hex/length-field claims, so the 1366=1+33+1300+32 / 2600 / 32-33-66-292-1060 decomposition checks are N/A here.

claims.quote: Setters enforce the constraint expiryAB ≤ expiryBC ≤ expiryCD: as you move left-to-right across the cards, expiries can only go up. Lower values are clamped to the previous card's value.
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 bolt04.md:1499-1503 requires incoming cltv_expiry - outgoing_cltv_value >= cltv_expiry_delta, so the UPSTREAM (sender-side) HTLC must have the LARGER expiry. On path Alice->Bob->Charlie->Dave the correct ordering is expiryAB > expiryBC > expiryCD (A->B largest, C->D smallest). This code clamps the OPPOSITE (expiryAB<=expiryBC<=expiryCD), forcing the destination-side HTLC to be largest. It also CONTRADICTS this component's own simulate(): charlieClaimSucceeds = (expiryCD-1)+blocksPerHop < expiryBC requires expiryBC > expiryCD, and bobClaimSucceeds requires expiryAB > expiryCD+1. Under the enforced clamp expiryAB<=expiryCD, so bobClaimSucceeds can NEVER be true and the lab's 'everyone settled cleanly' success state (step 3 success branch + step 4 clean summary) is UNREACHABLE by the student.
claims.correctVersion: Invert the constraint to expiryAB >= expiryBC >= expiryCD. handleSetExpiryAB should cascade DOWN (BC/CD lowered if they exceed AB) or set AB's floor above BC; the per-card minExpiry props (lines 339/350/361) and clamp mins must be flipped so the A->B (upstream) HTLC is forced to be the largest. Comment should read 'expiryAB >= expiryBC >= expiryCD ... expiries can only go DOWN left-to-right.'

claims.quote: // Cascade upward: BC and CD may need to rise to keep BC ≥ AB and CD ≥ BC.
claims.evidence: Same root cause as C1. 'BC >= AB and CD >= BC' encodes the inverted ordering. BOLT 4 bolt04.md:1499-1503 requires the upstream HTLC (AB) to have the larger expiry, i.e. AB >= BC >= CD. This comment + the if (expiryBC < next) cascade on lines 271-273 push downstream expiries UP to match the sender, which is the wrong direction.
claims.correctVersion: Cascade should keep AB >= BC >= CD: raising A->B must NOT force B->C/C->D up; lowering an upstream card should pull downstream cards down. Comment should encode AB >= BC >= CD.

claims.quote: minExpiry={expiryAB}
claims.evidence: Wires the B->C card's input min to expiryAB, enforcing expiryBC >= expiryAB in the rendered number input (and the title tooltip 'Must be >= {minExpiry}', line 670). BOLT 4 bolt04.md:1499-1503 requires the opposite: expiryAB (upstream) >= expiryBC. The student is UI-blocked from entering the only configurations the simulation rewards.
claims.correctVersion: The upstream A->B card should be the one with the higher floor; the B->C card's min should not be tied to expiryAB in a way that forces BC>=AB. Flip so AB>=BC>=CD is the enforced/typed-in ordering.

claims.quote: minExpiry={expiryBC}
claims.evidence: Wires the C->D card's input min to expiryBC, enforcing expiryCD >= expiryBC, i.e. the destination-side HTLC is forced largest. BOLT 4 bolt04.md:1499-1503: the destination-side (C->D) HTLC must be the SMALLEST. Combined with C3 this makes C->D the global max, exactly backwards.
claims.correctVersion: C->D is the downstream-most hop and should have the smallest expiry; its min should be MIN_EXPIRY (the floor), not expiryBC.

claims.quote: const daveRevealsAt = expiryCD - 1; ... const charlieClaimsBobAt = daveRevealsAt + blocksPerHop; const bobClaimsAliceAt = daveRevealsAt + 2 * blocksPerHop; const charlieClaimSucceeds = charlieClaimsBobAt < expiryBC; const bobClaimSucceeds = charlieClaimSucceeds && bobClaimsAliceAt < expiryAB;
claims.evidence: This is the BOLT-4-correct half. Dave reveals just before C->D expires (expiryCD-1); each upstream claim needs blocksPerHop more blocks; Charlie succeeds only if his claim lands before B->C expires; Bob only if before A->B expires AND Charlie already succeeded (the preimage propagates upstream). Success requires expiryAB > expiryBC > expiryCD, exactly the BOLT 4 ordering (bolt04.md:1499-1503). The bug is that the CONSTRAINT/UI half (C1-C4) contradicts this correct simulation.

claims.quote: Defaults to all 140 (the broken same-expiry case).
claims.evidence: With all expiries=140, blocksPerHop=1: daveRevealsAt=139, charlieClaimsBobAt=140, charlieClaimSucceeds = 140 < 140 = false -> Charlie burned. The same-expiry case correctly fails because a forwarder has zero margin to claim upstream after learning the preimage. Matches BOLT 4 cltv_expiry_delta>0 requirement (bolt04.md:1499-1503). Internally consistent default.

claims.quote:
const BOB_TO_CHARLIE = DAVE_AMOUNT + CHARLIE_FEE; // 10,002
const ALICE_TO_BOB = DAVE_AMOUNT + CHARLIE_FEE + BOB_FEE; // 10,003
claims.evidence: 10000+2=10002 and 10000+2+1=10003; inline comments match. Cumulative-fee model is consistent with BOLT 7 fee direction (each forwarder's incoming HTLC = outgoing amount + its fee). bolt07.md:981 gives fee_base + amt*ppm/1e6; flat per-hop sat fees are an acceptable scoped simplification (amounts in sats, fee details scoped out).

claims.quote: just before his HTLC expires, Dave reveals the preimage on-chain to claim Charlie's payment. He pockets 10,000 sats.
claims.evidence: daveRevealsAt=expiryCD-1 is one block before the C->D HTLC (expiryCD) expires (line 80). DAVE_AMOUNT=10,000 (line 28). Dave is payee on the C->D HTLC, so he collects 10,000. Consistent.

claims.quote: It confirms before block ${sim.expiryBC}, so Charlie collects ${BOB_TO_CHARLIE.toLocaleString("en-US")} sats.
claims.evidence: Success branch is gated on charlieClaimSucceeds = charlieClaimsBobAt < expiryBC (line 84), so 'confirms before block expiryBC' is accurate. Charlie is payee on B->C HTLC, collects BOB_TO_CHARLIE=10,002. Consistent.

claims.quote: It confirms before block ${sim.expiryAB}, so Bob collects ${ALICE_TO_BOB.toLocaleString("en-US")} sats and keeps a ${BOB_FEE}-sat fee for his trouble.
claims.evidence: Branch gated on bobClaimSucceeds requiring bobClaimsAliceAt < expiryAB (line 85). ALICE_TO_BOB=10,003, BOB_FEE=1. Bob receives 10,003, paid Charlie 10,002, nets 1 sat. Arithmetic consistent. (Reachability of this branch is blocked by the C1 constraint bug, but the text itself is numerically correct.)

claims.quote: Charlie earned ${CHARLIE_FEE} sats, Bob earned ${BOB_FEE} sat, and Alice paid ${ALICE_TO_BOB.toLocaleString("en-US")} sats to deliver ${DAVE_AMOUNT.toLocaleString("en-US")} to Dave.
claims.evidence: CHARLIE_FEE=2, BOB_FEE=1, ALICE_TO_BOB=10,003, DAVE_AMOUNT=10,000. Alice pays 10,003; Bob keeps 1; Charlie keeps 2; Dave receives 10,000. 10000+2+1=10003 balances. Consistent.

claims.quote: his claim broadcast at block ${sim.bobClaimsAliceAt}, but Alice's HTLC expired at block ${sim.expiryAB}, so he missed it by ${-slack + 1} block(s).
claims.evidence: slack = expiryAB - bobClaimsAliceAt (line 199). Failure means bobClaimsAliceAt >= expiryAB (success needs strict <), so slack <= 0 and missed-by = -slack+1 >= 1. At bobClaimsAliceAt == expiryAB, the HTLC is already timed out at that height, so a claim landing at expiryAB is 1 block too late: 'missed by 1' is correct off-by-one handling.

claims.quote: His claim broadcast at block ${sim.charlieClaimsBobAt}, but Bob's HTLC to him expired at block ${sim.expiryBC}, so he missed it by ${-slack + 1} block(s).
claims.evidence: slack = expiryBC - charlieClaimsBobAt. charlieClaimSucceeds needs charlieClaimsBobAt < expiryBC, so failure -> slack <= 0, missed-by >= 1. Same correct off-by-one as C12. 'Bob's HTLC to him' correctly identifies the B->C HTLC where Charlie is payee.

claims.quote: assume each on-chain claim takes 1 block to confirm ... so Charlie claims at block {sim.charlieClaimsBobAt}, Bob at block {sim.bobClaimsAliceAt}
claims.evidence: CLAIM_LATENCY=1 (line 25) is passed as blocksPerHop (line 232). charlieClaimsBobAt = daveRevealsAt+1, bobClaimsAliceAt = daveRevealsAt+2 (lines 82-83). Footer text matches the simulation's derived values. Stated assumption, internally consistent.

claims.quote: id === "alice" ? "Sender" : id === "dave" ? "Destination" : "Forwarder"
claims.evidence: Alice=Sender, Dave=Destination, Bob/Charlie=Forwarder. Matches approved terminology ('forwarder', not 'router'). No stray Carol/Hazel; grep confirmed only Alice/Bob/Charlie/Dave on the path.

claims.quote: ⏱ expired, refunded to ${fromLabel}
claims.evidence: On HTLC timeout the payer (from = upstream node) reclaims the funds. For A->B HTLC fromLabel=Alice, etc. Refund-to-payer-on-timeout is correct HTLC semantics (BOLT 3/2 timeout path). Consistent with captions e.g. line 157 'Bob's HTLC times out and Bob gets refunded'.

claims.quote: const DEFAULT_EXPIRY = 140; const MIN_EXPIRY = 101; ... const MAX_EXPIRY = 300;
claims.evidence: Illustrative absolute block heights with room for the default-140 broken case and student edits up to 300. No spec value to contradict (these are example heights, not BOLT constants). Internally consistent with clamp/cascade ranges.

secondaryWording.note: Footer 'so Charlie claims at block X, Bob at block Y' restates values already derivable from / shown in the step captions (steps 2 and 3 state the same block numbers). Mild redundancy with the per-step narration, though it doubles as a standing assumption label so it is largely justified.

Step 0 caption is long and partly over-explains ('Nothing interesting happens until the deadlines get close, so let's pick it up right before they do...') beyond what the StepCaption title 'Setup' plus the block counter already convey.

claims.quote: a 140px wide cream rectangle
