Callers pass hop.color from the shared palette (ComputedRouteDiagram.tsx:610, RouteCalcExercise.tsx:379). Shape and intent match.

claims.quote: 1.5px ink border
claims.evidence: Comment line 5 claims a 1.5px ink border; code applies a 1.5px border with borderColor INK (the ink hex, line 16). Consistent.

claims.quote: carrying the 1,366 bytes of onion data
claims.evidence: BOLT2 2790: 1366-byte onion_routing_packet; onion-test.json packet=1366B.

claims.quote: ss_AB = SHA256(bob_privkey x E_AB)
claims.evidence: BOLT4 715-719: ECDH point serialized compressed then SHA256; chapter elides compress step.
claims.correctVersion: ss=SHA256(compress(bob_privkey x E_AB)).

claims.quote: rho_B, mu_B, um_B, or ammag_B (the four per-hop keys, all derived from ss_AB)
claims.evidence: BOLT4 108-121 rho/mu/um from ss; ammag also per-hop from ss (1070). 4th core type pad is origin-only, never derived by forwarder; ammag is the right substitution. All four derive from ss_AB.

claims.quote: perform ECDH between his node private key and E_AB, recovering the very same shared secret
claims.evidence: BOLT4 725-728: hop uses ephemeral pubkey + own node privkey; same value.

claims.quote: version (1 byte) ... ephemeral_pubkey (33 bytes) ... hop_payloads (1,300 bytes) ... outer_hmac (32 bytes)
version (1 byte): must be 0x00. Any other value gets rejected.
claims.evidence: BOLT4 162 const 0x00; 918-919 if version!=0 MUST abort/fail.

claims.quote: mu_B = HMAC('mu', ss_AB) ... rho_B = HMAC('rho', ss_AB)
claims.evidence: BOLT4 119-121,935,940: HMAC(keytype,ss); order matches.

claims.quote: recomputes the HMAC tag over the encrypted hop_payloads concatenated with associated_data
claims.evidence: BOLT4 936-939: HMAC256(mu, hop_payloads||AD) over encrypted payload, verified before decrypt; matches step3-before-step4 ordering.

claims.quote: append 1,300 zero bytes ... making a 2,600-byte working buffer ... XORs it across the full 2,600 bytes
claims.evidence: BOLT4 941-942: bytestream of TWICE hop_payloads length (2600); unwrapped=hop_payloads XOR bytestream; zero-extend to 2600 is exact (x XOR 0=x).

claims.quote: rho keystream that's twice the wire size and XORs it across a 2,600-byte working buffer
Bob only needs the first s_B of remaining keystream (~60 in our example), he will discard the rest
claims.evidence: Leading 1300B = Bob's mix-header (942); s_B~60 matches course example size. Phrase conflates payload SIZE with keystream AMOUNT; intent (tail beyond 1300 discarded) correct but loose.
claims.correctVersion: s_B (~60) is Bob's payload LENGTH, not an amount of remaining keystream; the buffer tail beyond 1300B is what's discarded/truncated.

claims.quote: BOLT 4 sidesteps this by specifying that hops should generate enough keystream that any possible hop size is covered
claims.evidence: BOLT4 941: 2600B keystream covers any payload length before bigsize length is parsed.

claims.quote: bigsize_LEN (1-9 bytes)
claims.evidence: BOLT1 587: BigSize is 1,3,5, or 9 bytes (not 2/4/6/7/8); 1-9 is a correct outer range, loosely implies intermediates.
claims.correctVersion: BigSize prefix is 1,3,5, or 9 bytes.

claims.quote: TLV fields (amt_to_forward, outgoing_cltv_value, short_channel_id)
claims.evidence: BOLT4 193-202: types 2/4/6; length excludes prefix+HMAC (99-100).

claims.quote: next_hmac (32 bytes): ... outer HMAC field of his outgoing packet to Charlie (this is charlie_hmac)
claims.evidence: BOLT4 950-952 next_hmac from front; 961 outgoing hmac=next_hmac; construction commits each hop HMAC over next layer (810-814).

claims.quote: For TLV-section lengths under 253 bytes ... it's just a single byte
claims.evidence: BOLT1 591: uint8(x) if x<0xfd (253).

claims.quote: the 1,300 bytes right after Bob's hop payload are the hop_payloads Alice built for Charlie ... If Bob lifted anything other ... the HMAC ... would fail
claims.evidence: BOLT4 960 truncate to 1300; 801-805 right-shift places next hop behind; Charlie HMAC over exact hop_payloads||AD (936).

claims.quote: bf_AB = SHA256(E_AB concat ss_AB)
claims.evidence: BOLT4 743-746,956: SHA256(compressed pubkey||ss); mod-n elided (acceptable).

claims.quote: E_AC = bf_AB x E_AB
claims.evidence: BOLT4 737-738,959,775-776: scalar mult of point by blinding factor; epk_next=bf x epk.

claims.quote: the same E_AC Alice derived ... perform ECDH between his node privkey and E_AC ... same ss_AC
claims.evidence: BOLT4 732-741 deterministic blinding; 934 ss=ECDH(public_key,node_privkey); Charlie ss_AC matches.

claims.quote: version stays 0x00 ... ephemeral_pubkey = E_AC ... hop_payloads = the 1,300-byte slice ... outer_hmac = next_hmac (equals charlie_hmac)
claims.evidence: BOLT4 957-961: version 0, public_key=incoming x tweak, hop_payloads truncated 1300, hmac=next_hmac.

claims.quote: hands the packet to Charlie over the BOLT 8 noise transport
claims.evidence: BOLT8 is the Noise-based Lightning peer transport; update_add_htlc travels over it.

claims.quote: Since it's 1,366 bytes, Charlie has no idea how many hops there are before or after him
claims.evidence: BOLT4 13-19: nodes cannot learn route length or their position. Adversarial: middle forwarder holds only fixed 1300B region; filler (971-982) is indistinguishable from real payloads, so neither upstream count/position nor downstream count inferable. Matches spec guarantee.

claims.quote: Bob rejects and sends back an error onion
claims.evidence: BOLT4 938-939 HMAC mismatch MUST abort/fail; error return-onion (1070+). Detail deferred to ch11.

claims.quote: return three things: the next packet, Bob's raw hop-payload bytes ... and the shared secret
claims.evidence: Consistent w/ deferral: parse->ch10, error-encryption uses ammag/um derived from shared secret->ch11 (1070,1196).

claims.quote: text="SHA256(curve_point)" (ECDH panel: priv_label · E_X = SHA256(curve_point) -> ss_AX)
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 bolt04.md:715-719 'Elliptic-curve Diffie-Hellman between the sender's ephemeral key at that hop and the hop's node ID key. The resulting curve point is serialized to the compressed format and hashed using SHA256.' During forwarding (bolt04.md:725-727) the hop uses 'the ephemeral public key and its own node ID private key'. Diagram shows bob_priv · E_Bob -> SHA256(curve_point) -> ss_AB, matching. Compressed-serialization detail is omitted but is taught in chapter prose 4.0-shared-secrets.md:12.

claims.quote: Bob ... derive ss_AB ... Charlie ... gives him ss_AC ... Dave ... gives him ss_AD
claims.evidence: Shared-secret labels ss_AB/ss_AC/ss_AD match chapter 4.0-shared-secrets.md:71,77,83 (Alice side) and :98,102 (forwarder side), and the checkpoint explanation in onion-routing-tutorial.tsx:357. Naming is internally consistent across diagram, prose, and checkpoint.

claims.quote: He runs his own private key against E_Bob from his hop payload to derive ss_AB
claims.evidence: This is the commutative ECDH property: bob_priv · E_Bob = e_Bob · B (chapter 4.0:98 'this is the same ss_AB that Alice derived using e_AB · B, because scalar multiplication on the curve is commutative'). Matches BOLT 4 bolt04.md:727-728.

claims.quote: Charlie's and Dave's hop payloads are still riding along, and they're still pubkeys Bob has no use for.
claims.evidence: Adversarial wire-byte check: in this no-blinding strawman Bob holds the whole packet and sees E_Charlie/E_Dave in the clear, but they ECDH only with Charlie's/Dave's node keys, not Bob's, so Bob derives nothing usable from them. The diagram correctly does NOT claim Bob can recover other ephemeral private keys (that is the WRONG distractor option 4 in onion-routing-tutorial.tsx:353,357). No over-claim about route-length inference either.

claims.quote: ephemeral_pubkeys · in the clear ... 3 entries
claims.evidence: Chapter 4.0-shared-secrets.md:44 'she'll include all three ephemeral pubkeys in the packet ... Each key will sit in plain sight, as they have to be readable: a forwarder needs its ephemeral pubkey to perform ECDH'. 3 entries for the 3-hop Bob/Charlie/Dave forwarder set is consistent. This is the cost in checkpoint option 0 (onion-routing-tutorial.tsx:350).

claims.quote: amt_to_forward 10,002 sat (Bob); amt_to_forward 10,000 sat (Charlie); final_amt 10,000 sat (Dave)
claims.evidence: Internal fee consistency: Bob forwards 10,002 to Charlie; Charlie forwards 10,000 to Dave (keeps 2-sat fee); Dave's final_amt 10,000 == Charlie's amt_to_forward. Amounts decrease toward recipient, matching BOLT 7 bolt07.md:1114-1130 convention (amt_to_forward in a hop payload is what that hop sends out; intermediate keeps the fee = base + amt*ppm/1e6). Sats-not-msat is an approved simplification.

claims.quote: outgoing_cltv block 220 (Bob); outgoing_cltv block 180 (Charlie); final_cltv block 180 (Dave)
claims.evidence: Internal CLTV consistency: Bob's outgoing HTLC to Charlie expires at 220; Charlie's outgoing HTLC to Dave expires at 180; Dave's received/final cltv 180 == Charlie's outgoing_cltv. Delta 220-180=40 = Charlie's cltv_expiry_delta. CLTVs decrease toward recipient, matching BOLT 7 bolt07.md:1124-1130 / :520-521 (each hop subtracts its cltv_expiry_delta).

All three illustrative ephemeral pubkeys use valid secp256k1 compressed-point prefix bytes (0x02 or 0x03 only). BOLT 4 bolt04.md:142 '33-byte compressed secp256k1 public_key'. The truncated '...' hex is illustrative, not a test vector, which is appropriate for a pre-Sphinx teaching strawman.

claims.quote: (e_Bob, E_Bob), (e_Charlie, E_Charlie), (e_Dave, E_Dave) ... 3 keypairs persisted
claims.evidence: Matches chapter 4.0-shared-secrets.md:40 'Alice would generate one (ephemeral private key, ephemeral public key) pair per forwarder ... (e_Bob, E_Bob), (e_Charlie, E_Charlie), and (e_Dave, E_Dave)'. lowercase-private/uppercase-public convention upheld. 3 keypairs for a 3-forwarder route is correct and is checkpoint option 2 (onion-routing-tutorial.tsx:352).

claims.quote: Errors come back encrypted with these shared secrets, so she can't toss them out mid-payment.
claims.evidence: Matches the rationale in checkpoint answer onion-routing-tutorial.tsx:357 option 3: 'failures come back encrypted with those same shared secrets and she needs the keys to decrypt the error onion'. Consistent with BOLT 4 error-onion design (um key derived from the shared secret). Internally consistent with the chapter's stated cost.

claims.quote: payment_hash: 0xa3f1...e9c4 (shown both at top of ONION_PACKET header and inside Dave's final hop payload)
claims.evidence: In real BOLT 4, payment_hash travels in update_add_htlc (BOLT 2), not the onion header; inside the onion only the final hop payload carries payment-related TLVs. Here a top-level header payment_hash is a loose pre-Sphinx convenience, but it is internally consistent (same value 0xa3f1...e9c4 appears in Dave's final hop payload, line 98). visual HmacChainDiagram.tsx.coverage: Fully read client/src/components/onion-routing-draft/HmacChainDiagram.tsx (202 lines). This is a SINGLE-VIEW SVG diagram with hover-focus interaction (3 packet rows: Bob/Charlie/Dave), NOT a multi-beat stepper, so there are no sequential steps to enumerate; hovering each of the 3 packets isolates the same factual content (its commit bracket + outgoing nesting arrow + the next packet's outer HMAC). I extracted every factual assertion from: the 3 PACKETS specs (lines 48-58: ephemeral keys E_AB/E_AC/E_AD, lenHex 0x1B/0x2F/0x43, payloadBytes 60/80/100 B, inner/outer HMAC names, ghost segments), the "1,366 bytes" size label (line 123), the CommitBracket label (line 183), the NestingArrow label (line 197), the encrypted-remainder label (line 153), and the StepCaption title+caption (lines 91-92). Cross-checked against BOLT 4 (bolt04.md): packet structure (137-171), construction (797-818), decryption/all-zero-final-HMAC (936-965), filler (971-1024); BOLT 1 BigSize length rule (01-messaging.md:99-100, 138); and the onion-test.json vector. Also cross-checked the course's own ch5 markdown (5.0-onion-routing-101.md:79-88) which precedes the <hmac-chain> tag and the E_AB/E_AC/E_AD convention defined in ch4 (4.0-shared-secrets.md:70/76/82) and used in ch6 (6.0-key-derivation.md:10). I could NOT render the component in a browser; verification is static (source + spec + arithmetic). The ghost-segment pixel widths are explicitly illustrative (w percentages), so I judged only their LABELS/ORDER for internal consistency, not exact byte proportions.

claims.quote: {tag} is {next}'s outer HMAC  (renders: 'charlie_hmac is Charlie's outer HMAC', 'dave_hmac is Dave's outer HMAC')

Only 2 arrows rendered (PACKETS.slice(0,2), line 83), correctly omitting Dave who has no next hop.

claims.quote: {spec.outerHmac} commits to all of this  (bracket spans whole payload area PAY_L..PAY_R)

PAY_L=HEADER_R=118, PAY_R=C.encR=762 spans the full payload region (own entry + encrypted remainder), so the bracket correctly shows the HMAC covering the whole buffer.

claims.quote: Each HMAC commits to the layer beneath it
claims.evidence: The HMAC actually commits to the ENTIRE hop_payloads buffer including the hop's OWN entry (LEN+payload+inner HMAC), not only the layers 'beneath it' (bolt04.md:936). The body of THIS diagram (CommitBracket at :183 spans the whole payload area) and ch5 md:86 ('commits to everything below it: the hop payload, the next-inner HMAC, and all the encrypted layers underneath') both render the fuller, correct scope. The title's 'the layer beneath it' is a slight understatement, but the rendered bracket + caption correct it, so impact is low.
claims.correctVersion: 'Each HMAC commits to everything in the payload buffer beneath and including its own entry' (or keep, since bracket/caption already clarify).

claims.quote: if a forwarder alters any byte beneath its own layer, the next hop's recomputed HMAC won't match what's in the packet, so the packet gets dropped.
claims.evidence: After Bob strips his layer and forwards, Charlie recomputes HMAC256(mu_charlie, hop_payloads||ad) and compares to charlie_hmac (bolt04.md:936-939, 'If the computed HMAC and hmac differ: MUST abort processing the packet and fail.'). Any byte Bob alters in the forwarded buffer (everything beneath his stripped layer) changes Charlie's recomputed HMAC, which won't equal the charlie_hmac Alice baked in. Tamper detection by the NEXT hop is accurate.

BigSize length EXCLUDES the 1-byte length prefix and the 32-byte trailing HMAC (bolt04.md:99-100, 01-messaging.md:138). With single-byte BigSize prefix (values <0xFD): 60-1-32=27=0x1B; 80-1-32=47=0x2F; 100-1-32=67=0x43. All three match exactly. Fixed 60/80/100 sizes are the sanctioned simplification.

Matches BOLT 4 single-session-key blinded chain (bolt04.md:762-776). Internally consistent.

Widths are explicitly illustrative (w percentages, line 116). Minor cosmetic inconsistency: 'filler' is drawn for Charlie/Dave but omitted from Bob's ghost row, though filler is part of all three 1300B buffers; since the remainder is uniform ciphertext to a forwarder anyway, this is a teaching device, not a wire-fact error.

No over-claim that the hop can infer route length/position is made.

Verified every literal: SLOT_BYTES {bob60,charlie80,dave100}, SLOT_BIGSIZE_HEX {0x1B,0x2F,0x43}, the caption tlvSize formula, layer counts (Bob1/Charlie2/Dave3/padding3), packet assembly order, version 0x00, ephemeral pubkey, HMAC chain pills, and the 1300 / 32 / 33 / 1 decomposition. Could NOT execute the React component in a browser (static read only, per review-only constraint), so visual rendering/animation timing was reasoned from code, not observed. claims.quote: SLOT_BIGSIZE_HEX = { bob: "0x1B", // 60-byte slot → 27 ; charlie: "0x2F", // 80-byte slot → 47 ; dave: "0x43", // 100-byte slot → 67 }

BOLT 4 line 100-101 confirms the bigsize excludes both the 1-byte prefix and the 32-byte trailing HMAC. The correct comment at lines 643-644 already says 'slot total minus the 1-byte bigsize prefix and the trailing 32-byte HMAC field'. claims.correctVersion: Should read '(slot_size - 33)' i.e. slot_size minus the 1-byte bigsize prefix and the 32-byte HMAC field. (Also: the single-byte bigsize applies when the encoded length value is <253, i.e. slot_size<286, not literally 'hop payloads ≤252 bytes'.)

claims.quote: const tlvSize = slotSize - 1 - 32; ... His full hop payload runs ${slotSize} bytes (1-byte bigsize prefix + ${tlvSize}-byte TLV records + 32-byte HMAC field).
claims.evidence: slotSize = 1 (bigsize) + tlvSize (TLV payload) + 32 (HMAC). So tlvSize = slotSize-33: Bob 27, Charlie 47, Dave 67. Matches the bigsize LEN value (the LEN encodes exactly the TLV payload length). Consistent with BOLT 4 hop_payloads structure [bigsize length][length*byte payload][32*byte hmac] (lines 167-172) and shift_size = 1+l+32 (line 801).

claims.quote: we'll right-shift hop_payloads by ${slotSize} bytes ... The trailing ${slotSize} bytes fall off the back so we stay at 1,300 ... Right-shift hop_payloads by ${slotSize} bytes (last ${slotSize} bytes drop off)
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 lines 801-803: shift_size = 1+l+32 = slot total; 'hop_payloads field is right-shifted by shift_size bytes, discarding the last shift_size bytes that exceed its 1300-byte size.' slotSize here equals 1+tlv+32 = full slot = shift_size. Matches spec exactly.

A region accumulates one layer per encrypt round at/after its own write. Computed: Dave present for all 3 rounds =3; Charlie for charlie+bob =2; Bob for bob only =1; padding present since step0 =3. 'keyed by Bob's shared secret' is a teaching simplification: the mu key is derived from the shared secret (mu = HMAC256('mu', ss)); acceptable. associated_data = payment_hash is the common case (BOLT line 759 'payment_hash is commonly used').

Both denote the same operation; this is an internal notation inconsistency, not a BOLT error. BOLT 4 line 85 specifies a fixed null nonce, so the explicit 0 is defensible; the two forms just disagree with each other.

The component subtracts cumulative SLOT_BYTES of all written slots, matching the real buffer (trailing bytes that fell off are correctly no longer counted). Final padding 1060 = 1300-60-80-100. Decomposition with the three slots (240) + padding (1060) = 1300. Correct.

This nesting lets each forwarder verify the packet it receives. Matches BOLT 4 decryption (line 936 HMAC check) and the per-hop nested-HMAC design. nextHopName via BUILD_ORDER.indexOf+1 (dave→charlie, charlie→bob) is correct.

claims.quote: HEADER ... version 0x00 ... ephemeral pubkey E_AB
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 line 162 version constant 0x00; lines 142-143 33-byte compressed pubkey used for shared-secret generation. onion-test.json confirms leading 00 then 33-byte pubkey. E_AB = ephemeral pubkey for the first hop (Bob) is the correct label for the header field.

claims.quote: HEADER (34 bytes). Two fields: a 1-byte version (0x00) and the 33-byte ephemeral public key.
claims.evidence: bolt04.md:141-143,157-158 version byte plus 33-byte pubkey; 1+33=34; 0x00 at :162.

claims.quote: Each forwarder performs ECDH between this pubkey and its node private key to derive its shared secret with Alice.
claims.evidence: bolt04.md:142-143 pubkey for shared-secret gen; ephemeral key tweaked per hop (:956-959), so downstream forwarder ECDHs a tweaked pubkey not literal E_AB. Standard ch5 teaching; consistent with E_AB token.

claims.quote: ephemeral pubkey E_AB ... 34 bytes

claims.quote: PAYLOAD AREA (1,300 bytes). A fixed 1,300-byte buffer.
claims.evidence: bolt04.md:144,165 hop_payloads is 1300 bytes.

claims.quote: Always the same size whatever the route length, so no one (forwarders included) can tell how long the route is.
claims.evidence: Fixed 1300B (:165); filler indistinguishable from real payloads (:806-809). Middle forwarder re-pads to same 1300B (:960), downstream encrypted, upstream gone; cannot infer route length or position. Sphinx unlinkability.

claims.quote: HMAC TAG (32 bytes). Authentication tag over the payload area. The hop recomputes it ... checks it before decrypting; a mismatch drops the packet.
claims.evidence: bolt04.md:146 32-byte hmac; mu-key over hop_payloads (:810-811); verified before payload extraction (:940-952). over the payload area also covers associated_data (:811), acceptable ch5 simplification; ordering correct.

claims.quote: Bob's hop payload (60 bytes): amt_to_forward, outgoing_cltv_value, and short_channel_id ... Ends with a 32-byte HMAC field (charlie_hmac), which Bob lifts into the outer HMAC of the packet he sends Charlie.
claims.evidence: TLV types 2/4/6 (:194-202); forwarding includes short_channel_id (:282). HMAC lifting: forwarded hmac set to next_hmac (:961). 60B slot is agreed simplification.

claims.quote: Charlie's hop payload (80 bytes). Its HMAC field carries dave_hmac, which Charlie lifts into the outer tag of the packet he forwards to Dave.
claims.evidence: Same lifting mechanic (:961). 80B slot is agreed simplification.

claims.quote: Dave's hop payload (100 bytes): amt_to_forward, outgoing_cltv_value, and payment_data (payment_secret + total_msat from the invoice). Its HMAC field is 32 zero bytes, the signal that Dave is the destination and should claim the payment, not forward it.
claims.evidence: payment_data type 8 = payment_secret+total_msat (:203-206); last-node values (:690-700). All-zero next_hmac = final destination (:955,964). 100B slot is agreed simplification.

claims.quote: slotBytes dave 100/charlie 80/else 60; lenHex dave 0x43/charlie 0x2F/else 0x1B
claims.evidence: LEN = total-1-32 (bolt04.md:100,801). 60-33=27=0x1B; 80-33=47=0x2F; 100-33=67=0x43 (Python). Below 0xfd is single BigSize byte (01-messaging.md:591), no expansion.

claims.evidence: Matches bolt04.md:100 and shift_size 1+l+32 (:801).

visual PeelPrimerDiagram.tsx.coverage:
Read the full PeelPrimerDiagram.tsx (1583 lines). This is a 10-beat stepped diagram: step 0 = INIT (Bob receives the onion), steps 1-3 = Bob verify/decrypt/read, steps 4-6 = Charlie verify/decrypt/read, steps 7-9 = Dave verify/decrypt/read (read = destination panel). I examined the caption generator captionForStep() for every substep type (INIT, verify, decrypt, forwarder-read, Dave/destination-read), the KeystreamXorRow (decrypt operand), the HmacChainIndicator pills, the PacketHeader (version/ephemeral), PacketOuterHmacCell, SlotCell sub-cells (LEN bigsize / hop-name / next-hop HMAC), PaddingRegion, DestinationPanel, and all literal constants (SLOT_BYTES 60/80/100, SLOT_BIGSIZE_HEX 0x1B/0x2F/0x43, TOTAL_BUFFER_BYTES 1300, blinding annotations). Cross-checked against BOLT 4 (key gen, packet structure, onion decryption, filler generation, blinding), BOLT 1 (bigsize length convention), and the course's own ch4/ch5/ch7/ch9 markdown for internal consistency. Verified arithmetic with python3 (bigsize hex, 1366 decomposition, 2600, padding sums). Could NOT exercise the live rendered widget (no browser run); all verdicts are from source + spec + arithmetic. The headline finding is the keystream-length divergence (C1/C2/C3): the primer teaches a 1,300-byte peel-time keystream, while BOLT 4 and the course's OWN ch7/ch9 (incl. a ch9 checkpoint) teach 2,600 bytes; because the primer sits in ch5 (before filler is introduced) and ch5:49 flags related details as deferred, I rated it MISLEADING rather than a hard INCORRECT, but it is the one substantive issue. Everything else (bigsize hex, decomposition, blinding formulas, all-zero destination HMAC, next-hmac promotion, privacy framing, character set) is correct or an accepted simplification.</parameter>

claims.quote: Now, {hopName} XORs the whole 1,300-byte `hop_payloads` buffer, byte-by-byte, against a 1,300-byte `chacha20` keystream from his shared secret with Alice.

It then generates the pseudo-random byte stream, of matching length'. Keystream is 2,600 bytes, not 1,300. Course ch7 (7.0-fixed-size-and-filler.md:58) 'one XOR pass over a 2,600-byte buffer ... XORs the whole thing against chacha20(rho_B, 2600)' and ch9 (9.0-peeling.md:60) '2,600-byte working buffer ... XORs it across the full 2,600 bytes'; ch9 checkpoint answer (onion-routing-tutorial.tsx:240) is the 2,600 figure.
claims.correctVersion: Present the keystream as 2,600 bytes (2x ROUTING_INFO_SIZE) to match BOLT 4 and ch7/ch9, OR explicitly flag the 1,300 figure as a simplified preview (as ch5:49 does for the wrap primer) so it does not read as a definitive length ch9 later contradicts.

claims.correctVersion: chacha20(rho_bob, 2600) to match BOLT 4 and ch7/ch9, or label as a deferred simplification. Key 'rho' is correct.

claims.quote: bob: "0x1B", // 60-byte hop payload → 27 ; charlie: "0x2F", // 80-byte hop payload → 47 ; dave: "0x43", // 100-byte hop payload → 67
claims.evidence: BOLT 1/BOLT 4 (bolt04.md:99-100) bigsize length 'excluding the prefix and the trailing HMAC'. 60-1-32=27=0x1B; 80-1-32=47=0x2F; 100-1-32=67=0x43, verified arithmetically. Comment lines 74-75 correctly state LEN = total minus 1-byte prefix minus 32-byte HMAC.

claims.quote: Alice hands Bob a 1,366-byte onion. To Bob, that whole 1,300-byte payload area is just encrypted bytes he can't read yet (the gray block).
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 packet structure (bolt04.md:139-146,782): version(1)+pubkey(33)+hop_payloads(1300)+hmac(32)=1366. 1+33+1300+32=1366 verified. 'returns a single 1366-byte packet'.

claims.quote: PAYLOAD AREA · 1,300 BYTES
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 (bolt04.md:144,159) '1300-byte hop_payloads'.

claims.quote: First, {hopName} recomputes `HMAC-SHA256` over the incoming `hop_payloads` with a key derived from his shared secret with Alice, then checks it against the outer HMAC tag in the packet. No match? The packet gets dropped.
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 (bolt04.md:935-939): mu=HMAC256('mu',ss); HMAC=HMAC256(mu, hop_payloads || associated_data); if computed differs MUST abort. Caption omits the associated_data (payment_hash) concatenation, a reasonable ch5-primer simplification. HMAC is SHA256 (bolt04.md:77-80), key from ss — correct. Drop-on-mismatch correct.

claims.quote: The HMAC field at the end is `0x00…` (32 zero bytes), which is the universal "you're the destination" signal. So Dave checks the payment hash and claims the funds.
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 onion decryption (bolt04.md:955,964-965): 'If next_hmac is not all-zero (not the final node)' ... 'Otherwise (all-zero next_hmac): This is the final destination'. 32-byte hmac (bolt04.md:146). Final-node payment check (bolt04.md:702-707).

claims.quote: Its HMAC field is 0x00… (32 zero bytes), the universal "you're the destination" signal. So Dave checks that the payment hash is the one he was expecting and claims the funds. There's no next-hop HMAC to pull out and no packet to forward.
claims.evidence: Same as C8 (bolt04.md:955,964-965). Destination has no next_hmac to extract and nothing to forward.

claims.quote: Where does the new outer HMAC come from? It's `{nextHopLower}_hmac`, the value that was tucked inside {hopName}'s hop payload, now promoted to the outer tag
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 onion decryption (bolt04.md:949-961): processing removes payload then 'Remove 32 bytes as next_hmac from the front', and the forwarded packet has 'hmac set to next_hmac'. Next-hop HMAC inside the current hop payload becomes the forwarded outer hmac.

claims.quote: ephemeralAnnotation = nextHop === "charlie" ? "E_AC = bf_AB · E_AB" : "E_AD = bf_AC · E_AC"
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 blinding (bolt04.md:773-776,956-959): next ephemeral pubkey = current public_key × blinding_tweak, blinding_tweak=SHA256(public_key||ss). Matches ch4 (4.0-shared-secrets.md:100 'E_AC = bf_AB · E_AB'), ch9 (9.0-peeling.md:88), BlindingFactorDiagram. Internally consistent: bf_AB advances E_AB→E_AC; bf_AC advances E_AC→E_AD.

claims.quote: The new header carries `{outgoingE}`, which he derives from the incoming `{incomingE}` with his blinding factor.
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 (bolt04.md:956-959): forwarded public_key = incoming public_key × blinding_tweak. Bob: E_AB→E_AC; Charlie: E_AC→E_AD. Matches incomingE/outgoingE assignment (lines 374-375).

claims.quote: Ephemeral labels E_AB/E_AC/E_AD per hop (Bob/Charlie/Dave) and HMAC chain bob_hmac → charlie_hmac → dave_hmac → 0x00…
claims.evidence: Bob's incoming header carries E_AB (from Alice), Charlie's E_AC, Dave's E_AD — matches the ephemeral advance chain (bolt04.md:773-776). Outer HMAC order bob→charlie→dave→zero matches nesting (innermost dave all-zero per bolt04.md:964). Ch5 markdown (5.0-onion-routing-101.md:81-84) corroborates the same chain.

claims.quote: paddingBytes = 1300 - sum(slot bytes): incoming Bob 1060 / Charlie 1120 / Dave 1200; outgoing Bob 1120 / Charlie 1200
claims.evidence: Arithmetic verified: 1300-(60+80+100)=1060; 1300-(80+100)=1120; 1300-100=1200. Internally self-consistent within the simplified single-1300-byte-buffer model. Real protocol regenerates FILLER on the forwarded tail and the working buffer momentarily reaches 2600 (bolt04.md:984-987); primer defers filler to ch7 (5.0-onion-routing-101.md:49), so 'padding' label is an accepted pre-ch7 simplification. 60/80/100 are sanctioned example sizes.

claims.quote: {SLOT_BYTES[hop]} B (60 B / 80 B / 100 B hop-payload total sizes)
claims.evidence: 60/80/100 are the prompt-sanctioned fixed example hop-payload sizes for Alice→Bob→Charlie→Dave. Internally consistent with the LEN bigsize hex (C4): 60=1+27+32, 80=1+47+32, 100=1+67+32 (bolt04.md:801 shift_size=1+l+32).

claims.quote: only the hop payload they can read shows internal structure. The rest of the buffer ... is rendered as a single gray block ... that's what an honest forwarder actually sees: encrypted bytes they can't decode.
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 overview (bolt04.md:12-19,130): a forwarder recovers only 'the address and HMAC of the next hop' from its own decrypted payload; other hops' payloads remain ChaCha20 noise. Diagram does NOT claim a forwarder can read other hops or infer its position/upstream count, so consistent with the privacy model.

claims.quote: version 0x00 (header field)
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 (bolt04.md:162) 'version has a constant value of 0x00' for version 0.

claims.quote: Main path characters Alice / Bob / Charlie / Dave only; HMAC pills bob_hmac/charlie_hmac/dave_hmac/0x00…
claims.evidence: Grep confirms no stray Carol/Hazel/Erin/Frank in the file; only the four sanctioned main-path names appear. Hop order Alice(12%)→Bob(38%)→Charlie(62%)→Dave(88%) matches the canonical route.

The '·' denotes EC point mult whose serialized point is SHA256'd — standard pedagogical shorthand, consistent with ch6 prose 6.0-key-derivation.md:10.

claims.quote: const KEYSTREAM = [0x8c,...,0xc4]; const TOTAL_BYTES = KEYSTREAM.length
claims.evidence: KEYSTREAM has 23 bytes; TOTAL_BYTES=23. Matches the 23-byte plaintext (1 len + 2+5 amt + 2+3 cltv + 2+8 scid = 23). Verified in Python.

claims.quote: plaintext.map((b, i) => b ^ KEYSTREAM[i]) ... parsed.bytes.map((b, i) => b ^ KEYSTREAM[i])
claims.evidence: Encrypt = plaintext XOR keystream; decrypt = ciphertext XOR same keystream. Python confirms round-trip recovers plaintext exactly (XOR is its own inverse). Matches bolt04.md:806-807 'rho-key ... applied, with XOR, to the hop_payloads field'.

claims.quote: Hit Encrypt to XOR each byte with the rho keystream.
claims.evidence: rho is the per-hop obfuscation key (bolt04.md:108-109, 806-807). Caption terminology is accurate; ch5 prose (5.0-onion-routing-101.md:15) corroborates the shared-secret keystream framing.

claims.quote: same XOR, same keystream, run against the ciphertext this time. The keystream cancels itself ... Since XOR is its own inverse, the plaintext falls right back out.
claims.evidence: Core ch5 lesson (XOR symmetry). Mathematically exact; matches 5.0-onion-routing-101.md:17 'the keystream cancels itself out'.

TLV type bytes 02=amt_to_forward, 04=outgoing_cltv_value, 06=short_channel_id match bolt04.md:194,197,200. Encode/decode offsets are mutually consistent; round-trip decodes back to the same amt/cltv/scid (Python verified).

claims.quote: leading 0x16 length-prefix byte on the plaintext row
claims.evidence: 0x16 = 22. BOLT 1 bigsize length 'excludes the prefix and the trailing HMAC' (bolt04.md:99-100). 22 = the 22 payload bytes after the length byte (23 total - 1 prefix). Internally consistent. (Note: this demo has no trailing HMAC, which is fine for a single-record teaching view.)

claims.quote: const amtBytes = uintToBytes(amt, 5); const cltvBytes = uintToBytes(cltv, 3); ... 0x02, 0x05, ...amtBytes, 0x04, 0x03, ...cltvBytes
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 uses tu64/tu32 (truncated, leading zeros MUST be omitted — 01-messaging.md:237-242). Default amt=10001000 encodes as 00 00 98 9a 68 (length 0x05) — real tu64 minimal would be 98 9a 68 (length 3); cltv=220 -> 00 00 dc (length 0x03) vs real tu32 'dc' (length 1). Fixed-width is a deliberate ch5 simplification (prompt allows fixed example sizes; 5.0-onion-routing-101.md:9 defers byte-level TLV detail to ch7). Encode and decode use the same fixed offsets, so the round-trip is internally consistent.
claims.correctVersion: For full BOLT-4 fidelity, amt/cltv would use minimal-length tu64/tu32 with leading zeros omitted and the length byte would shrink accordingly; acceptable as-is for the ch5 XOR lesson.

claims.quote: paste 46 hex characters ... (46 hex chars total)
claims.evidence: 23 bytes * 2 hex chars/byte = 46. Consistent across all three references and with parseHex bounds (TOTAL_BYTES*2).

claims.quote: const SCID_BYTES = [0x00, 0x0a, 0xae, 0x60, 0x00, 0x00, 0x01, 0x00]; const DEFAULT_SCID = "700000:1:0"; lastGoodScidRef = useRef(SCID_BYTES)
claims.evidence: The SCID_BYTES constant decodes to 2734:6291456:256, NOT 700000:1:0. 700000:1:0 actually encodes to 0a ae 60 00 00 01 00 00. SCID_BYTES is only the initial ref seed and is immediately overwritten on first render because DEFAULT_SCID parses validly (line 239), so it is NEVER rendered — no student-visible error. But it is an internally inconsistent/dead constant that could mislead a maintainer.
claims.correctVersion: SCID_BYTES should be [0x0a,0xae,0x60,0x00,0x00,0x01,0x00,0x00] to match DEFAULT_SCID '700000:1:0' (or just initialize the ref from parseSCID(DEFAULT_SCID)).

claims.quote: BOLT 7 short_channel_id: "block:tx:output", encoded as 8 bytes 3 bytes block_height || 3 bytes tx_index || 2 bytes output_index
claims.evidence: Matches bolt07.md:41-43 (3 bytes block height, 3 bytes tx index, 2 bytes output index = 8 bytes). parseSCID bounds (block/tx <=0xffffff, out<=0xffff) are correct.

claims.quote: DEFAULT_SCID = "700000:1:0" rendered with colon separators; format label "block:tx:out"
claims.evidence: BOLT 7's canonical human-readable separator is 'x' (e.g. 539268x845x1, bolt07.md:50), not ':'. The component consistently uses ':' and labels the format 'block:tx:out', and no other tutorial chapter contradicts it. Internally consistent; minor deviation from the spec's display convention only.
claims.correctVersion: Optionally use the BOLT-7 'x' separator (700000x1x0) to match the spec's standard human-readable format.

claims.quote: amt_to_forward ... `${decoded.amt} msat` (input unit also "msat")
claims.evidence: amt_to_forward is in millisatoshis per bolt04.md:223. Unit 'msat' is consistent between the editable input card and the decoded output panel. (Real tu64 max is 2^64-1; the demo caps at the 5-byte max, a consequence of the accepted fixed-width simplification.)

claims.quote: decoded panel labels the field outgoing_cltv while the input card labels it outgoing_cltv_value
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 field name is outgoing_cltv_value (bolt04.md:197). The input ProtocolMessageCard uses the correct full name 'outgoing_cltv_value' (line 1187) but the decoded-output DecodedRow truncates it to 'outgoing_cltv' (line 973). Same field shown two different ways within one component.
claims.correctVersion: Use 'outgoing_cltv_value' in the decoded panel to match BOLT 4 and the input card.

claims.quote: const MAX_AMT = 1099511627775; const MAX_CLTV = 16777215
claims.evidence: 1099511627775 = 256^5-1 (max value for the 5-byte amt field); 16777215 = 256^3-1 (max for the 3-byte cltv field). Caps exactly match the fixed byte widths used in computePlaintextBytes, so clamping is internally consistent. (Narrower than real tu64/tu32 maxima, but that follows from the accepted fixed-width simplification.)

claims.quote: Those bytes look like noise, and without the keystream you'd never get them back.
claims.evidence: For a one-time keystream (stream cipher with non-reused key), ciphertext = plaintext XOR keystream is indistinguishable from random without the key. Conceptually accurate for the ch5 teaching scope.

claims.quote: the source bytes just weren't a real Sphinx hop payload / a real onion hop payload
claims.evidence: BOLT 4 routing is 'based on the Sphinx construction' (bolt04.md:9); calling a hop payload a 'Sphinx hop payload' is accurate. Terminology uses approved 'hop payload' (not slot/slice).

claims.quote: placeholder="e.g. 8a 5d 01 7a... (46 hex chars total)"
claims.evidence: Placeholder hex is illustrative only (Alice's real default ciphertext starts 9a 5d a6 19). As a textarea placeholder it only needs to model the hex format and the 46-char count, both correct.

claims.quote: Same keystream both ways. Same XOR. The copy/paste literally simulates the wire transmission. / values look like fields in a wire protocol message (think update_add_htlc or similar)
claims.evidence: Internal comments. The hop payload (TLV with amt_to_forward/outgoing_cltv_value/short_channel_id) rides inside the onion, conceptually adjacent to update_add_htlc fields (BOLT 2). The 'think ... or similar' hedge keeps it accurate as an analogy. No student-visible claim.

secondaryWording.note: The invalid-TLV message is stated twice in near-identical wording: the StepCaption (line 912 'The XOR did its job, these source bytes just weren't a real onion hop payload') and the decoded panel (line 996 'The XOR ran fine, the source bytes just weren't a real Sphinx hop payload'). Both render simultaneously in the invalid-decrypt state — redundant.

The connector chip '↓ encoded as TLV bytes ↓' plus the PLAINTEXT row's region tinting plus the comment-described field-to-byte color mapping all restate the same 'these bytes encode those fields' idea already carried by the color coding; the chip label is somewhat redundant with the visible tinting.
Decoded value reads '220 block' (singular). Minor: 'blocks' or 'block height' would read better; redundant with the field name already implying a block-based CLTV.

claims.quote: One key per hop
claims.evidence: Accurate framing of the ch3 strawman: each hop's slice is locked to that hop's own key. Consistent with chapter prose 3.0-the-privacy-problem.md:47 'encrypt it with that hop's public key'.

claims.quote: next_hop Charlie / amt_to_forward 10,002 sat / outgoing_cltv block 220
claims.evidence: Matches sibling PlaintextMessageTear.tsx:45-48 exactly. Sats not msat is a stated intended simplification. Bob fee = 10002-10000 = 2 sat; CLTV delta Bob->Charlie = 220-180 = 40 blocks. Internally consistent.

claims.quote: next_hop Dave / amt_to_forward 10,000 sat / outgoing_cltv block 180
claims.evidence: Matches PlaintextMessageTear.tsx:52-56. Charlie forwards 10000 to Dave; Dave final_amt=10000 (line 83) so Charlie takes 0 fee (last forwarder), consistent. outgoing_cltv 180 matches Dave final_cltv block 180.

claims.quote: final_amt 10,000 sat / final_cltv block 180 / payment_hash 0xa3f1...e9c4
claims.evidence: final_amt 10000 == Charlie amt_to_forward 10000 (line 71); final_cltv 180 == Charlie outgoing_cltv 180 (line 72); payment_hash 0xa3f1...e9c4 matches envelope payment_hash line 346 and sibling PlaintextMessageTear.tsx:64.

claims.quote: cipher arrays of hex bytes (9 lines, 10 bytes each)
claims.evidence: Decorative illustrative ciphertext for a strawman; no BOLT 4 test vector is claimed. Verified all 9 lines are valid 2-char hex, 10 tokens each (python check). No BigSize/length-prefix conventions apply because the TLV hop_payload layout is not introduced until later chapters.

claims.quote: So, Alice locks each per-hop slice with that hop's own public key ... only the matching private key can open one. Without it, a slice is just noise.
claims.evidence: This is the deliberate ch3 per-public-key strawman (allowed by audit scope). Matches chapter prose 3.0:47. BOLT 4 uses ephemeral/blinded keys not raw node-identity keys (bolt04.md:32-33), but that is exactly the limitation ch4+ introduces; the visual correctly presents the naive version first.

claims.quote: He tries his private key against the whole stack, and only his slice opens. Charlie's and Dave's are sitting right there, but Bob can't read them.
claims.evidence: Adversarial check: Bob holds Charlie's+Dave's ciphertext on the wire but lacks their private keys, so he cannot decrypt them. Contents stay hidden - correct. Trial-decryption framing is a fair teaching simplification.

claims.quote: Did Bob learn anything about Dave or the final amount? Nope. Those slices stayed shut while they rode through him.
claims.evidence: Adversarial: claim is scoped to CONTENTS ('Dave or the final amount'), which Bob genuinely cannot read without the key. It does NOT claim Bob learns nothing overall - the chapter cp-still-vulnerable-draft (onion-routing-tutorial.tsx:407-416) separately admits Bob still infers position via packet shrink and key-correlation. Properly scoped, no overclaim.

claims.quote: Charlie tries his private key. His slice opens, Dave's stays sealed. So Charlie still has no idea who the destination is or what they'll get.
claims.evidence: Adversarial: Charlie holds Dave's ciphertext but not Dave's private key, so Dave's slice contents stay hidden. 'no idea who the destination is' = correct for slice CONTENTS. Charlie's own slice reveals only neighbor next_hop=Dave, which is legitimate, not 'the destination' as a final-recipient fact.

claims.quote: Then Charlie peels off his slice and sends Dave's sealed slice on by itself. Each forwarder only ever opened what its own private key could.
claims.evidence: Consistent with isRemoved/isDecrypted helpers (lines 120-130): after step 4 only Dave's slice remains; each forwarder decrypts only its own slice. Matches BOLT 4 peel-and-forward intuition (one payload per hop).

claims.quote: Bob never saw past himself, Charlie never saw past himself, and only Dave learned the final details.
claims.evidence: Adversarial: 'saw past himself' is about slice CONTENTS (downstream amounts/destination), which Bob/Charlie cannot decrypt - true. 'final' is italicized to scope to final details, not all info. Does not contradict the residual leaks (shrink/key-correlation) the next prose section raises. No overclaim.

claims.quote: This is the node-identity public key Alice used to lock this hop's slice. {label} opens it with the matching private key.
claims.evidence: Deliberate strawman: real Sphinx never encrypts to raw node-identity keys (it derives ECDH shared secrets from an ephemeral key, bolt04.md:32-33,142). ch3 explicitly teaches the node-identity-key version first (3.0:47) and the cp-still-vulnerable checkpoint (onion-routing-tutorial.tsx:412) names exactly this leak. Internally consistent; not an error per audit scope.

claims.quote: Alice locked this slice to {forHop}'s node-identity public key. Only {forHop} can open it, using the matching private key.
claims.evidence: Same strawman framing as C12, and consistent with it. Wording near-duplicates the node-badge tooltip (C12) - see secondaryWording note.

claims.quote: this intro visual already trains 'vertical = Bob's encryption', 'diagonal = Charlie', 'horizontal = Dave'
claims.evidence: Matches encryptionHatch.tsx LAYER_ANGLES: bob 90deg vertical (lines 5,26), charlie 45deg diagonal (lines 6,27), dave 0deg horizontal (line 7). Internal comment only.

claims.quote: HOP_KEY_COLORS bob #3b6aa0 / charlie #2d7a7a / dave #7b4b8a; HOP_COLORS alice #b8860b ...
claims.evidence: HOP_COLORS identical to PlaintextMessageTear.tsx:30-35; HOP_KEY_COLORS match each hop's stroke color and encryptionHatch.tsx hop colors (bob #3b6aa0, charlie #2d7a7a, dave #7b4b8a). Canonical palette aligned per comment lines 44-45.

claims.quote: field key 'outgoing_cltv'

claims.quote: Bob first, then Charlie. Dave's the final hop, so he doesn't shift anything and adds no filler.
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:1011 filler loop runs `for i := 0; i < numHops-1`, and :1035 'The last hop need not obfuscate the filler.' On Alice->Bob->Charlie->Dave the filler iterates over Bob+Charlie only; Dave (last hop) contributes nothing.

claims.quote: Bob's hop payload is 60 bytes wide (a small intermediate-hop TLV plus the 32-byte HMAC).
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:801 shift_size = 1 + l + 32 for l<253. 60 = 1(bigsize len) + 27(payload) + 32(hmac). The 60/80/100 example sizes are the sanctioned simplification; 'TLV + 32-byte HMAC' decomposition is consistent with the spec hop_payload structure (length prefix + payload + hmac).

claims.quote: Bob's keystream (1,360 bytes) ... ROUTING_INFO_SIZE + 60 = 1,360 bytes
claims.evidence: 1300 + 60 = 1360 = BOB_KEYSTREAM_LEN (line 69). Alternative filler model: sender needs Bob's rho keystream bytes at offsets 1300..1360 (the trailing slice). Equivalent to BOLT 2*1300 deobfuscation (bolt04.md:941 'twice the length').

claims.quote: keep just the last len(filler) = 60 bytes of Bob's keystream, the part that spills past the 1,300 boundary.
claims.evidence: At Bob's iteration filler length = 60. Last 60 of a 1360 stream = offsets 1300..1360, exactly past the 1300 boundary.

claims.quote: Since zeros XOR keystream is just the keystream itself, the result is 60 bytes carrying Bob's encryption layer.
claims.evidence: 0 XOR k = k. First filler iteration: zero-fill then xor with rho slice yields rho bytes (one layer). Matches BOLT filler Go (bolt04.md:1017,1024).

claims.quote: When Charlie peels downstream, this region lands at the byte offsets where Charlie's HMAC was computed.
claims.evidence: Filler bytes occupy the trailing region of the 1300-byte hop_payloads Charlie receives and HMACs over (bolt04.md:936). Slightly loose phrasing but not factually wrong.

claims.quote: It's now 140 bytes: 60 in Bob's encryption layer, plus 80 fresh zeros for Charlie's iteration.
claims.evidence: 60 + 80 = 140 = FINAL_FILLER_LEN (line 71). After Bob's iteration the 60-byte region carries Bob's rho; Charlie appends 80 zeros.

claims.quote: Charlie's keystream (1,380 bytes) ... ROUTING_INFO_SIZE + 80 = 1,380 bytes
claims.evidence: 1300 + 80 = 1380 = CHARLIE_KEYSTREAM_LEN (line 70). Same model as Bob.

claims.quote: keep the last len(filler) = 140 bytes of Charlie's keystream. Notice it overlaps Charlie's regular 1,300 region by 60 bytes.
claims.evidence: Trailing 140 of a 1380 stream = offsets 1240..1380. Inside 1300 region = 1240..1300 = 60 bytes; past 1300 = 80 bytes. 1380-140=1240, 1300-1240=60. Exact.

claims.quote: The leading 60 bytes pick up Charlie's layer on top of Bob's (two stacked layers now). The trailing 80 go from zero to Charlie-encrypted.
claims.evidence: Bob-rho XOR Charlie-rho = 2 layers on leading 60; 0 XOR Charlie-rho on trailing 80. Matches XorEquation final-filler rows (lines 862-883): leading 60 hops=[bob,charlie], trailing 80 hops=[charlie].

claims.quote: Alice fills the 1,300-byte hop_payloads buffer with pseudorandom bytes from a special 'pad-key' chacha20 keystream.
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:787-792: 1300 random bytes from pad key via ChaCha20 all-zero nonce. Key 'pad' = 0x706164 (bolt04.md:116).

claims.quote: XORs the whole buffer with Dave's rho keystream, then OVERWRITES the trailing 140 bytes with the filler ... written in exactly once, on this innermost iteration only.
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:808-809: filler overwrites the tail only on the last/first iteration. 140 = final filler length.

claims.quote: Alice shifts the buffer right by Charlie's hop-payload size (80 bytes), writes Charlie's hop payload at the front, then XORs the whole buffer with Charlie's rho keystream.
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:801-807: right-shift by shift_size (full hop_payload incl length+hmac, here 80), write payload at front, XOR with 1300-byte rho. Consistent with the diagram's defined shift size.

claims.quote: Alice shifts right by Bob's hop-payload size (60 bytes) ... Bob's hop payload at 1 layer, Charlie's at 2, Dave's at 3.
claims.evidence: Reverse-order wrap: Dave 3 layers, Charlie 2, Bob 1. Matches ShiftedRegionRow layer sets (lines 1329-1334).

claims.quote: Alice attaches the 66-byte envelope: a 1-byte version (0x00), her 33-byte ephemeral pubkey for Bob (E_AB), and the outer 32-byte HMAC. The 1,366-byte Sphinx packet is ready for Bob.
ROUTING_INFO_SIZE = 1300 ... FULL_PACKET_BYTES = 1 + 33 + 1300 + 32 // 1,366
ROUTING_INFO_SIZE: the fixed size of every onion's hop_payloads field. Real wraps XOR over exactly this many bytes; for filler we extend past it.
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:806-807 real wrap XORs a 1300-byte rho stream; filler model extends past 1300 (bolt04.md:941 deobfuscation 2*1300).

claims.quote: ROUTING_INFO_SIZE + payload_sizes[i] = 1,300 + {sLabel} = {totalLen}. The extra {sLabel} bytes simulate {hop}'s shift and produce the bytes downstream HMACs expect at the trailing positions.
claims.evidence: Bob 1300+60=1360; Charlie 1300+80=1380. Extra bytes model the shift-induced trailing region that pre-generated filler must match (bolt04.md:978-980).

claims.quote: Running length of the filler buffer. Grows by payload_sizes[i] on each iteration.
claims.evidence: Buffer 0 -> 60 -> 140 across iterations (fillerRegionsAt). Matches per-hop payload-size growth.

claims.quote: Dave's packet was only his own hop payload (166 bytes on the wire). He decrypts it, reads the payment_data and final amount, and claims the HTLC.

claims.quote: the byte count *shrank at every hop*, so anyone watching the wire could tell exactly where each forwarder sits in the route.
claims.evidence: This is the inverse of BOLT4 bolt04.md:16: fixed packet size ensures nodes 'cannot learn the length of the route or their position within it.' Without fixed size, size N=66+sum(remaining payloads) directly reveals downstream-hops-remaining, i.e. distance-to-destination = the forwarder's position. A global wire observer sees the monotone shrink and reconstructs ordering. The strawman framing ('without a fixed size') is explicit (caption 0, header banner). Adversarial note: a single-link passive observer learns downstream depth, not upstream count, but 'where each forwarder sits' (distance to destination) IS exactly leaked; informal 'exactly/anyone' is pedagogically fair and spec-aligned.

claims.quote: The length encoded is the TLV-payload size (payload total minus the 33-byte HMAC), so the canonical 60/80/100-byte hop payloads encode size-33 = 27/47/67 = 0x1B/0x2F/0x43.
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:99-100 'bigsize encoding the length in bytes, excluding the prefix and the trailing HMAC'; the trailing per-hop hmac is 32 bytes (bolt04.md:146,160,171 `32*byte`:`hmac`; :801 shift_size = 1 + l + 32). The subtracted overhead is 1 (bigsize prefix) + 32 (HMAC) = 33, NOT a '33-byte HMAC'. This file's own line 64-65 correctly says '32-byte HMAC', so the file is self-contradictory. The arithmetic RESULT (27/47/67) and the rendered 0x1B/0x2F/0x43 are correct because 60-1-32=27, matching sibling visuals (WrapTraceDiagram.tsx:80 '60-byte slot → 60 - 1 - 32 = 27', PayloadShrinkDiagram.tsx:63, PeelTraceDiagram.tsx:164).
claims.correctVersion: The bigsize LEN excludes the 1-byte prefix AND the 32-byte HMAC: 60-1-32=27 (0x1B), 80-1-32=47 (0x2F), 100-1-32=67 (0x43). The HMAC is 32 bytes, not 33; the '33' is the combined 1-byte prefix + 32-byte HMAC overhead.

claims.quote: len sub-cell: BigSize length prefix (size-33)
claims.evidence: Same as C1: '(size-33)' conflates the 1-byte bigsize prefix with the 32-byte HMAC. BOLT4 bolt04.md:801 shift_size = 1 + l + 32. Internal comment only (not student-visible); rendered value 0x1B is correct.
claims.correctVersion: BigSize length prefix encoding (size minus 1-byte prefix minus 32-byte HMAC) = size-33 total overhead, e.g. (60-1-32).

claims.quote: badgeLabel: "Encrypt the return error" (step 4, ammag) ... badgeLabel: "Authenticate the return error" (step 5, um)
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:1056-1072: the erring node FIRST builds the return packet with the `um`-key hmac authenticating the body, THEN generates the `ammag` key and XOR-encrypts the whole packet (HMAC included). Order is authenticate-then-encrypt (MAC-then-encrypt). The diagram presents encrypt(ammag)-then-authenticate(um), which is reversed. The course's own ch11 (11.0-error-onion.md:9 'authenticates the failure message with its `um` key, encrypts it with its `ammag` keystream'; :44-53 Step3 Authenticate with um → Step5 Wrap with ammag) teaches the correct MAC-then-encrypt order, so the diagram also contradicts the course. NOTE: the FORWARD path (steps 2-3, rho then mu) IS correctly encrypt-then-MAC per bolt04.md:806-811; the error path uses the opposite order.
claims.correctVersion: For the return/error path: authenticate first with `um` (HMAC over failure msg+pad), then encrypt the whole packet (HMAC included) with the `ammag` keystream. Reverse steps 4 and 5, or relabel so 'authenticate with um' precedes 'encrypt with ammag'.

claims.quote: Bob computes a 32-byte authentication tag (`HMAC-SHA256`) over the error packet and tacks it on the back.
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:1059-1067 lists `hmac` as the FIRST field of the return packet ('[`32*byte`:`hmac`]' before failure_len/failuremsg/pad), and ch11 (11.0-error-onion.md:48 'he prepends the HMAC to the payload: `error_hmac (32 bytes) || payload (260 bytes)`') confirms the error HMAC is PREPENDED (front), not appended. The forward-packet HMAC IS on the back (bolt04.md:160), but the error HMAC is at the front.
claims.correctVersion: ...and PREPENDS it to the front of the error packet (error_hmac || payload). The error HMAC goes at the front, unlike the forward packet's trailing HMAC.

claims.quote: padding ... ~192 bytes
claims.evidence: Diagram's own comment (line 1000-1002) says the padding 'fills the rest of the 256-byte buffer'. The error-onion test vector (onion-error-test.json) and ch11 (11.0-error-onion.md:45,48) put failuremsg+pad at 256 bytes (payload 260B, packet 292B). With a tiny failure record (the diagram shows just the 2-byte code), padding should be ~252 bytes, not ~192. The '~192' does not reconcile with the stated 256-byte buffer minus the small FAIL record. Internally inconsistent student-visible number.
claims.correctVersion: If the buffer is 256 bytes (failuremsg+pad) and the FAIL record is a few bytes, padding is ~252 bytes; or label it relative to whatever fixed total is intended. As drawn (FAIL ~40% + padding) the number should be in the ~150-252 range, not 192.

claims.quote: FAIL record LEN '0x02' ... 'temporary_channel_failure' / '0x1007'
claims.evidence: The error packet's length field is a u16 `failure_len` (bolt04.md:1061 '[`u16`:`failure_len`]'), not a BigSize, and it counts the whole failuremsg. For temporary_channel_failure, failuremsg = 2-byte code (0x1007) + u16 len + channel_update (bolt04.md:1303-1306), so failure_len would be far more than 2. Rendering a BigSize-style 'LEN 0x02' sub-cell mimicking the forward hop-payload grammar mislabels the field type and size. Acceptable as a simplified visual but the 0x02/grammar reuse is a minor inaccuracy.
claims.correctVersion: The error length is a u16 `failure_len`, not a BigSize; and temporary_channel_failure carries a channel_update so it is longer than 2 bytes. Either drop the BigSize-style LEN cell or note it is illustrative.

claims.quote: BOLT 4 failure code `temporary_channel_failure` (`0x1007`)
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:1303 'type: UPDATE|7 (`temporary_channel_failure`)'; flag bolt04.md:1269 '0x1000 (UPDATE)'. 0x1000 | 0x0007 = 0x1007. Exact match.

claims.quote: Bob can't forward the payment (say his channel to Charlie is out of liquidity for the moment). So he writes a short error with the BOLT 4 failure code `temporary_channel_failure`
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:1308-1309 temporary_channel_failure = 'The channel from the processing node was unable to handle this HTLC, but may be able to handle it, or others, later.' Out-of-liquidity (transient) correctly maps to temporary_channel_failure.

claims.quote: Alice fills the empty payload area with random-looking bytes from `pad`. ... empty space at the back of the packet would quietly tell observers how short the route really is.
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:112-113 pad 'use to generate random filler bytes for the starting mix-header packet'; :787-792 packet initialized with 1300 random bytes from ChaCha20 using the pad key; rationale :976-982 the field would otherwise shorten/reveal route length, so it is pre-padded. Plain-English statement is accurate.

claims.quote: Alice scrambles the whole payload with a stream cipher ... with `rho`, a `ChaCha20` keystream, adding one round of encryption for each hop
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:108-109 rho generates the pseudo-random byte stream; :131-132 stream generated via ChaCha20; :806-807 rho-key generates a 1300-byte stream applied with XOR. One layer per hop is applied in reverse-order construction (:797-807).

claims.quote: Alice computes a 32-byte authentication tag (`HMAC-SHA256`) over the payload ... with `mu` ... Every forwarder recomputes and checks this tag the moment it arrives, before decrypting anything
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:110 mu used during HMAC generation; :119 keys via HMAC-SHA256; :810-811 next HMAC computed with mu over hop_payloads. Processing order :935-942 derive mu → compute HMAC → constant-time compare → abort if differ → THEN derive rho and XOR-decrypt. HMAC verification is indeed before decryption. Tag size 32 bytes (:160).

claims.quote: encrypts it with a different stream cipher ... with `ammag`
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:1070-1072 erring node generates `ammag` key, used to generate a pseudo-random stream applied via XOR. It is a distinct stream cipher from rho. (Operation identity correct; only the step ORDERING vs um is wrong — see C3.)

claims.quote: Bob computes a 32-byte authentication tag ... with `um`
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:1060,1066-1067 return packet has a `32*byte` hmac with key type `um`. Key/size correct. (Placement 'on the back' and order are wrong — see C3/C4.)

claims.quote: keyCardPropsFor: pad => HMAC("pad", session_key); others => HMAC("<key>", ss_AB) with ss_AB = SHA256(e_AB · B)
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:866 paddingKey = generateKey('pad', sessionKey); generateKey = HMAC(keytype, secret) (:119-121) → pad = HMAC('pad', session_key). For per-hop keys: ss = SHA256(ECDH) (:501,531,744) and rho/mu/um/ammag = HMAC256(name, ss) (:502,533,935,940,1067,1070). For the first hop the ephemeral key equals the session key, so ss_AB = SHA256(e_AB · B) is correct. Both card formulas match the spec.

claims.quote: keyRoleTitle: rho='Stream cipher key', mu='Integrity HMAC key', um='Error HMAC key', ammag='Error stream key'
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:108-111 rho=pseudo-random byte stream (stream cipher), mu=HMAC generation (integrity), um=error reporting (error HMAC); :1070-1072 ammag=error stream cipher. Role titles accurate.

claims.quote: HEADER: version 0x00 ... ephemeral pubkey E_AB
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:157-162 version byte = 0x00 (version 0); :142 33-byte compressed public_key; :764 'ephemeral public key'. E_AB (uppercase = public ephemeral key) is the correct label for the packet's public_key field.

claims.quote: HMAC ... '32-byte tag'
claims.evidence: BOLT4 bolt04.md:146,160 the packet hmac is `32*byte`. Both forward and error HMACs are 32 bytes (:1060).

claims.quote: NEXT_HOP_LABEL bob='for Charlie', charlie='for Dave', dave='none'; HOP_PAYLOAD_BYTES bob:60, charlie:80, dave:100
claims.evidence: Each hop payload's trailing HMAC points to the next hop's view (bolt04.md:171 per-hop `32*byte`:`hmac`); the final hop (Dave) has an all-zero next HMAC ('none'). Sizes 60/80/100 are the course's documented canonical example sizes (CLAUDE/MEMORY 'fixed example hop-payload sizes 60/80/100') and match sibling visuals. Internally consistent.

claims.quote: title 'onion_routing_packet (at Alice, preparing)' / '(Alice → Bob)'; 'error_packet (at Bob, preparing)' / '(Bob → Alice)'
claims.evidence: Forward packet name onion_routing_packet matches BOLT2 (02-peer-protocol.md update_add_htlc field onion_routing_packet) and BOLT4 onion_packet. error_packet is the BOLT4 return packet (bolt04.md:1056). Directions match the 5-step Alice→Bob→error→Alice narrative; characters restricted to Alice/Bob/Charlie/Dave.

claims.quote: hmacValue = isError ? '0x9b a3…4d' : '0x4f c2…7a'
claims.evidence: Illustrative/decorative truncated hex placeholders, not drawn from the BOLT4 test vectors (onion-test.json / onion-error-test.json). No spec value to compare; clearly cosmetic.

claims.quote: The 4-hop route Alice → Bob → Charlie → Dave is shown for context
claims.evidence: Internal code comment only (not student-visible). Alice (origin) + 3 forwarders (Bob, Charlie, Dave) = 4 nodes / 3 hops. The 3 forward encryption layers and 3 NEXT_HOP_LABEL entries are internally consistent with 3 forwarders. 'Four-hop' is loose (4 nodes) but not student-facing.

claims.quote: 'Five operations, five keys' / 'Five operations across a roundtrip'
claims.evidence: The five keys pad/rho/mu/ammag/um are all real BOLT4 keys (bolt04.md:108-116 rho/mu/um/pad; :1070 ammag). Each maps to one of the five operations (pad=fill, rho=encrypt fwd, mu=auth fwd, ammag=encrypt err, um=auth err). 'one per operation' is accurate.

visual WrapTraceDiagram.tsx.coverage: Read the entire file (2149 lines) and all 13 beats (BEATS array lines 125-235), every region definition in regionsForBeat (steps 1-13, lines 261-532), every special view (WrapMorphView steps 3-4, XorView steps 4/8/11, HmacView steps 6/9/12, EnvelopeView step 13), SlotCell subcell labels/hex, key-derivation cards (keysPropsForStep, hmacKeyProps), tooltips (filler "what's filler?", AD/associated_data, 1300-byte and 1366-byte hovers), and all literal numbers/hex. Cross-checked against BOLT 4 (/tmp/onion-audit/bolt04.md): packet structure lines 137-176, key generation 104-125, pseudo-random stream 127-135, onion construction pseudocode 788-904, filler generation 971-1036, onion decryption/processing order 906-970; BOLT 1 BigSize rule (uint8(x) if x<0xfd, length excludes prefix+HMAC). Independently simulated the filler layer structure symbolically to verify the Bob+Charlie / Charlie-only split, and cross-checked against the sibling FillerTraceDiagram (ch7) and ForwarderPeelDiagram for course-internal consistency. Did NOT render the component in a browser (static audit only); animation/morph timing and visual layout not exercised, only the literal text/data they emit. All hex/arithmetic verified with python3. No factual errors found; the only item is a Low-severity rendering simplification (step-5 hatch shows the whole 140B filler as Bob+Charlie, while the precise split is 60B Bob+Charlie / 80B Charlie-only — step-7 prose states the precise split correctly).

claims.quote: FULL_PACKET_BYTES = PACKET_VERSION_BYTES + PACKET_PUBKEY_BYTES + ROUTING_INFO_SIZE + PACKET_HMAC_BYTES; // 1,366
claims.evidence: BOLT4 lines 157-160: version byte + 33-byte public_key + 1300-byte hop_payloads + 32-byte hmac = 1+33+1300+32=1366. Canonical decomposition.

claims.quote: ROUTING_INFO_SIZE = 1300; BOB_SLOT=60; CHARLIE_SLOT=80; DAVE_SLOT=100; FILLER_LEN = BOB_SLOT + CHARLIE_SLOT; // 140
claims.evidence: hop_payloads is 1300B (BOLT4:144,165). Filler length = sum of shift_sizes of all hops except innermost (Dave); per generateFiller (BOLT4:1006-1029) returns (numHops-1) hops worth = Bob(60)+Charlie(80)=140.

claims.quote: SLOT_BIGSIZE_HEX: bob 0x1B (60-1-32=27), charlie 0x2F (47), dave 0x43 (67)
claims.evidence: BigSize length EXCLUDES the 1-byte length prefix and 32-byte trailing HMAC (BOLT1:100; BOLT4:801 shift_size=1+l+32). 27=0x1B, 47=0x2F, 67=0x43; all <0xfd so single-byte bigsize (BOLT1:591).

claims.quote: Fill the buffer with pad_key noise ... chacha20(pad_key, 1300), where pad_key = HMAC('pad', session_key)
claims.evidence: BOLT4:866-868 paddingKey=generateKey("pad", sessionKey); generateKey=HMAC256(keytype,secret) (BOLT4:119-121); paddingBytes=generateCipherStream(paddingKey,1300). pad keytype 0x706164 (BOLT4:115).

claims.quote: So the destination can't tell 'this packet was built for a short route' from what's left over.
claims.evidence: BOLT4:976-982: per-hop left-shift would shorten the field and let an attacker deduce route length; pad/filler pre-pad to fixed 1300 so a short route is indistinguishable. The final node sees deepest into the buffer, so naming 'destination' as the party that must not learn route length is a fair pedagogical framing.

claims.quote: Right-shift by 100 bytes ... Drop the last 100 bytes off the right and prepend 100 empty placeholder bytes ... exactly the size of Dave's hop payload (bigsize + TLV + HMAC), and the total stays at 1,300.
claims.evidence: BOLT4:801-803 shift_size=1+l+32 (Dave: 1+67+32=100); right-shift discarding last shift_size bytes, total stays 1300 (BOLT4:881 rightShift over fixed RoutingInfoSize).

claims.quote: Write Dave's bigsize-prefixed TLV records (amt_to_forward, outgoing_cltv_value, payment_data), then a 32-byte all-zero HMAC. Those zeros are how Dave knows he's the destination.
claims.evidence: Final node payload includes amt_to_forward(2), outgoing_cltv_value(4), payment_data(8) (BOLT4:280,284-289). All-zero next_hmac signals final node (BOLT4:962-963: 'Otherwise (all-zero next_hmac): This is the final destination').

claims.quote: XOR with rho_D over the whole 1,300 bytes ... Generate chacha20(rho_D, 1300) and XOR it onto the whole buffer.
claims.evidence: BOLT4:806-807 rho-key generates 1300-byte pseudo-random stream XORed onto hop_payloads; BOLT4:879,885 generateCipherStream(rhoKey,1300) then xor over RoutingInfoSize.

claims.quote: Overwrite the trailing 140 bytes with filler ... only do on the innermost iteration ... It already carries Bob's and Charlie's encryption layers ... the very bytes that, after each forwarder peels, land right where Charlie's and Dave's HMACs were computed over.
claims.evidence: BOLT4:808-809 'if this is the last hop, i.e. the first iteration, the tail is overwritten with filler' (BOLT4:888-889 i==numHops-1). Filler carries Bob+Charlie rho passes (BOLT4:1011 loop i<numHops-1). 'Charlie's and Dave's HMACs' = the two downstream HMAC inputs the regenerated filler must match for a 3-hop Bob/Charlie/Dave path. len(filler)=140.

claims.quote: dave_hmac = HMAC(mu_D, buffer || associated_data) ... associated_data (the 32-byte payment_hash) ... hold onto dave_hmac as next_hmac
claims.evidence: BOLT4:810-811,893 nextHmac=calcMac(muKey, mixHeader||assocData); mu keytype (BOLT4:115). associated_data=payment_hash for payments (32B). next_hmac carried to next iteration (BOLT4:862,876).

claims.quote: Right-shift by 80 and write Charlie's hop payload ... Drop the last 80 bytes off the right (the Charlie-only tail of the filler) ... write Charlie's TLV records plus dave_hmac ... The Bob+Charlie portion of the filler stays put in the trailing 60 bytes.
claims.evidence: Symbolic simulation of generateFiller (BOLT4:1006-1029) shows returned filler = [front Bob-region: {Bob,Charlie}] + [tail Charlie-region: {Charlie only}]. Charlie's shift_size=80 drops the trailing 80 (Charlie-only), leaving 60 (Bob+Charlie). Matches ch7 FillerTraceDiagram:168 verbatim ('leading 60 ... two stacked layers ... trailing 80 ... Charlie-encrypted'). Charlie's HMAC field holds dave_hmac (next_hmac).

claims.quote: After Charlie XOR: Charlie's hop payload gets its first encryption layer. Dave's ... at 2 layers now. The middle padding goes from 1 layer to 2.
claims.evidence: Rendered regions (lines 426-444): charlie ['charlie'], dave ['dave','charlie'], pad ['dave','charlie']. Each rho XOR adds one layer to the whole buffer (BOLT4:807). Consistent with ch7 FillerTraceDiagram:193.

claims.quote: Right-shift by 60 and write Bob's hop payload ... Drop the last 60 bytes off the right (the Bob+Charlie filler residue) ... write Bob's TLV records plus charlie_hmac
claims.evidence: shift_size(Bob)=1+27+32=60. After Charlie iteration the 60B residue (Bob+Charlie layers) is the trailing region; Bob's 60B shift drops it. Bob's HMAC field holds charlie_hmac (next_hmac from C tag). Matches BOLT4:801,876.

claims.quote: After this XOR, Bob's hop payload has 1 layer (Bob), Charlie's has 2 (Bob + Charlie), Dave's has 3 (Bob + Charlie + Dave), and the trailing padding has 3 too ... every region looks equally encrypted.
claims.evidence: Rendered regions (lines 498-525): bob ['bob'], charlie ['charlie','bob'], dave ['dave','charlie','bob'], pad ['dave','charlie','bob']. After final XOR all 1300 bytes are pseudo-random on the wire regardless of layer count (XOR with keystream). Matches ch7 FillerTraceDiagram:201.

claims.quote: bob_hmac = HMAC(mu_B, buffer || associated_data) ... goes in the packet's outer hmac field. When the packet lands, Bob checks it first thing, before he decrypts anything.
claims.evidence: Final HMAC is the first-hop packet hmac (BOLT4:813-818, 900). Processing order (BOLT4:939-948): derive mu, compute HMAC over hop_payloads||associated_data, constant-time compare and abort on mismatch, THEN derive rho and XOR-decrypt. So HMAC verify precedes decryption.

claims.quote: Attach the envelope ... Prepend the 1-byte version (0x00) and the 33-byte ephemeral pubkey E_AB, then append the 32-byte bob_hmac. That's 1 + 33 + 1,300 + 32 = 1,366 bytes
claims.evidence: BOLT4:157-162 version=0x00 (v0), 33-byte point public_key, 1300 hop_payloads, 32 hmac. Packet returns version, ephemeral pubkey for first hop, first-hop HMAC, obfuscated hop_payloads (BOLT4:816-818,896-901).

claims.quote: HMAC('rho', ss_AD) → rho_D; HMAC('mu', ss_AD) → mu_D; ss from inputs e_AD, dave_pubkey; formulaOverride SHA256(e_AD · dave_pubkey)
claims.evidence: rho/mu derived as HMAC256(keytype, ss) (BOLT4:119-121,873-874). ss = SHA256(ECDH point) (BOLT4:836-838 sha256.Sum256(ecdhResult.SerializeCompressed())); 'SHA256(e_Ai · hop_pubkey)' is a faithful notation for SHA256 of the ECDH point.

claims.quote: NEXT_LABEL: bob 'for Charlie', charlie 'for Dave', dave 'none' (HMAC subcell of each hop's slot)
claims.evidence: Each hop's payload HMAC field holds the next_hmac the NEXT hop verifies: Bob holds charlie_hmac (for Charlie), Charlie holds dave_hmac (for Dave), Dave holds all-zero (none, final). Consistent with wrap placement in C11/C13 and BOLT4:876,962.

claims.quote: hop_payloads buffer ... 1,300 bytes; envelope rightLabel '1,366 bytes total'; byte 0 / byte 1,299 (buffer) and byte 1,365 (envelope)
claims.evidence: hop_payloads fixed 1300B → indices 0..1299 (BOLT4:165). Full packet 1366B → indices 0..1365 (FULL_PACKET_BYTES-1=1365). Tooltip 'same fixed size at every hop' matches BOLT4:148-151.

claims.quote: After each forwarder XORs away its layer and shifts the buffer, these trailing bytes land exactly where every downstream HMAC was computed over, so each forwarder's integrity check still passes.
claims.evidence: BOLT4:978-980,991-993: origin pre-generates identical filler so HMACs computed over the obfuscated padding match what each hop regenerates on peel. 'every downstream HMAC' is the correct generalization of C9's 'Charlie's and Dave's HMACs'.

claims.quote: AD is the associated_data, the 32-byte payment_hash. Folding it into the HMAC ties this onion to one specific HTLC, so nobody can replay the packet against a different payment.
claims.evidence: HMAC is over hop_payloads||associated_data (BOLT4:810-811,941). For payments associated_data binds the onion; payment_hash is 32B. Replay-binding is the standard rationale.

claims.quote: Step 5 renders the whole 140-byte filler region with layers ['bob','charlie']; step 7 filler-residue (60B) with layers ['bob','charlie'].
claims.evidence: Ground truth (symbolic sim + ch7 FillerTraceDiagram:168): the 140B filler splits into 60B {Bob,Charlie} + 80B {Charlie only}. Step-5 hatch shows the aggregate Bob+Charlie over all 140 (cannot split a single HatchOverlay region), mildly understating that the trailing 80 is Charlie-only — which step-7 prose (C11) states precisely. Step-7's 60B residue layers ['bob','charlie'] is exactly correct. Prose is correct throughout; only the step-5 hatch is a schematic aggregate.
claims.correctVersion: Optional: split the step-5 filler into two sub-regions (60B Bob+Charlie, 80B Charlie-only) to match ch7, or leave as an accepted schematic aggregate.

claims.quote: KeystreamBar: chacha20(rho_D, 1300) ... 'rho_D · 1,300 bytes' (uniform keystream bar on XOR beats)
claims.evidence: Wrap-side rho stream is 1300 bytes (BOLT4:806,879). (Contrast: peel-side uses 2600 = 2×1300, BOLT4:984-986; correctly NOT used here.)

claims.quote: fillerResiduePct = DISPLAY_FILLER_PCT * (60 / 140)  // after dropping 80 from the 140B filler, 60 remain
claims.evidence: 140-80=60; residue fraction 60/140. Arithmetic and the drop-80-keep-60 logic match C11 and BOLT4 shift semantics.

claims.quote: HmacOutputChip: outer step '32 bytes · becomes the packet's outer HMAC tag'; inner '32 bytes · feeds next iteration's next_hmac'
claims.evidence: All HMACs are 32B (BOLT4:146,171). bob_hmac (step 12, outer) becomes packet hmac field; dave_hmac/charlie_hmac feed next_hmac of the following iteration (BOLT4:862,876,893).

claims.quote: Iteration 1 of 3 (Dave, innermost) ... Iteration 2 of 3 (Charlie) ... Iteration 3 of 3 (Bob, outermost) — reverse route order
claims.evidence: BOLT4:797 'For each hop in the route, in reverse order'; BOLT4:872 loop i=numHops-1..0 → Dave(innermost) first, Bob(outermost) last. Characters Alice/Bob/Charlie/Dave only.

visual NodeKeyAttemptDiagram.tsx.coverage: Full file 599 lines; all 6 steps, packet and ECDH panels, gossip callout, badge; checked BOLT4 BOLT7 BOLT2 onion-test.json; deliberate ch3 ch4 naive strawman.

claims.quote: forwarder priv with Alice pub gives ss via SHA256, pubkey matches node_announcement so sender is Alice
claims.evidence: BOLT4 L725-728 ECDH, L717-719 ss; BOLT7 L293-299 node_id leaks sender; strawman not Sphinx L32-34.

claims.quote: payment_hash shown as a field inside ONION_PACKET
claims.evidence: BOLT4 L756-759 payment_hash is HMAC associated data not in the packet, L155-160.
claims.correctVersion: Frame as HTLC associated data, not an onion field.

claims.quote: slices labeled slice for bob charlie dave
claims.evidence: Locked term hop payload not slice, L101 L499; maybe intended ch3 metaphor L11.
claims.correctVersion: Use hop payload unless ch3 metaphor.

claims.quote: Alice sends the 1,366-byte onion to Bob.
claims.evidence: BOLT4 L782 returns a single 1366-byte packet; decomposition 1+33+1300+32=1366.

claims.quote: Bob's hop payload has 1 encryption layer, Charlie's has 2, Dave's has 3, and the padding has 3.
claims.evidence: BOLT4 L797-811 reverse-order XOR: region i is wrapped by hops 1..i. On A->B->C->D path Bob=1, Charlie=2, Dave=3; filler tail obfuscated by all 3=3. packetStateFor step<=3 (L429-434) encodes exactly bob:[bob], charlie:[bob,charlie], dave:[bob,charlie,dave], padding:[bob,charlie,dave]. Spec-correct and internally consistent.

claims.quote: Bob computes ss_AB = ECDH(node_priv_B, E_AB) ... rho_B = HMAC(rho, ss_AB) and mu_B = HMAC(mu, ss_AB).
claims.evidence: BOLT4 L934 ECDH(public_key,node_privkey); L935 mu=HMAC256(mu,ss); L940 rho=HMAC256(rho,ss). Key-type strings match L115.

claims.quote: Bob recomputes HMAC-SHA256(mu_B, hop_payloads concat associated_data) and checks it against the HMAC field.
claims.evidence: BOLT4 L936 HMAC256(mu, hop_payloads concat associated_data); L937-939 constant-time compare and abort on mismatch. Verify-with-mu before decrypt-with-rho matches spec order L935-942.

claims.quote: Bob tacks 1,300 zero bytes onto hop_payloads, growing it from 1,300 to 2 x ROUTING_INFO_SIZE = 2,600 bytes. He generates a matching 2,600-byte rho_B keystream from chacha20(rho_B, 2600).
claims.evidence: BOLT4 L984-986 pads with 1300 0x00 so total length is 2*1300; stream of matching length; L941 bytestream twice length of hop_payloads. 2*1300=2600. ChaCha20 null nonce L131-133.

claims.quote: single XOR pass: (a) decrypts Bob's hop payload at front (60 bytes to plaintext), (b) peels one encryption layer off downstream payloads and padding, (c) generates 60 new padding bytes at back from zeros XOR rho_B[1300..1359].
claims.evidence: BOLT4 L986-989: XOR deobfuscates own info while obfuscating the appended 0x00 tail. 60 = Bob shift_size (1+27+32). Offsets 1300..1359 are the tail of Charlie's 1300-byte view after the 60-byte front slice. zeros XOR keystream = keystream. Internally consistent.

claims.quote: What's left is exactly 1,300 bytes ... the last 60 are the new ones the XOR made. Those 60 are exactly what Alice's filler precomputed.
claims.evidence: BOLT4 L960 hop_payloads truncated to incoming size (1300); L991-994 origin pre-generates an identical filler so Bob's tail matches Alice's precomputed filler exactly. 60 = Bob shift_size.

claims.quote: Bob swaps the ephemeral pubkey (E_AB to E_AC, derived by blinding) and the outer HMAC (bob_hmac to charlie_hmac, pulled from his decrypted TLV).
claims.evidence: BOLT4 L955-961: next_hmac nonzero -> public_key = incoming public_key times blinding_tweak (blinding_tweak = SHA256(public_key concat ss), L956); hmac = next_hmac extracted from decrypted payload L952. Blinded next ephemeral labeled E_AC; new outer HMAC = charlie_hmac. Correct.

claims.quote: LEN value 0x1B, subValue 27 B TLV; HMAC charlie_hmac 32 bytes; Bob hop payload total 60 bytes (BOB_PAYLOAD=60 L63).
